Because there is no explicit synchronization, threads never block on each other; under heavy contention, however, the retry loop can spin, which is a trade-off worth mentioning.

SECTION 7 - CAS (COMPARE AND SWAP)

Q19. What Is CAS?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Concept

Compare-and-swap is an atomic CPU operation that takes three inputs: a memory location, an expected old value, and a new value. It updates the location to the new value only if the location currently holds the expected value; otherwise it does nothing and reports failure. The entire compare-then-set happens as one indivisible hardware instruction.

This single primitive is the foundation of lock-free programming. Algorithms use it in a retry loop: read the current value, compute the desired new value, then CAS; if it fails because another thread intervened, simply re-read and try again. This is how Atomic classes and many concurrent collections achieve thread safety without locks.

Example


Expected = 5

Current = 5

New = 6

Update Success
Q20. Advantages Of CAS

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

The core advantage of CAS is that it achieves thread safety without blocking. Because a thread never waits to acquire a lock, it cannot be suspended by the scheduler mid-operation, which avoids the expensive context switches and the risk of deadlock that come with lock-based approaches.

  • No Blocking
  • Better Performance
  • Reduced Context Switching
  • Lock-Free Operations

The trade-off, worth raising in a senior interview, is that CAS performs best under low to moderate contention. When many threads hammer the same variable, the retry loop can spin repeatedly and waste CPU, in which case a lock or a different data structure may actually perform better.

Q21. What Is ABA Problem?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior-Level Favorite

Scenario

The ABA problem is a subtle flaw in naive CAS usage. A value changes from A to B and then back to A between a thread's read and its CAS. The CAS succeeds because it only checks that the value still equals A, yet the underlying state may have changed in ways that matter, so the thread proceeds on a false assumption. 


A → B → A

CAS sees the same value A and assumes nothing changed, but the value actually changed twice in between. This is dangerous in structures like lock-free stacks, where a node could be popped, freed and recycled, leaving a dangling reference that CAS happily accepts.

Solution


AtomicStampedReference

The fix is to attach a version stamp or counter to the value, as AtomicStampedReference does, so CAS compares both the value and its stamp. Even if the value returns to A, the stamp will have advanced, so the stale CAS correctly fails.

SECTION 8 - EXECUTOR FRAMEWORK

Q22. Why Executor Framework?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Problem

Creating a new thread for every task is expensive: each thread consumes memory for its stack and requires operating system bookkeeping, and creation and teardown have real cost. Worse, if tasks arrive faster than they finish, unbounded thread creation can exhaust memory and crash the application.

Solution

The Executor framework solves this by decoupling task submission from task execution. You submit work, and a managed thread pool reuses a fixed set of threads to run it, queueing tasks when all threads are busy. This bounds resource usage, amortises thread creation cost, and gives you a clean API for lifecycle management, scheduling and result handling.

Q23. What Is ExecutorService?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

ExecutorService is the central interface of the Executor framework for managing a pool of worker threads. It accepts tasks via execute() and submit(), schedules them onto pooled threads, and provides lifecycle methods such as shutdown() and awaitTermination() to drain and close the pool gracefully.

You typically obtain an implementation from the Executors factory, though in production it is often better to construct a ThreadPoolExecutor directly so you can control the queue type, pool sizes and rejection policy explicitly rather than relying on the defaults.

Example


ExecutorService executor =

Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);
Q24. Benefits Of Thread Pools

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

A thread pool maintains a set of pre-created worker threads that are reused across many tasks, which delivers the following benefits. The recurring theme is that you trade unbounded, unpredictable thread usage for controlled, reusable capacity.

  • Reuse Threads
  • Lower Creation Cost
  • Better Resource Management
  • Improved Throughput

The most important benefit operationally is resource management: a bounded pool acts as a natural backpressure mechanism. If work arrives faster than it can be processed, tasks queue up instead of spawning unlimited threads, which keeps the service stable under load instead of crashing.

Q25. Fixed Thread Pool vs Cached Thread Pool

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

A fixed thread pool keeps a constant number of threads and queues any excess tasks, giving predictable, bounded resource usage. A cached thread pool creates new threads on demand and reuses idle ones, which is flexible for bursty, short-lived tasks but has no upper bound on thread count.

Fixed Cached
Fixed Threads Dynamic Threads
Predictable Potentially Unbounded

Interview Tip

Fixed thread pools are generally safer for production. A cached pool can spin up thousands of threads under a traffic spike and bring down the JVM with out-of-memory errors. Senior engineers often avoid the Executors factory entirely and configure a ThreadPoolExecutor with an explicit bounded queue and a sensible rejection policy, so overload degrades predictably rather than catastrophically.

Q26. submit() vs execute()

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Both methods hand a task to the executor, but they differ in what they accept and what they return. execute() takes only a Runnable and returns nothing, while submit() accepts a Runnable or Callable and returns a Future you can use to retrieve the result or check for completion.

execute submit
No Future Returns Future
Runnable Runnable/Callable

There is an important exception-handling difference too. With execute(), an uncaught exception propagates to the thread's UncaughtExceptionHandler. With submit(), the exception is captured and stored in the Future, so it stays silent until you call future.get() and it is rethrown wrapped in an ExecutionException. Forgetting this is a common source of swallowed errors.

Q27. What Is Future?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

A Future represents the eventual result of an asynchronous computation. When you submit a task to an executor you get a Future immediately, and you can later call get() to retrieve the result, isDone() to poll for completion, or cancel() to attempt cancellation.

The crucial behaviour to understand is that get() blocks the calling thread until the result is ready. This makes Future useful for kicking off work in parallel and then collecting results, but it also means careless use can reintroduce the very blocking you were trying to avoid.

Example


Future future =
executor.submit(task);
Q28. Problems With Future

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Future was a first step toward async programming, but it is limited. Its biggest weakness is that the only way to read the result is the blocking get(), which ties up a thread and makes it awkward to build non-blocking pipelines.

  • Blocking get()
  • No Chaining
  • No Combination
  • Poor Composition

You also cannot register a callback to run when a Future completes, chain a transformation onto its result, or combine several Futures into one. CompletableFuture, introduced in Java 8, solves all of this with methods like thenApply, thenCompose and allOf, enabling fully non-blocking, composable async workflows.

SECTION 9 - LOCKS

Q29. synchronized vs ReentrantLock

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

synchronized is built into the language and acquires and releases the lock implicitly at block boundaries, which is simple and hard to misuse. ReentrantLock is a class-based lock that you acquire and release explicitly, giving you far more control at the cost of having to manage the unlock yourself.

synchronized ReentrantLock
Implicit Lock Explicit Lock
Less Flexible More Features
Auto Release Manual Unlock

ReentrantLock Features

  • tryLock()
  • Fair Locking
  • Interruptible Locking

These extra capabilities are why senior engineers reach for ReentrantLock in advanced scenarios: tryLock() lets a thread give up rather than block forever (useful for deadlock avoidance), fair locking grants the lock in request order to prevent starvation, and interruptible acquisition lets a blocked thread be cancelled. The catch is that you must always release in a finally block, otherwise a thrown exception leaks the lock permanently.

Q30. What Is ReentrantLock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

ReentrantLock is reentrant, meaning a thread that already holds the lock can acquire it again without blocking itself. The lock maintains a hold count that increments on each acquisition and decrements on each release, and the lock is only truly freed when the count returns to zero.

This property is essential whenever a synchronized or locked method calls another method that needs the same lock, such as recursion or one guarded method delegating to another. Without reentrancy, a thread would deadlock against itself. Note that Java's intrinsic synchronized locks are also reentrant for the same reason.

Example

Method A acquires the lock, then calls Method B which acquires the same lock. Because the lock is reentrant, this is allowed; the hold count simply rises to 2 and falls back as each method returns.

Q31. What Is ReadWriteLock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

A ReadWriteLock maintains a pair of locks: a shared read lock and an exclusive write lock. Many threads may hold the read lock simultaneously as long as no thread holds the write lock, but the write lock is exclusive and blocks all readers and other writers while held.

  • Readers
  • Writers

Benefit

This is a major optimisation for read-heavy workloads such as caches or configuration that is read constantly but updated rarely. Allowing concurrent reads dramatically improves throughput compared with a plain mutual-exclusion lock that would serialise every access. A caveat to mention is writer starvation: under a constant stream of readers, a waiting writer may be delayed, which is why ReentrantReadWriteLock offers a fair mode.

SECTION 10 - DEADLOCKS

Q32. What Is Deadlock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

A deadlock is a situation where two or more threads are each waiting for a resource the other holds, so none of them can ever proceed. Each thread is blocked indefinitely, and because no thread will release its lock until it acquires the one it is waiting for, the system is permanently stuck.

Example


Thread 1

Lock A

Waiting For Lock B

Thread 2

Lock B

Waiting For Lock A

Thread 1 holds A and wants B; Thread 2 holds B and wants A. Neither will give up what it has, so both wait forever and the affected part of the system hangs. In production this typically manifests as requests timing out while threads sit blocked, which you can confirm with a thread dump that shows the circular wait.

Q33. Deadlock Conditions

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

A deadlock can only occur when all four of the Coffman conditions hold simultaneously. This matters practically because breaking any single one of them is enough to prevent deadlock entirely, which is the basis of every prevention strategy.

  1. Mutual Exclusion
  2. Hold And Wait
  3. No Preemption
  4. Circular Wait

In short: a resource can be held by only one thread (mutual exclusion), threads hold resources while waiting for more (hold and wait), resources cannot be forcibly taken away (no preemption), and there is a cycle of threads each waiting on the next (circular wait). Circular wait is the easiest condition to break in practice, usually by imposing a global lock ordering.

Q34. How To Prevent Deadlocks?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Deadlock prevention works by ensuring at least one Coffman condition can never hold. The techniques below each target one of those conditions, and the most reliable in everyday code is consistent lock ordering.

  • Consistent Lock Ordering
  • Reduce Lock Scope
  • Use tryLock()
  • Avoid Nested Locks

If every thread always acquires locks in the same global order, a circular wait becomes impossible. Where ordering is hard to guarantee, tryLock() with a timeout lets a thread back off and retry instead of blocking forever, breaking the hold-and-wait condition. Keeping critical sections small and avoiding holding multiple locks at once further reduces the surface area for deadlock.

Q35. What Is Livelock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

In a livelock, threads are not blocked but keep responding to each other's actions in a way that prevents any of them from making progress. They are busy and consuming CPU, repeatedly retrying or yielding, yet no useful work ever completes. The classic analogy is two people in a hallway who keep stepping the same direction to let each other pass.

Difference

Deadlock: the threads are completely stopped and idle, waiting on locks.

Livelock: the threads remain active and keep changing state, but cannot advance. Livelock often arises from naive deadlock-avoidance logic where threads release and retry in lockstep; adding randomised backoff usually breaks the symmetry and lets one proceed.

Q36. What Is Starvation?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Starvation occurs when a thread is perpetually denied the resources or CPU time it needs to make progress, because other threads keep getting priority. Unlike deadlock, the system as a whole keeps working; it is one or more specific threads that are continually passed over.

Common causes include unfair locks that always favour certain threads, thread priorities that let high-priority threads monopolise the CPU, or a steady stream of readers starving a writer in a read-write lock. The usual remedy is fairness policies, which grant access in roughly the order it was requested so no thread waits indefinitely.

SECTION 11 - THREAD SAFETY

Q37. What Is Thread Safety?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Code is thread safe if it continues to behave correctly when accessed by multiple threads at the same time, regardless of how the runtime schedules those threads, and without requiring extra coordination from the callers. Correctness here means the data stays consistent and the program's invariants hold no matter how operations interleave.

The need for thread safety arises specifically around shared mutable state. Stateless code and immutable objects are inherently thread safe because there is nothing to corrupt; the danger appears only when multiple threads can read and write the same changing data, which is what synchronization, locks and atomics exist to manage.

Q38. Ways To Achieve Thread Safety

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

There are several complementary strategies for thread safety, and a good engineer chooses based on the access pattern rather than reaching for the heaviest tool every time. They range from explicit locking to designs that avoid shared mutable state altogether.

  • Synchronization
  • Locks
  • Atomic Classes
  • Immutable Objects
  • Concurrent Collections

synchronized and explicit Locks provide mutual exclusion for arbitrary critical sections; Atomic classes give lock-free safety for single variables; concurrent collections like ConcurrentHashMap handle the locking internally for you; and immutable objects sidestep the problem entirely. The general preference is to avoid shared mutable state first, then use the lightest mechanism that the situation allows.

Q39. Why Are Immutable Objects Thread Safe?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Immutable objects are thread safe because their state is fixed at construction and can never change afterward. Since there are no writes after the object is published, there is nothing for concurrent threads to corrupt, and every thread always sees a consistent value without any synchronization.

This is why immutability is one of the most powerful and underused concurrency tools: it eliminates the entire class of race conditions by design rather than guarding against them at runtime. Once safely published, an immutable object can be shared freely across any number of threads, which also makes it ideal for caching and as map keys.

Example


String

LocalDate

Record Classes

PART 2 COMPLETE

Next Part: * Java Memory Model * Happens-Before Relationship * Visibility Problems * Instruction Reordering * CountDownLatch * CyclicBarrier * Semaphore * ForkJoinPool * CompletableFuture Internals * Advanced Production Scenarios These are the questions typically asked in strong product companies and senior backend interviews.

06 - JVM & GARBAGE COLLECTION INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

This section is one of the strongest differentiators between: Junior/Mid Developers and Senior Backend Engineers. Most developers use Java. Few understand how Java actually runs. Interviewers frequently ask JVM questions because they reveal: * Performance Understanding * Memory Management Knowledge * Troubleshooting Skills * Production Readiness

TOP ROI JVM QUESTIONS

  1. JVM Architecture
  2. Heap vs Stack
  3. What Happens When Object Is Created?
  4. Class Loader
  5. Young Generation
  6. Old Generation
  7. Minor GC
  8. Major GC
  9. G1 GC
  10. Memory Leak
  11. OutOfMemoryError
  12. StackOverflowError
  13. Metaspace
  14. String Pool
  15. GC Tuning Basics

SECTION 1 - JVM ARCHITECTURE

Q1. What Is JVM?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is an abstract computing machine that provides the runtime environment in which Java bytecode executes. It is the component that makes Java's "write once, run anywhere" promise real: the same compiled .class file runs unchanged on Windows, Linux or macOS because each platform ships a JVM that knows how to turn portable bytecode into native machine instructions for that hardware.

JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is responsible for:

  • Loading Classes
  • Executing Bytecode
  • Managing Memory
  • Garbage Collection
  • Security
  • Optimization

Beyond simply interpreting bytecode, a modern JVM such as HotSpot includes a Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler that profiles your running code and compiles hot paths into optimised native code, which is why a long-running Java service often gets faster after warming up. It also owns memory management and garbage collection, enforces bytecode verification for security, and abstracts the operating system away from your application code.

Flow


.java

↓

javac

↓

.class

↓

JVM

↓

Machine Code

The flow above is the lifecycle of Java code: you write .java source, javac compiles it into platform-independent .class bytecode, and the JVM loads, verifies and executes that bytecode, ultimately producing native machine code. Understanding this pipeline is the foundation for every other JVM and garbage-collection topic in this handbook.

Q2. JVM vs JRE vs JDK

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

JDK JRE JVM
Development Execution Virtual Machine
Contains JRE Contains JVM Runs Bytecode

These three are nested layers, not alternatives. The JVM is the engine that actually runs bytecode. The JRE (Java Runtime Environment) wraps the JVM together with the core class libraries needed to run an application, but it has no compiler. The JDK (Java Development Kit) is the full developer toolkit: it contains the JRE plus development tools such as javac, jar, javadoc and the diagnostic utilities (jmap, jstack).

A useful way to remember it: JDK is for building applications, JRE is for running them, and the JVM is the abstract machine inside the JRE that executes the bytecode. Note that since Java 11 the standalone JRE is no longer shipped separately by Oracle, so in practice teams install the JDK everywhere, but the conceptual distinction is still asked in interviews.

SECTION 2 - JVM MEMORY AREAS

Q3. JVM Memory Structure

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High 


JVM Memory

├── Heap

├── Stack

├── Metaspace

├── PC Register

└── Native Method Stack

The JVM divides its runtime memory into distinct regions, each with a clear responsibility. The Heap is the large, shared area where all objects and arrays live and where garbage collection operates. Each thread gets its own Stack, which stores method call frames, local variables and partial results. Metaspace holds class metadata and lives in native memory outside the heap.

The remaining two are per-thread and smaller: the PC (Program Counter) Register tracks the address of the bytecode instruction the thread is currently executing, and the Native Method Stack supports native (non-Java) method calls made through JNI. Knowing which structures are shared across threads (Heap, Metaspace) versus per-thread (Stack, PC Register, Native Method Stack) is exactly the distinction that explains thread-safety and memory-error behaviour later in this file.

Q4. Heap vs Stack

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Interview Favorite

Heap Stack
Objects Method Frames
Shared Per Thread
GC Managed Automatic Cleanup
Large Smaller

The Heap stores every object instance and is shared across all threads, which is why object access can require synchronization and why the heap is the domain of the garbage collector. The Stack is private to each thread and holds method frames containing local variables and the operand stack for that method call. Stack memory is reclaimed automatically and deterministically: when a method returns, its frame is popped, so there is no garbage collection involved.

This difference drives the two classic memory errors. Exhausting the heap produces an OutOfMemoryError, while exhausting a thread's stack (typically through deep or infinite recursion) produces a StackOverflowError. The stack is also far smaller than the heap by design, since each thread needs its own copy.

Example


User user = new User();

Reference → Stack Object → Heap

In the line above, the new User() object itself is allocated on the heap, while the local variable user — which merely holds a reference (a pointer) to that object — lives in the current method's stack frame. When the method returns, the reference disappears from the stack, and once no live reference points to the object, it becomes eligible for garbage collection on the heap.

Q5. What Happens When Object Is Created?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Steps

  1. Class Loaded
  2. Memory Allocated In Heap
  3. Fields Initialized
  4. Constructor Executes
  5. Reference Returned

When you call new, the JVM first ensures the class is loaded, linked and initialized (this happens only once per class). It then allocates a contiguous block of memory on the heap large enough for the object's fields, and zeroes that memory so every field starts at its default value (0, false or null). Next the constructor chain runs — instance initializers and the constructor body assign the real values — and finally a reference to the fully constructed object is returned and stored in the variable on the stack.

This ordering matters in interviews because it explains why fields have safe defaults before the constructor runs, and why an object can briefly exist in a partially-initialized state. In modern JVMs the actual heap allocation is extremely cheap thanks to thread-local allocation buffers (TLABs), which let each thread bump-allocate in its own slice of Eden without contending for a global lock.

Follow-Up

Where is reference stored? Answer: Stack

The object lives on the shared heap, but the reference to it is stored wherever the variable lives — for a local variable that means the current thread's stack frame. If the reference is instead an instance field of another object, it is stored inside that object on the heap, and if it is a static field it is reachable from the class metadata. The interviewer is checking that you do not confuse the location of the object with the location of the reference pointing to it.

Q6. What Is Metaspace?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Stores class metadata. Introduced in Java 8. Replaced PermGen.

Metaspace is the region where the JVM keeps class-level information — the runtime representation of each loaded class, including its methods, field layouts and constant pool. The critical change in Java 8 is that Metaspace lives in native memory rather than inside the Java heap, so it is no longer bounded by the heap size and grows dynamically as classes are loaded.

This move solved the chronic java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: PermGen space failures that plagued earlier versions, especially in application servers that repeatedly loaded and reloaded classes. It is still possible to exhaust Metaspace (you would see OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace), typically caused by classloader leaks or dynamically generating an unbounded number of classes, so it can be capped with -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize.

Contains

  • Class Definitions
  • Method Metadata
  • Runtime Information
Q7. PermGen vs Metaspace

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

PermGen Metaspace
Java 7 Java 8+
Fixed Size Dynamic
Inside JVM Heap Native Memory

PermGen (Permanent Generation) was the pre-Java-8 home for class metadata, and it had two painful limitations: it was a fixed-size region carved out of the Java heap, and its default size was small. Applications that loaded many classes — or worse, leaked classloaders during hot redeploys — would hit OutOfMemoryError: PermGen space even when plenty of heap remained.

Metaspace replaces it by moving metadata into native memory that grows automatically by default. This removed the most common reason developers had to tune -XX:MaxPermSize, but it shifted the risk: an unbounded Metaspace leak now consumes native OS memory instead. The practical senior takeaway is that you rarely tune Metaspace, but you should still set a ceiling in long-lived services to catch classloader leaks before they exhaust the machine.

SECTION 3 - GARBAGE COLLECTION BASICS

Q8. What Is Garbage Collection?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Automatic memory management process. Removes objects that are no longer reachable.

Garbage collection is the JVM's automatic memory-management mechanism. Instead of forcing developers to manually allocate and free memory the way C and C++ require, the JVM tracks which objects are still in use and reclaims the memory of objects that can no longer be reached by the running program. This eliminates entire categories of bugs — dangling pointers, double frees and most manual memory leaks — that dominate lower-level languages.

Benefit

Developers do not manually free memory.

The trade-off is that the developer gives up precise control over when memory is released and pays a runtime cost for the GC itself, including occasional pauses while it works. Understanding that GC is automatic but not free is the foundation for the performance and tuning discussions later in this handbook: a senior engineer's job is not to avoid GC but to write code that produces less garbage and to pick and tune the right collector.

Q9. How Does JVM Identify Garbage?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Using Reachability Analysis.

If an object cannot be reached from GC Roots, it becomes eligible for collection.

The JVM does not use reference counting (which cannot handle cyclic references). Instead it performs reachability analysis: starting from a set of known-live anchors called GC Roots, it traces every reference chain and marks every object it can reach as live. Anything not reached during this trace is, by definition, unreachable by the program and therefore garbage. This is why two objects that reference only each other but are not reachable from any root are still collected — the cycle is unreachable as a whole.

GC Roots

  • Local Variables
  • Static Variables
  • Active Threads
  • JNI References

GC Roots are the entry points the collector trusts as always live: local variables and parameters on active stack frames, static fields of loaded classes, live thread objects, and references held by native code through JNI. This list also explains the most common Java memory leaks — a static collection or a long-lived ThreadLocal keeps an object reachable from a root forever, so even though the object is logically useless, the GC correctly refuses to collect it.

Q10. Is Eligible For GC Same As Guaranteed GC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

No. Eligibility only means JVM may collect it. There is no guarantee when GC will occur.

Eligibility and collection are two separate things. An object becomes eligible the moment it is no longer reachable from any GC Root, but the actual collection happens only when the JVM decides to run a GC cycle — typically when a memory region fills up. The object could be reclaimed milliseconds later, or it could sit in memory for a long time if there is no allocation pressure.

This is also why calling System.gc() is only a hint: the JVM is free to ignore it, and relying on it in production code is an anti-pattern. The senior-level point interviewers are listening for is that you never write code whose correctness depends on when an object is collected — that is exactly the mistake the old finalize() method encouraged, and why deterministic cleanup belongs in try-with-resources instead.

SECTION 4 - HEAP GENERATIONS

One of the most common JVM interview topics. Interviewers often ask: "Where does a newly created object go?" "Why is Young Generation needed?"

Q11. Heap Memory Structure

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High 


Heap

├── Young Generation
│   ├── Eden
│   ├── Survivor S0
│   └── Survivor S1
│
└── Old Generation

The heap is split into generations based on a powerful empirical observation known as the weak generational hypothesis: most objects die young, and the few that survive tend to live a long time. The Young Generation is where all new objects are born, and it is subdivided into Eden (where objects are first allocated) and two Survivor spaces, S0 and S1, that hold objects which have survived at least one collection.

The Old Generation (also called the Tenured generation) holds objects that have survived enough Young Generation collections to be considered long-lived. This separation lets the JVM collect the Young Generation very frequently and cheaply while touching the Old Generation only rarely, which is the core idea behind generational garbage collection.

Q12. What Is Young Generation?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Newly created objects are allocated here.

The Young Generation is the nursery of the heap: nearly every object you create with new starts its life here, in Eden. It is sized to be collected often and quickly, because the generational hypothesis tells us most of these objects will become garbage almost immediately.

Reason

Most objects die young. Examples: Request DTOs Temporary Collections Method Variables

In a typical Spring Boot service this pattern is everywhere: a request DTO, the intermediate collections you build while processing it, and the local variables inside a method are all created to serve one request and are discarded the moment the response is sent. Because these short-lived objects dominate allocation, concentrating them in a small, frequently-collected region makes garbage collection fast — a Young Generation collection only has to copy out the handful of survivors and can discard everything else wholesale.

Q13. What Is Eden Space?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

New objects are initially created in Eden.

Eden is the specific slice of the Young Generation where brand-new objects are allocated. Allocation here is extremely fast because it is essentially a pointer bump — the JVM simply advances a pointer to carve out the next chunk of memory, often within a thread-local allocation buffer so threads do not contend. When Eden fills up, a Minor GC is triggered.

Flow


New Object

↓

Eden

↓

Minor GC

↓

Survivor

↓

Old Generation

The flow above traces an object's possible journey: it is born in Eden, and if it is still reachable when a Minor GC runs, it is copied into a Survivor space rather than discarded. Each time it survives another collection its "age" counter increments, and once that age crosses a tenuring threshold the JVM promotes it to the Old Generation, assuming it has proven itself long-lived. The vast majority of objects never make it past the first step — they are simply reclaimed when Eden is collected.

Q14. What Are Survivor Spaces?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Temporary holding areas for objects that survive Minor GC. 


S0

S1

Objects move between survivor spaces before promotion.

There are two Survivor spaces, S0 and S1, and at any moment one is empty while the other holds the current survivors. During each Minor GC the JVM uses a copying algorithm: it copies all live objects from Eden and the in-use Survivor space into the previously empty Survivor space, then wipes the now-vacated regions clean. The two spaces simply swap roles on each cycle.

This ping-pong design serves a purpose — it compacts live objects together (eliminating fragmentation) and lets the JVM track how many collections each object has survived. Once an object's survival count reaches the tenuring threshold, instead of being copied to the other Survivor space it is promoted to the Old Generation. Having one Survivor space always empty is also why you should not be alarmed to see one of them showing zero usage in monitoring tools.

Q15. What Is Old Generation?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Stores long-lived objects.

The Old Generation (Tenured space) is where objects end up once they have survived enough Young Generation collections to be deemed durable. It is collected far less frequently than the Young Generation, but those collections are more expensive because the region is larger and the objects in it are densely packed with live data.

Examples

  • Application Caches
  • Singleton Beans
  • Large Data Structures
  • Long-Lived Sessions

The examples here are the kinds of objects that genuinely should live a long time: an in-memory cache that serves the whole application, the singleton Spring beans created at startup and held for the lifetime of the context, large reference data structures, and long-lived session state. Because these legitimately occupy the Old Generation, a healthy service shows a stable Old Generation; an Old Generation that keeps climbing over time is a classic memory-leak signature worth investigating.

Q16. What Is Object Promotion?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Moving surviving objects from Young Generation to Old Generation.

Why?

Object survived multiple garbage collection cycles. JVM assumes it is long-lived.

Promotion (also called tenuring) happens when an object's survival count crosses the tenuring threshold, signalling that it has outlived the assumption that "most objects die young." Rather than keep copying it back and forth between Survivor spaces on every Minor GC, the JVM moves it to the Old Generation where collections are rare, sparing the cost of repeatedly relocating an object that clearly is not going away.

A senior-level nuance worth mentioning is premature promotion: if the Young Generation or Survivor spaces are too small, objects get promoted before they have had a chance to die, polluting the Old Generation with short-lived garbage and triggering more frequent, expensive Old Generation collections. This is one of the most common reasons GC tuning involves resizing the Young Generation.

SECTION 5 - GARBAGE COLLECTION TYPES

Q17. What Is Minor GC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

GC performed on Young Generation.

A Minor GC collects only the Young Generation (Eden plus the active Survivor space). It is triggered when Eden fills up, and because it uses a copying algorithm that only needs to relocate the small set of survivors, it is fast. Importantly, even a Minor GC is a stop-the-world event — all application threads are briefly paused — but the pause is usually short enough to be imperceptible.

Characteristics

  • Frequent
  • Fast
  • Usually Low Impact

Minor GCs are frequent precisely because the Young Generation is small and high-churn, but each one is cheap, so in a healthy application they barely register. The pattern to watch for in production is not the frequency of Minor GCs but whether each one is promoting too much into the Old Generation — that is the early warning sign of a heap that is mis-sized or an application that is retaining objects longer than it should.

Q18. What Is Major GC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

GC performed on Old Generation.

A Major GC collects the Old Generation, where long-lived objects accumulate. Because the Old Generation is larger and its objects are mostly live (they earned their place by surviving), reclaiming space there involves more work — marking live objects, then sweeping or compacting — and therefore causes longer pauses than a Minor GC.

Characteristics

  • Less Frequent
  • More Expensive
  • Longer Pauses

The terminology can be ambiguous in interviews, so it is worth being precise: in many JVMs a Major GC effectively coincides with a Full GC because cleaning the Old Generation often requires touching the whole heap. The practical concern is the same regardless of the label — Major and Full collections are the ones that produce the noticeable application pauses, so reducing how often objects reach the Old Generation is a key tuning goal.

Q19. What Is Full GC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Collection of entire heap.

A Full GC collects the entire heap — Young Generation, Old Generation, and typically Metaspace as well — in a single, comprehensive pass. It is the heaviest collection the JVM performs and almost always involves a long stop-the-world pause during which every application thread is frozen.

Impact

Can cause significant application pauses.

During a Full GC the application does no useful work, so requests queue up and latency spikes. A Full GC is triggered by conditions such as the Old Generation filling up, Metaspace pressure, or an explicit (and discouraged) System.gc() call. In a latency-sensitive service even a single multi-second Full GC can cause request timeouts, health-check failures and cascading retries.

Interview Gold Point

Frequent Full GC is often a production problem.

If you see Full GCs happening repeatedly, it is rarely a tuning detail — it is usually a symptom of a real defect. The most common root causes are a memory leak slowly filling the Old Generation, an undersized heap for the workload, or excessive premature promotion stuffing short-lived objects into Old. The strong interview answer is to treat recurring Full GC as a signal to capture GC logs and a heap dump and find what is being retained, rather than to simply enlarge the heap and hope.

Q20. Why Is Full GC Dangerous?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

  • Long Stop-The-World Pauses
  • Reduced Throughput
  • API Latency Spikes
  • User Experience Impact

The danger of a Full GC is that it is a "stop-the-world" event: the JVM suspends every application thread while it walks and cleans the entire heap. For the duration of that pause your service is effectively offline — it accepts no work, completes no requests, and answers no health checks. In a system with tight latency SLAs, a Full GC pause of even a second or two translates directly into a visible reliability incident.

Production Symptom

Spring Boot APIs suddenly become slow.

In practice this shows up as a Spring Boot service whose response times are normally fast but periodically spike, often in a sawtooth pattern that lines up exactly with Full GC events in the GC logs. Downstream, load balancers may mark the instance unhealthy during the pause and clients may retry, amplifying the load. The senior engineer's instinct is to correlate the latency spikes with GC logs, confirm Full GC is the cause, and then attack the underlying reason — a leak, an undersized heap, or the wrong collector — rather than masking the symptom.

SECTION 6 - GARBAGE COLLECTORS

Q21. Common Garbage Collectors

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

  • Serial GC
  • Parallel GC
  • CMS (Deprecated)
  • G1 GC
  • ZGC
  • Shenandoah

The JVM ships with several collectors, each optimised for a different balance of throughput, latency and footprint. Serial GC uses a single thread and is fine only for small, single-core or low-memory environments. Parallel GC (the "throughput collector") uses multiple threads to maximise total work done, at the cost of longer pauses, which suits batch jobs. CMS (Concurrent Mark Sweep) historically reduced pause times by doing most marking concurrently, but it has been removed in recent JDKs.

G1 GC is the modern default and aims for a predictable balance of low pauses and good throughput on multi-gigabyte heaps. ZGC and Shenandoah are the newest generation of concurrent collectors, designed to keep pauses in the sub-millisecond range even on very large heaps. The senior takeaway is that there is no universally "best" collector — the right choice depends on whether your application prioritises throughput (Parallel) or low and predictable latency (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah).

Q22. What Is G1 Garbage Collector?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

G1 (Garbage First) is the default collector in modern JVMs.

G1, the Garbage-First collector, has been the default since Java 9 and is designed for server applications running on multi-core machines with large heaps. Its defining innovation is that it divides the heap into many equally-sized regions and dynamically labels each region as Eden, Survivor or Old, rather than using fixed contiguous generations. It then collects the regions that contain the most garbage first — hence "Garbage First" — to reclaim the most memory for the least effort.

Features

  • Low Pause Times
  • Region-Based Heap
  • Predictable Performance
  • Large Heap Friendly

The headline feature is that G1 lets you specify a soft pause-time goal (via -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis), and it sizes its collection work to try to meet that target, which gives far more predictable latency than the older collectors. Because it works region by region and does much of its marking concurrently with the application, it handles large heaps gracefully without the long, monolithic pauses that Parallel GC would incur on the same heap.

Interview Gold Point

G1 divides heap into regions instead of fixed generations.

This is the single sentence that signals you actually understand G1 rather than just naming it. In the older collectors the Young and Old generations are fixed, contiguous blocks of memory; in G1 the generations are logical labels applied to a grid of uniform regions, and a region can be reassigned from one role to another between collections. This region model is what enables incremental collection, compaction without a full-heap pass, and the tunable pause-time targets that make G1 the sensible default for most enterprise services.

Q23. Why G1 GC Is Popular?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Balances:

  • Latency
  • Throughput
  • Memory Efficiency

Suitable for most enterprise applications.

G1 is popular because it is the sensible "general-purpose" default: it delivers low and predictable pause times without sacrificing too much throughput, and it scales comfortably from a few gigabytes up to very large heaps. For the overwhelming majority of Spring Boot services and enterprise web applications, G1 gives good behaviour out of the box with little or no tuning, which is exactly why it was made the default.

The honest senior framing is that G1 wins on being a strong all-rounder rather than being the best at any single metric. If you needed absolute maximum throughput for a batch workload you might choose Parallel GC, and if you needed sub-millisecond pauses on a huge heap you would reach for ZGC or Shenandoah — but for typical request/response services, G1's balance is hard to beat, so it is the safe choice you should be prepared to justify.

Q24. What Is ZGC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Increasingly Common

Answer

Low-latency garbage collector designed for very large heaps.

ZGC is a scalable, concurrent collector built specifically to keep pause times extremely low regardless of heap size. It achieves this by performing almost all of its work — including the expensive relocation of live objects — concurrently with the running application, using techniques such as coloured pointers and load barriers so that threads almost never have to stop. It scales from small heaps up into the terabyte range.

Goal

Keep pause times extremely low.

ZGC's design goal is for pause times to stay in the sub-millisecond range and, crucially, to remain flat as the heap grows — a 10 GB heap and a 1 TB heap should see similar pauses. This is fundamentally different from older collectors whose pause times grow with the amount of live data, and it is the property that makes ZGC attractive for systems where even occasional tens-of-milliseconds GC pauses are unacceptable.

Typical Use Cases

  • Large Scale Systems
  • Trading Systems
  • High Throughput APIs

The natural fit for ZGC is latency-critical workloads on large heaps: financial trading platforms where microseconds matter, real-time bidding and gaming backends, and high-throughput APIs with strict tail-latency requirements. The trade-off to mention is that this near-pauseless behaviour costs some throughput and additional memory overhead compared with G1 or Parallel, so ZGC is the right tool when latency is the dominant requirement rather than a default for every service.

SECTION 7 - MEMORY PROBLEMS

Q25. What Is Memory Leak In Java?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Important Point

Java can still have memory leaks.

A common misconception is that garbage collection makes memory leaks impossible. It does not. Garbage collection only reclaims objects that are unreachable — so if your code unintentionally keeps a reference to an object you no longer need, the GC will faithfully keep it alive forever. In Java a memory leak is therefore a logical leak: the memory is still referenced but is no longer useful.

How?

Objects remain reachable but no longer useful.

Examples

  • Static Collections
  • Cache Misuse
  • Listener Leaks
  • ThreadLocal Leaks

The classic culprits all share the pattern of an object staying reachable from a GC Root indefinitely. A static collection lives for the entire application lifetime, so anything added and never removed accumulates forever. A cache without an eviction policy grows without bound. Registered listeners or callbacks that are never deregistered keep their target objects alive. And a ThreadLocal on a pooled thread (such as a Tomcat request thread) can pin objects long after the request ends because the thread itself is never garbage collected. Recognising these patterns — and removing entries, bounding caches, and cleaning up ThreadLocals in a finally block — is the practical defence.

Q26. Example Of Memory Leak

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High 


private static List users
    = new ArrayList<>();

Objects continuously added. Never removed. Memory grows forever.

This is the textbook Java leak. Because the list is static, it is reachable from a GC Root (the class) for the entire life of the application, and every User it holds is therefore reachable too. If code keeps appending users — for example caching every request's user without ever evicting them — the list grows unbounded, the Old Generation steadily fills, Full GCs become more frequent and longer, and eventually the JVM throws an OutOfMemoryError.

The fix depends on intent: if the collection is meant to be a cache, give it a bounded, evicting implementation (such as a size- or time-limited cache rather than a raw ArrayList); if entries become obsolete, remove them explicitly; and be especially wary of static collections, since their root-level reachability is exactly what turns an ordinary list into a leak.

Q27. What Is OutOfMemoryError?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

JVM cannot allocate required memory.

An OutOfMemoryError is thrown when the JVM cannot allocate memory for a new object and the garbage collector cannot free enough space to satisfy the request. It is an Error, not an Exception, signalling that the situation is generally not recoverable by ordinary application logic. The most common variant is OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space, but you can also see Metaspace, GC overhead limit exceeded (when the JVM spends almost all its time in GC reclaiming almost nothing), and unable to create new native thread.

Common Causes

  • Memory Leak
  • Huge Collections
  • Insufficient Heap
  • Infinite Object Creation

The causes fall into two buckets: the heap is genuinely too small for a legitimate workload (just raise -Xmx), or — far more often in production — something is retaining objects that should have been released, i.e. a leak. The professional response is to run the JVM with -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError so a heap dump is captured automatically when it happens, then analyse that dump in a tool like Eclipse MAT to find the dominator objects and reference chains holding the memory, rather than blindly increasing the heap and merely delaying the crash.

Q28. What Is StackOverflowError?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Occurs when call stack exceeds available stack memory.

A StackOverflowError happens when a thread's call stack runs out of space. Every method call pushes a new frame (holding parameters, local variables and the return address) onto the thread's stack, and that stack has a fixed maximum size. When the nesting of calls goes too deep, the stack limit is breached and the JVM throws this error. Unlike OutOfMemoryError, it concerns the per-thread stack rather than the shared heap.

Common Cause


Infinite Recursion

By far the most common cause is unbounded recursion — a method that calls itself (directly or through a cycle) without a correct base case, so the frames pile up until the stack is exhausted. Less commonly it can result from extremely deep but legitimate recursion on a problem that should have been solved iteratively. The fix is to add or correct the base case, convert deep recursion to an iterative loop, and only as a last resort increase the thread stack size with -Xss.

Q29. OutOfMemoryError vs StackOverflowError

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

OOM SOE
Heap Related Stack Related
Memory Exhaustion Deep Recursion

The two errors look similar in name but originate in completely different memory regions, which is exactly why the comparison is asked. OutOfMemoryError is about the shared heap (or related native areas like Metaspace) running out of allocatable space, typically due to a memory leak, oversized data structures, or an undersized heap. StackOverflowError is about a single thread's call stack overflowing, almost always due to runaway recursion.

Both extend Error rather than Exception, so neither should normally be caught and "handled" in business logic. The clean mental model to state in an interview is: heap problem versus stack problem, and memory-retention bug versus recursion bug. Getting that distinction right immediately shows you understand the JVM memory model from Section 2.

SECTION 8 - PRODUCTION TROUBLESHOOTING

Q30. Spring Boot Service Memory Keeps Growing. What Do You Check?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer Framework

  1. Heap Usage
  2. GC Logs
  3. Heap Dumps
  4. Large Collections
  5. Caches
  6. ThreadLocals
  7. Static References

This is a leak investigation, and interviewers want to hear a systematic process rather than guesses. Start by confirming the symptom with monitoring: watch heap usage over time and check whether the Old Generation trends upward across GC cycles instead of returning to a stable baseline — a steadily rising post-GC floor is the signature of a leak rather than normal churn. Then enable and read the GC logs to see whether Full GCs are becoming more frequent and reclaiming less each time.

Once a leak is confirmed, capture a heap dump and analyse it to find what is being retained. The usual suspects map directly to the list above: unbounded or unevicted caches, large collections that only ever grow, static references holding objects for the application's lifetime, and ThreadLocal values left on pooled request threads. Walking the reference chain from the biggest retained objects back to a GC Root tells you precisely which code is holding on.

Tools

  • jmap
  • jstack
  • VisualVM
  • JMC
  • MAT

Each tool has a role: jmap captures a heap dump (or prints a histogram of live objects), jstack dumps thread stacks to spot stuck or runaway threads, VisualVM and Java Mission Control (JMC) give live monitoring of heap, GC and threads, and Eclipse Memory Analyzer (MAT) is the gold standard for offline heap-dump analysis with its leak-suspect reports and dominator tree. Naming these and knowing what each is for is what distinguishes an engineer who has actually debugged a production leak from one who has only read about them.

Q31. How Do You Analyze Heap Dumps?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Look for:

  • Largest Objects
  • Retained Memory
  • Leak Suspects
  • Reference Chains

Heap-dump analysis (typically in Eclipse MAT) starts by sorting objects by retained size rather than shallow size. Shallow size is just the object itself; retained size is all the memory that would be freed if that object were collected — including everything reachable only through it. The objects with the largest retained size are the "dominators," and they are where the memory is actually trapped.

From those dominators you follow the reference chain back to a GC Root using a "path to GC Roots" view, which reveals exactly which field, static reference or ThreadLocal is keeping the memory alive. Tools like MAT also generate an automatic Leak Suspects report that flags unusually large retained sets. The whole exercise is about answering one question: what is still holding a reference to memory that should have been released, and from where?

Q32. How Would You Reduce GC Pressure?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

  • Reduce Object Creation
  • Reuse Objects Carefully
  • Optimize Collections
  • Remove Memory Leaks
  • Tune Heap Size
  • Choose Proper GC

GC pressure is the rate at which your application creates garbage; the more short-lived objects you allocate, the more often the collector must run. The highest-leverage fix is almost always to allocate less in hot paths — avoid creating throwaway objects inside tight loops, prefer streaming over materialising huge intermediate collections, and size collections like ArrayList and HashMap with their expected capacity so they do not repeatedly resize and discard backing arrays.

"Reuse objects carefully" is deliberately cautious advice: object pooling can help for genuinely expensive resources, but pooling cheap objects often does more harm than good because modern Young Generation collection of short-lived objects is extremely fast, and pooling can instead create leaks or false sharing. After reducing allocation, eliminate any real leaks (they force premature promotion and Full GCs), then tune heap sizing and finally select the collector that matches your latency-versus-throughput goal — for example G1 for balanced services or ZGC for ultra-low-pause requirements.

SENIOR INTERVIEW RAPID FIRE

  1. Can Java have memory leaks? → Yes
  2. Can GC guarantee memory cleanup immediately? → No
  3. Where are objects stored? → Heap
  4. Where are references stored? → Stack (local variables)
  5. What replaced PermGen? → Metaspace
  6. What triggers Minor GC? → Young Generation pressure
  7. What triggers Full GC? → Various heap pressure conditions
  8. Can System.gc() force GC? → No guarantee
  9. Default modern GC? → G1 GC
  10. Purpose of ZGC? → Very low pause times

JVM FILE COMPLETE

You now have coverage for: * JVM Architecture * Heap & Stack * Metaspace * Young Generation * Old Generation * Minor GC * Major GC * Full GC * G1 GC * ZGC * Memory Leaks * OutOfMemoryError * StackOverflowError * Production Troubleshooting

NEXT FILE: 07-SPRING-CORE.html

This is where interview relevance becomes extremely high for your profile. We'll cover: * IoC * Dependency Injection * Bean Lifecycle * Bean Scopes * @Component vs @Service vs @Repository * @Autowired * Circular Dependencies * ApplicationContext * Spring Internals * AOP * Proxy Pattern * Transaction Internals These questions appear in almost every Spring Boot interview.

07 - SPRING CORE INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

This is one of the highest ROI sections for Spring Boot interviews. Most interviewers start with Spring Core before moving into: * Spring Boot * Spring Security * JPA/Hibernate * Microservices A surprising number of developers use Spring daily but cannot explain: * How Dependency Injection actually works * Bean Lifecycle * Bean Scopes * AOP Internals * Proxy Pattern * Transaction Management These are favorite senior-level interview topics.

TOP ROI QUESTIONS

  1. What Is Spring Framework?
  2. What Is IoC?
  3. What Is Dependency Injection?
  4. Constructor vs Field Injection
  5. Bean Lifecycle
  6. Bean Scopes
  7. @Component vs @Service vs @Repository
  8. ApplicationContext vs BeanFactory
  9. What Is AOP?
  10. How @Transactional Works?
  11. Proxy Pattern
  12. Circular Dependency
  13. Spring Bean Creation Process

SECTION 1 - SPRING FUNDAMENTALS

Q1. What Is Spring Framework?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Spring is an enterprise Java framework that simplifies application development through:

  • Dependency Injection
  • Inversion Of Control
  • AOP
  • Transaction Management
  • Integration Support

At its heart, Spring is a container that creates and wires your objects for you. Instead of classes constructing their own collaborators, the framework manages object creation, configuration and lifecycle, which inverts the traditional flow of control. This single idea is what makes the rest of the ecosystem possible.

Around that core, Spring layers powerful infrastructure: AOP for cross-cutting concerns like logging and security, declarative transaction management, and a vast set of integrations for databases, messaging and web. Spring Boot then sits on top of all of this to provide auto-configuration and opinionated defaults, which is why most modern Java teams build on the Spring stack.

Benefits

  • Loose Coupling
  • Testability
  • Maintainability
  • Scalability

Because dependencies are injected rather than hard-coded, classes depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations. This loose coupling is what makes Spring code easy to unit test (you can inject mocks) and easy to evolve (you can swap implementations without editing callers), which directly translates into long-term maintainability on large teams.

Q2. What Problems Did Spring Solve?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Before Spring


UserService service =
new UserService();

Application manually created dependencies. Every class was responsible for constructing the objects it needed, so the wiring logic was scattered throughout the codebase. This created tight coupling: a class was bound to a specific concrete implementation, and changing that implementation meant editing every place that constructed it.

Manual wiring also made unit testing painful. Because a class created its own collaborators with new, there was no clean way to substitute a mock or stub, so tests often required a real database or external service to run at all.

With Spring

Container manages dependencies. Spring builds the object graph for you and injects collaborators where they are needed, so wiring lives in one place (configuration) instead of being duplicated across the application.

This promotes loose coupling. Classes declare what they need through interfaces, and the container supplies an implementation at runtime. The result is code that is easier to test, configure and refactor, because the dependency relationships are explicit and externally controlled rather than hard-wired.

SECTION 2 - IOC (INVERSION OF CONTROL)

Q3. What Is IoC?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Short Answer

Control of object creation is transferred from application code to the Spring container. Instead of your code deciding when and how to build its dependencies, the framework takes over that responsibility, which is why it is called "inversion" of control.

Without IoC


UserRepository repo =
new UserRepository();

With IoC


@Autowired

UserRepository repo;

Spring creates and manages the object. In the first example the class explicitly constructs its dependency and is locked to that concrete type. In the second, the class simply declares that it needs a UserRepository and the container provides one, so the class no longer knows or cares how that dependency is built.

The practical payoff is that you can change which implementation is supplied (for example a real repository in production and a mock in tests) without touching the consuming class. IoC is the general principle; Dependency Injection is the specific technique Spring uses to achieve it.

Q4. What Is IoC Container?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

The IoC container is the core Spring component responsible for:

  • Bean Creation
  • Dependency Injection
  • Lifecycle Management
  • Configuration Management

The container reads bean definitions (from annotations, Java config or XML), instantiates the objects, resolves and injects their dependencies in the correct order, and manages them from creation through destruction. It is essentially a factory and registry combined: it knows how to build every managed object and hands out the right instances on demand.

In Spring this container is represented by the ApplicationContext. Understanding that "the container" is just the runtime engine driving creation, wiring and lifecycle is the foundation for explaining bean scopes, lifecycle callbacks and proxy creation later in the interview.

SECTION 3 - DEPENDENCY INJECTION

Q5. What Is Dependency Injection?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Dependency Injection (DI) means dependencies are provided to an object externally instead of being created internally. Rather than a class calling new to build its collaborators, the container passes them in, typically through the constructor.

Example


@Service
public class UserService {

   private final UserRepository repo;

   public UserService(
      UserRepository repo
   ){
      this.repo = repo;
   }
}

Here UserService never constructs a UserRepository; it simply declares that it requires one in its constructor, and Spring supplies the bean at startup. This keeps the class focused on its own logic and decoupled from how its dependency is built or configured.

DI is the concrete mechanism that implements Inversion of Control. It makes dependencies explicit (you can see exactly what a class needs by reading its constructor) and makes the class trivially testable, because a test can pass in a mock repository directly without involving the Spring container at all.

Q6. Types Of Dependency Injection

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

  • Constructor Injection
  • Setter Injection
  • Field Injection

Constructor injection supplies dependencies through the constructor and is the recommended approach, because it makes dependencies mandatory and allows them to be final. Setter injection supplies them through setter methods, which is useful for genuinely optional dependencies that may be reconfigured after construction.

Field injection places the dependency directly onto a field using @Autowired. It is the most concise to write but is discouraged because it hides dependencies, prevents fields from being final, and makes the class hard to instantiate in a plain unit test without reflection or the Spring container.

Q7. Constructor Injection vs Field Injection

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Constructor Field
Immutable Mutable
Test Friendly Harder To Test
Recommended Avoid

Constructor injection lets dependencies be declared final, so the object is fully initialized and immutable once constructed, and it cannot exist in a half-wired state. Field injection assigns dependencies via reflection after construction, so fields cannot be final and an object can momentarily exist without its collaborators.

Testability is the other major difference. With constructor injection you simply call new with mock dependencies in a unit test. With field injection there is no constructor to pass mocks to, so you must rely on reflection or spin up a Spring context, which makes tests slower and more brittle.

Senior Engineer Answer

Constructor injection should be preferred. Dependencies become mandatory and explicit: the compiler and the container both guarantee that every required collaborator is supplied before the bean is usable.

A practical signal of poor design is a constructor with too many parameters. With field injection that smell is hidden, but with constructor injection it becomes visible and pushes you to split the class, which is exactly why senior engineers favour it.

Q8. Why Is Constructor Injection Preferred?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

  • Immutability
  • Testability
  • No Null Dependencies
  • Clear Design
  • Better Maintainability

Because dependencies are passed in once at construction, the fields can be final, giving an immutable, fully-formed object that is inherently safer to share across threads. There is no window in which the bean exists with null collaborators, which eliminates a class of NullPointerExceptions that field injection can allow during partial initialization.

It also makes the design self-documenting and maintainable: the constructor signature lists every dependency the class has, so reviewers can immediately see its responsibilities. Modern Spring even injects a single constructor automatically without requiring @Autowired, so there is no extra boilerplate cost to doing the right thing.

SECTION 4 - BEANS

Q9. What Is A Spring Bean?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

A Spring bean is simply an object that is instantiated, assembled and managed by the Spring IoC container. The container controls its creation, dependency injection and lifecycle, which is what distinguishes a bean from an ordinary object you create with new.

Beans are typically declared through stereotype annotations or @Bean methods, and they are registered in the application context under a name. When another bean needs one, the container resolves and injects the appropriate instance, which is the foundation of how Spring applications are wired together.

Examples


@Service

@Repository

@Component

@Controller
Q10. Bean Lifecycle

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Lifecycle


Instantiate

↓

Populate Dependencies

↓

@PostConstruct

↓

Ready For Use

↓

@PreDestroy

The container first instantiates the bean, then populates its dependencies (constructor or field/setter injection). After the dependencies are wired, any @PostConstruct method runs, which is the safe place to perform initialization that relies on those dependencies being present.

The bean then remains ready for use for as long as the container is alive. When the context is shutting down, the container invokes any @PreDestroy method so the bean can release resources. Understanding this ordering matters in practice: putting initialization logic in the constructor is risky because dependencies are not yet injected, whereas @PostConstruct runs only after wiring is complete.

Q11. What Is @PostConstruct?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

@PostConstruct marks a method that Spring executes after dependency injection completes but before the bean is put into service. It is the canonical place for one-time startup logic that needs the bean's dependencies to already be wired.

Use Cases

  • Cache Initialization
  • Configuration Validation
  • Startup Logic

Typical uses include warming a cache, validating that injected configuration values are present and sensible, or opening a connection. Because it runs after injection, it can safely call the methods of injected collaborators, which a constructor cannot always do reliably.

A common interview point is why you should not do this work in the constructor: at construction time field-injected dependencies may still be null, and putting heavy logic in a constructor makes the object harder to test. @PostConstruct cleanly separates "build the object" from "initialize the object."

Q12. What Is @PreDestroy?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

@PreDestroy marks a method that Spring calls just before a bean is destroyed, which happens during graceful shutdown of the application context. It gives the bean a final chance to clean up before it is discarded.

Use Cases

  • Close Resources
  • Flush Buffers
  • Cleanup Logic

Common uses are closing connection pools or files, flushing buffered data, and shutting down background threads or schedulers so the JVM can exit cleanly. This is important for production reliability, because skipping cleanup can leak connections or lose buffered writes during deployment restarts.

Note that @PreDestroy is only reliably invoked for singleton beans during an orderly context shutdown; it is not called for prototype beans, since Spring does not manage their full lifecycle after creation.

SECTION 5 - BEAN SCOPES

Q13. Bean Scopes In Spring

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

  • Singleton
  • Prototype
  • Request
  • Session
  • Application

A scope defines how many instances of a bean the container creates and how long each lives. Singleton (the default) means exactly one shared instance per container; prototype means a brand new instance every time the bean is requested.

Request, session and application are web-aware scopes: a request-scoped bean lives for one HTTP request, a session-scoped bean lives for one user session, and an application-scoped bean is shared across the whole web application. Choosing the right scope is mainly about state management — stateless logic belongs in singletons, while per-user or per-request state needs the narrower web scopes.

Q14. What Is Singleton Scope?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Singleton scope means one bean instance per Spring container, and it is the default scope. Every component that depends on that bean receives the same shared instance, which is created eagerly when the context starts.

Example


@Service

public class UserService {}

Note that Spring's singleton is "one per container," which is different from the classic Gang of Four singleton pattern of "one per JVM/classloader." Singletons are efficient because the object is built once and reused, but precisely because they are shared, they must be designed carefully when it comes to mutable state.

Q15. Singleton Bean Thread Safety

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Important Answer

Singleton does NOT mean thread safe. The "singleton" guarantee is only about instance count (one shared instance), not about concurrent access protection.

Multiple requests may access the same bean simultaneously, because in a web application many threads share that one instance. Therefore you must avoid mutable shared state in a singleton bean: instance fields that change per request will be corrupted by concurrent access and produce data leaking between users.

The standard rule is to keep singleton beans stateless. Pass request-specific data as method parameters or local variables (which live on each thread's stack), and store any genuinely shared mutable state in thread-safe structures or in the database rather than in bean fields.

Interview Gold Point

This question is asked extremely often, because it separates engineers who merely use Spring from those who understand its concurrency model. A strong answer states plainly that singleton refers to instance count, not safety, and then explains the practical defence: keep services stateless and never store per-request data in instance fields.

Q16. What Is Prototype Scope?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Prototype scope means a new bean instance is created every time the bean is requested from the container. Unlike a singleton, no instance is shared, so each injection point or getBean call receives its own fresh object.

A key subtlety is that Spring only manages a prototype bean up to creation and injection; it does not call its destruction callbacks, so cleanup of prototype beans is the caller's responsibility. Prototype scope is useful for beans that hold per-use mutable state, but injecting a prototype into a singleton is a common trap, since the singleton captures one prototype instance at startup unless you use a provider or method injection.

PART 1 COMPLETE

Next Part: * @Component vs @Service vs @Repository * ApplicationContext * BeanFactory * Circular Dependencies * AOP * Proxy Pattern * Spring Internals * Transaction Management Foundations These are some of the most frequently asked Spring Core topics in senior interviews.

SECTION 6 - STEREOTYPE ANNOTATIONS

Q17. What Is @Component?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

@Component marks a class as a generic Spring-managed bean so that component scanning detects it and registers it in the container. It is the base stereotype from which the more specialized annotations are derived.

Example


@Component
public class EmailUtil {

}

Use @Component when a class does not fit one of the specialized stereotypes such as a service, repository or controller. Utility helpers, mappers and general infrastructure beans are typical candidates.

Functionally @Service, @Repository and @Controller are all themselves annotated with @Component, so they are detected the same way. The difference is purely the semantic intent they communicate and, in some cases, extra framework behaviour layered on top.

Q18. What Is @Service?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

@Service marks a class as part of the business logic layer. Technically it behaves like @Component, but it expresses the intent that this bean holds the application's core service logic.

Example


@Service
public class UserService {

}

Purpose

Its main value is improving readability and architectural clarity: anyone reading the codebase can immediately tell which classes orchestrate business rules. This keeps the layered architecture (controller → service → repository) self-evident.

By convention, transactional boundaries and business orchestration live in the service layer, which is why @Transactional is most commonly placed on @Service methods rather than on controllers or repositories.

Q19. What Is @Repository?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

@Repository marks a class as part of the persistence layer, the layer responsible for data access. Like the other stereotypes it is a specialization of @Component, so it is picked up by component scanning.

Example


@Repository
public class UserRepository {

}

Special Feature

@Repository automatically translates database exceptions into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy. This means low-level, vendor-specific exceptions like SQLException are converted into a consistent, unchecked set of Spring exceptions.

This translation is valuable because it decouples your code from the underlying persistence technology and removes the need to handle checked SQL exceptions everywhere. It is enabled by a BeanPostProcessor that wraps repository beans, which is a nice example of stereotype annotations carrying real behaviour, not just intent.

Q20. What Is @Controller?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

@Controller marks a class as a web layer component that handles incoming HTTP requests. In a traditional MVC application it returns the logical name of a view (such as a JSP or Thymeleaf template) which is then rendered into HTML.

Example


@Controller

Methods inside a @Controller are mapped to URLs with annotations like @GetMapping and @PostMapping. The return value is interpreted by a view resolver, which is the key distinction from @RestController, where the return value is serialized directly into the response body.

Q21. What Is @RestController?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

@RestController is a convenience annotation that combines: 


@Controller

+

@ResponseBody

It returns JSON/XML directly. Because @ResponseBody is applied to every method, the return value is serialized straight into the HTTP response body (typically as JSON via Jackson) instead of being resolved to a view.

This is the standard annotation for building REST APIs and microservice endpoints, which is why it appears in almost every Spring Boot service. You use @Controller for server-rendered pages and @RestController for data APIs that the frontend or other services consume.

Q22. @Component vs @Service vs @Repository

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Annotation Purpose
@Component Generic Bean
@Service Business Layer
@Repository Persistence Layer

All three register a bean through component scanning, and @Service and @Repository are themselves meta-annotated with @Component. So at the level of bean detection and injection, they are interchangeable.

The differences are semantic and behavioural. @Component is the generic catch-all; @Service signals business logic; and @Repository signals data access and additionally enables exception translation into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy. Choosing the correct stereotype documents your architecture and, in the repository case, activates real framework behaviour.

Interview Gold Point

Technically all are Spring beans. The difference is semantic intent and additional behavior. The strongest answer notes that the only one with extra runtime behaviour is @Repository (persistence exception translation), while @Service and @Component differ only in the intent they communicate to the reader.

SECTION 7 - APPLICATIONCONTEXT

Q23. What Is ApplicationContext?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

ApplicationContext is the advanced Spring IoC container and the central interface for accessing beans in a Spring application. It builds on the basic BeanFactory and adds enterprise-grade features on top of plain dependency injection.

It is responsible for:

  • Bean Management
  • Dependency Injection
  • Events
  • AOP Integration
  • Internationalization

Beyond creating and wiring beans, the ApplicationContext publishes and listens to application events, integrates AOP proxy creation, supports message sources for internationalization, and resolves resources and property sources. This is why it is the container used in virtually every real Spring application, with Spring Boot creating one for you automatically at startup.

Q24. BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

BeanFactory ApplicationContext
Basic Container Advanced Container
Lazy Loading Eager Singleton Loading
Minimal Features Enterprise Features

BeanFactory is the most basic container, providing only core dependency injection, and it instantiates beans lazily, meaning a bean is created the first time it is requested. ApplicationContext extends BeanFactory and adds the enterprise features described earlier, while eagerly creating singleton beans at startup.
