Reserve @SpringBootTest for when you genuinely need the wired application context — it is the heaviest annotation and should be the exception, not the default.

Interview Gold Point

This is the most common real-world mistake: people sprinkle @SpringBootTest on every test "to be safe", turning a 2-second suite into a 5-minute one. A senior engineer mocks dependencies and keeps service tests pure.

Q11. When Do You Use @WebMvcTest vs @DataJpaTest vs @SpringBootTest?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Spring Boot provides "test slices" that load only part of the context, keeping tests fast and focused:

Annotation Loads Use For
@WebMvcTest Web layer only (controllers, filters) Controller tests with MockMvc; services mocked
@DataJpaTest JPA layer + in-memory DB Repository / query tests
@SpringBootTest Full application context Integration / end-to-end tests 
@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
class UserControllerTest {

    @Autowired MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean UserService userService;  // service is mocked

    @Test
    void returns404_whenUserMissing() throws Exception {
        when(userService.find(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.empty());

        mockMvc.perform(get("/users/1"))
               .andExpect(status().isNotFound());
    }
}

Interview Favorite

Note @MockBean (Spring) vs @Mock (plain Mockito). @MockBean replaces a bean in the Spring context with a mock, which is exactly what you need inside a slice test like @WebMvcTest.

SECTION 5 - PRACTICES & PHILOSOPHY

Q12. Is 100% Code Coverage the Goal?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

No. Coverage measures which lines executed during tests, not whether the behaviour was actually asserted. You can reach 100% coverage with tests that assert nothing. Chasing a number leads to low-value tests on trivial getters while real edge cases go untested.

The healthy framing: coverage is a tool to find untested code, not a target to hit. Aim for meaningful tests on business logic and branches; a pragmatic team target is often 70–85%, with the gaps being deliberate (generated code, simple DTOs).

Interview Gold Point

Mention branch coverage vs line coverage. A single if can be 100% line-covered while only one branch is exercised. Branch coverage is the more honest metric for logic-heavy code.

Q13. What Is TDD and Have You Used It?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Test-Driven Development follows the Red-Green-Refactor cycle:

  1. Red — write a failing test for the next small behaviour
  2. Green — write the minimum code to make it pass
  3. Refactor — clean up the code with the test as a safety net

The benefit is that it forces you to design testable, decoupled code and to think about the API from the caller's perspective before implementing it.

Honest Senior Answer

A credible answer admits TDD is not dogma: it shines for well-understood logic (algorithms, parsing, business rules) and is awkward for exploratory or UI work. Say you apply it where it adds value and write tests immediately after elsewhere — the non-negotiable is that the code ships with tests.

Q14. What Are Flaky Tests and How Do You Avoid Them?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

A flaky test passes sometimes and fails other times without any code change. They erode trust in the whole suite — once developers start re-running "just to be sure", the suite stops protecting you.

Common Causes & Fixes

  • Time / dates — inject a Clock instead of calling now()
  • Random data — seed the random generator
  • Shared state / order dependence — reset state in @BeforeEach
  • Real network / sleep-based timing — mock the dependency or use proper awaitility instead of Thread.sleep
  • Concurrency — avoid relying on thread scheduling for assertions

Interview Gold Point

Say you treat a flaky test as a bug, not a nuisance — quarantine it so it stops blocking the pipeline, but track and fix it. Silently re-running until green is how teams end up ignoring real failures.

Q15. How Do You Decide What to Test?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Prioritise by risk and value:

  • Business logic and branches — the core rules and every decision path
  • Boundaries and edge cases — empty, null, min, max, off-by-one
  • Bug fixes — write a failing test that reproduces the bug first, so it never regresses
  • Public behaviour, not private methods — test through the public API; private methods get covered indirectly

What you generally don't unit-test: trivial getters/setters, framework code, and generated code — testing those adds maintenance cost with no real safety.

Interview Gold Point

"Test behaviour, not implementation." If your test names describe what the code should do (not how), you can refactor freely and the tests still guard the contract. That single principle is what keeps a test suite an asset rather than a liability.

SECTION 6 - INTEGRATION & ADVANCED TESTING

Q16. How Do You Write an Integration Test in Spring Boot?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

An integration test loads a real (or partial) Spring context and verifies that components work together — controller, service, repository, and often a database. Use @SpringBootTest to boot the full context, and drive the app through HTTP with MockMvc (mock servlet) or TestRestTemplate/WebTestClient (real server when webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT). 

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
class OrderApiIntegrationTest {

    @Autowired TestRestTemplate rest;

    @Test
    void createsAndReturnsOrder() {
        ResponseEntity<Order> resp =
            rest.postForEntity("/orders", new Order(100), Order.class);
        assertEquals(HttpStatus.CREATED, resp.getStatusCode());
        assertNotNull(resp.getBody().getId());
    }
}

Interview Gold Point

Keep integration tests few and focused — they are slow because they boot the context. The test pyramid still applies: many fast unit tests at the base, a thin layer of integration tests verifying wiring and the critical happy paths.

Q17. What Is Testcontainers and Why Use It?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Testcontainers is a library that spins up real dependencies in throwaway Docker containers for your tests — a real Postgres, Kafka, or Redis — then tears them down afterwards. It replaces in-memory fakes (like H2) that behave subtly differently from production. 

@Testcontainers
@SpringBootTest
class RepositoryIT {

    @Container
    static PostgreSQLContainer<?> db =
        new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16");

    @DynamicPropertySource
    static void props(DynamicPropertyRegistry reg) {
        reg.add("spring.datasource.url", db::getJdbcUrl);
        reg.add("spring.datasource.username", db::getUsername);
        reg.add("spring.datasource.password", db::getPassword);
    }
    // tests now run against a REAL Postgres
}

Interview Favorite

The key argument: H2 tests pass but production breaks because H2 doesn't support Postgres-specific SQL, types, or constraints. Testcontainers gives "production parity" in tests at the cost of needing Docker available in CI. This is a strong senior-level testing practice to mention.

Q18. What Is Contract Testing?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Short Answer

Contract testing verifies that two services agree on the shape of their API without running both together in a slow end-to-end test. The consumer defines its expectations as a contract; the provider's build runs tests generated from that contract, so a breaking change fails the provider's CI immediately.

Common tools: Spring Cloud Contract and Pact. This is "consumer-driven contracts" — the consumer's needs drive what the provider must honour.

Interview Gold Point

Contract tests solve the microservices problem of "I changed my API and silently broke three downstream services". They are far faster and more reliable than spinning up the whole system — tie this back to the Microservices chapter.

Q19. What Is Mutation Testing?

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

Mutation testing measures the quality of your tests, not just coverage. A tool (e.g. PIT / Pitest) introduces small bugs ("mutants") — flipping > to >=, removing a method call — then runs your tests. If your tests still pass, they failed to "kill" the mutant, revealing a weak assertion. The mutation score is the percentage of mutants killed.

Interview Favorite

This directly answers the "is 100% coverage meaningful?" trap: code can be fully covered yet have tests that assert nothing. Mutation testing catches exactly those hollow tests. Mention it when asked how you ensure tests are actually valuable.

Q20. How Do You Test Asynchronous or Time-Dependent Code?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Short Answer

Never use Thread.sleep to "wait for" async work — it makes tests slow and flaky. Instead poll for the expected condition with a timeout using a library like Awaitility, and inject a Clock for time-based logic so you can control "now" deterministically. 

// Awaitility: poll until condition or timeout
await().atMost(5, SECONDS)
       .untilAsserted(() ->
           assertEquals(1, repository.count()));

Interview Gold Point

Injecting java.time.Clock instead of calling Instant.now() directly is the clean way to test schedulers, expiry, and timeouts — the test supplies a fixed clock. This ties to the flaky-test discussion: time and threading are the two biggest sources of flakiness.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

  • FIRST: Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely
  • AAA: Arrange, Act, Assert
  • Pyramid: many unit, some integration, few E2E
  • JUnit 5: @BeforeEach / @BeforeAll / assertThrows / @ExtendWith
  • Doubles: Stub (data) vs Mock (verify) vs Spy (real+stub) vs Fake
  • Mockito: when().thenReturn(), verify(), ArgumentCaptor, @Mock + @InjectMocks
  • Spring slices: @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, @SpringBootTest, @MockBean
  • Coverage is a tool, not a target — prefer branch coverage
  • Test behaviour, not implementation
  • Treat flaky tests as bugs
  • Integration: @SpringBootTest + MockMvc / TestRestTemplate (keep few)
  • Testcontainers: real DB/Kafka in Docker — production parity over H2
  • Contract testing (Spring Cloud Contract / Pact) catches API breakage between services
  • Mutation testing (PIT) measures test quality, not just coverage
  • Async tests: Awaitility (not Thread.sleep); inject Clock for time

18 - DESIGN PATTERNS INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

Design patterns are proven, reusable solutions to recurring design problems. For a Senior Software Engineer they matter for two reasons: interviewers test them directly, and — more importantly — the entire Spring framework is built on them, so understanding patterns explains why Spring works the way it does. Patterns fall into three families: * Creational — how objects are created (Singleton, Factory, Builder, Prototype) * Structural — how objects are composed (Adapter, Decorator, Proxy, Facade) * Behavioural — how objects interact (Strategy, Observer, Template Method, Command) A strong candidate does not just recite definitions — they say where the pattern appears in the JDK or Spring, and when NOT to use it. Patterns are tools, and over-engineering with patterns you do not need is a real anti-pattern.

TOP ROI DESIGN PATTERN QUESTIONS

  1. What Are Design Patterns and Why Use Them?
  2. Singleton Pattern (Thread-Safe)
  3. Factory vs Abstract Factory
  4. Builder Pattern
  5. Prototype Pattern
  6. Adapter Pattern
  7. Decorator Pattern
  8. Proxy Pattern
  9. Facade Pattern
  10. Strategy Pattern
  11. Observer Pattern
  12. Template Method Pattern
  13. Where Does Spring Use Design Patterns?
  14. SOLID Principles
  15. When Should You NOT Use a Pattern?

SECTION 1 - CREATIONAL PATTERNS

Q1. What Are Design Patterns and Why Use Them?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

A design pattern is a named, reusable solution to a problem that recurs across many designs. They are not code you copy — they are templates for how to structure classes and objects.

Their value is communication and proven structure: saying "use a Strategy here" instantly conveys intent to any engineer, and the pattern comes with known trade-offs so you avoid reinventing (and re-breaking) the wheel.

Interview Gold Point

The mature answer adds a caveat: patterns add indirection and abstraction, which is cost. Apply them when the flexibility is actually needed, not pre-emptively. "Make it work, make it right, make it fast" — patterns belong to the "make it right" step, not premature design.

Q2. Explain the Singleton Pattern and How to Make It Thread-Safe

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

A Singleton ensures a class has exactly one instance with a global access point — used for shared, stateless resources like configuration or connection pools. The challenge is making creation thread-safe without paying a lock cost on every access.

Double-Checked Locking

public class Config {
    private static volatile Config instance;
    private Config() {}

    public static Config getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {                 // 1st check (no lock)
            synchronized (Config.class) {
                if (instance == null)           // 2nd check (locked)
                    instance = new Config();
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}

The volatile keyword is essential — without it, another thread could see a partially constructed object due to instruction reordering.

Interview Gold Point

The cleanest thread-safe Singleton in Java is an enum: enum Config { INSTANCE; }. The JVM guarantees a single instance and handles serialization and reflection attacks for free. Also note: a Spring bean is a singleton per container by default — a different, framework-managed concept.

Q3. Factory vs Abstract Factory

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

The Factory Method hides object creation behind a method, so callers ask for a product by type without using new directly. The Abstract Factory goes one level up: it creates families of related objects (e.g. a UI toolkit that produces matching buttons, checkboxes and menus for a given theme). 

// Simple factory method
interface Notification { void send(String msg); }

class NotificationFactory {
    static Notification create(String type) {
        return switch (type) {
            case "EMAIL" -> new EmailNotification();
            case "SMS"   -> new SmsNotification();
            default -> throw new IllegalArgumentException(type);
        };
    }
}

Interview Favorite

The benefit is decoupling: callers depend on the Notification interface, not concrete classes, so adding a new channel does not touch existing call sites. Spring's BeanFactory is a large-scale factory.

Q4. Explain the Builder Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

The Builder constructs complex objects step by step, avoiding the "telescoping constructor" problem (constructors with many parameters, half of them optional). It produces readable, immutable objects. 

User user = User.builder()
    .name("Alice")
    .email("alice@example.com")
    .age(30)
    .build();

Interview Gold Point

Mention real-world Builders you have used: Lombok's @Builder, StringBuilder, Java's Stream.Builder, and the fluent builders all over Spring (e.g. HttpSecurity, WebClient.builder()). It shines when an object has many optional fields and you want it immutable once built.

Q5. What Is the Prototype Pattern?

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

The Prototype pattern creates new objects by cloning an existing instance rather than constructing from scratch — useful when object creation is expensive, or when you want a configured template copied many times.

In Java this maps to Cloneable/clone(), though copy constructors or copy factory methods are usually clearer and avoid clone()'s well-known pitfalls (shallow copy, broken contracts).

Interview Favorite

Spring's prototype scope is named after this pattern: each request for the bean yields a fresh instance, unlike the default singleton scope.

SECTION 2 - STRUCTURAL PATTERNS

Q6. Explain the Adapter Pattern

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

An Adapter converts the interface of one class into another that a client expects — the software equivalent of a travel power-plug adapter. Use it to make incompatible or third-party code work with your existing interfaces without modifying either side.

Interview Gold Point

JDK examples: Arrays.asList() adapts an array to a List, and the old InputStreamReader adapts a byte stream to a character stream. The key signal is "I have class A but my code needs interface B".

Q7. Explain the Decorator Pattern

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

A Decorator adds behaviour to an object at runtime by wrapping it in another object that implements the same interface. It is the composition-based alternative to subclass explosion: instead of BufferedGzipEncryptedStream, you wrap layers. 

InputStream in = new BufferedInputStream(
                     new GZIPInputStream(
                         new FileInputStream("data.gz")));

Interview Favorite

The entire java.io stream library is the textbook Decorator example. Each wrapper adds one capability (buffering, compression) while keeping the same InputStream interface, so they compose freely.

Q8. Explain the Proxy Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

A Proxy is a stand-in that controls access to a real object — adding lazy loading, access control, caching, or cross-cutting logic without the caller knowing. The proxy implements the same interface as the real subject.

Interview Gold Point

This is the most important pattern for Spring developers: Spring AOP, @Transactional, @Cacheable, and lazy-loaded Hibernate entities all use proxies (JDK dynamic proxies for interfaces, CGLIB for classes). This is also why calling an @Transactional method from within the same class bypasses the proxy — the famous "self-invocation" problem.

Q9. Explain the Facade Pattern

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

A Facade provides a single, simplified interface to a complex subsystem. Clients talk to the facade instead of wiring together many low-level classes themselves, which reduces coupling.

Interview Favorite

A typical Spring @Service often acts as a facade: one placeOrder() method hides the coordination of repositories, payment gateways and notification senders behind a clean API.

SECTION 3 - BEHAVIOURAL PATTERNS

Q10. Explain the Strategy Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

The Strategy pattern defines a family of interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface and lets you swap them at runtime. It replaces sprawling if/else or switch blocks with polymorphism. 

interface PaymentStrategy { void pay(double amount); }

class PaymentService {
    void checkout(PaymentStrategy strategy, double amount) {
        strategy.pay(amount);   // behaviour chosen by caller
    }
}
// new PaymentService().checkout(new PaypalStrategy(), 99.0);

Interview Gold Point

In Spring you rarely hand-code this — inject a List<PaymentStrategy> or a Map<String, PaymentStrategy> and Spring wires every implementation automatically. That combination of Strategy + dependency injection is a very strong senior answer.

Q11. Explain the Observer Pattern

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

The Observer pattern lets one subject notify many dependent observers automatically when its state changes — a publish/subscribe relationship. Observers can be added or removed without changing the subject.

Interview Favorite

Spring's ApplicationEvent / ApplicationListener (and @EventListener) is Observer in action. At an architectural scale, Kafka pub/sub is the same idea distributed across services — tie this back to the event-driven content in your Microservices chapter.

Q12. Explain the Template Method Pattern

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

The Template Method defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, deferring specific steps to subclasses. The overall sequence is fixed; only the variable steps are overridden.

Interview Gold Point

Spring's many *Template classes — JdbcTemplate, RestTemplate, TransactionTemplate — embody this: the boilerplate (open connection, handle errors, close) is fixed, and you supply only the variable bit (the SQL, the row mapper). That is also why they are called "templates".

SECTION 4 - PATTERNS IN PRACTICE

Q13. Where Does Spring Use Design Patterns?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

This is a favourite "do you really understand the framework" question. Strong answer:

Pattern Where in Spring
Singleton Default bean scope
Factory BeanFactory / ApplicationContext
Proxy AOP, @Transactional, @Cacheable, lazy entities
Template Method JdbcTemplate, RestTemplate
Strategy Injected interface implementations
Observer ApplicationEvent / @EventListener
Builder HttpSecurity, WebClient.builder()

Interview Gold Point

Tie it together: "Spring's whole programming model is patterns — dependency injection is the Inversion of Control principle, beans are managed singletons, and AOP is built on proxies." This shows you understand the framework, not just its annotations.

Q14. What Are the SOLID Principles?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

SOLID is five object-oriented design principles that lead to maintainable, loosely-coupled code — the foundation that makes patterns work:

  • S — Single Responsibility: a class should have one reason to change
  • O — Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification
  • L — Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be usable through their base type
  • I — Interface Segregation: many small interfaces beat one fat one
  • D — Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions

Interview Gold Point

Connect D directly to Spring: dependency injection is the Dependency Inversion Principle in practice — your classes depend on interfaces, and the container supplies the concrete implementation. Giving a concrete example for each letter beats reciting the acronym.

Q15. When Should You NOT Use a Design Pattern?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

When the flexibility it buys is not needed. Patterns add layers of abstraction and indirection, which makes simple code harder to read and debug. Introducing a Strategy for a value that will never change, or a Factory for a single concrete type, is over-engineering.

The senior mindset: start with the simplest code that works, and refactor toward a pattern only when a real, repeated need for flexibility appears — not in anticipation of one that may never come (YAGNI: "You Aren't Gonna Need It").

Interview Gold Point

Demonstrating restraint impresses senior interviewers more than naming twenty patterns. The strongest candidates know patterns deeply and know when plain, direct code is the better engineering choice.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

  • Three families: Creational, Structural, Behavioural
  • Singleton → one instance; prefer enum or double-checked + volatile
  • Factory hides creation; Abstract Factory creates families
  • Builder → complex/immutable objects with many optional fields
  • Adapter converts interfaces; Decorator adds behaviour by wrapping
  • Proxy controls access — the heart of Spring AOP / @Transactional
  • Strategy → swap algorithms (great with DI); Observer → pub/sub events
  • Template Method → fixed skeleton, variable steps (JdbcTemplate)
  • SOLID: SRP, Open/Closed, Liskov, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
  • Dependency Injection = Dependency Inversion in practice
  • Don't over-engineer — patterns are tools, not goals (YAGNI)

21 - REST API DESIGN & DOCUMENTATION INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

API design is one of the most under-rated topics in senior backend interviews. Almost every candidate has "built REST APIs", but very few can defend their design choices under pressure. Interviewers use this topic to separate engineers who copy controller boilerplate from those who understand the HTTP contract they are exposing to other teams. Expect questions that probe whether you can: * Explain what actually makes an API RESTful (not just "it returns JSON") * Pick the correct HTTP method and status code without hesitation * Reason about idempotency, safety and retries * Design clean, versioned, evolvable resource URIs * Handle pagination, filtering and consistent error contracts * Defend cross-cutting concerns: caching, rate limiting, idempotency keys * Auto-generate documentation and know when REST is the wrong tool A good answer ties design decisions to consumer impact: backward compatibility, network cost, retry safety and developer experience. A weak answer is just a list of annotations. Once you state a rule, interviewers usually continue with "and how would you handle it when...", pushing you toward versioning, partial failure, concurrency and real production trade-offs.

TOP ROI API DESIGN QUESTIONS

  1. What is REST and what are its core constraints?
  2. Richardson Maturity Model & what makes an API "RESTful"
  3. HTTP method semantics: GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE
  4. Safe vs Idempotent methods
  5. HTTP status codes and when to use which
  6. 201 vs 200, 400 vs 422, 401 vs 403, 404 vs 409
  7. Resource & URI naming conventions
  8. API versioning strategies and trade-offs
  9. Pagination: offset vs cursor/keyset
  10. Filtering, sorting and query parameter design
  11. Consistent error responses (RFC 7807 problem+json)
  12. Request validation
  13. Idempotency keys for safe POST retries
  14. Rate limiting and the 429 response
  15. HATEOAS, caching (ETag) and content negotiation
  16. OpenAPI/Swagger and REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC

SECTION 1 - REST FUNDAMENTALS

Q1. What is REST and what are its core architectural constraints?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style, defined by Roy Fielding, for building distributed systems on top of HTTP. It is not a protocol and not a framework. A system is RESTful when it obeys a set of constraints; in return you get scalability, evolvability and visibility.

The six constraints are:

  • Client-Server - separation of concerns; UI and data storage evolve independently.
  • Stateless - each request carries everything the server needs; the server stores no client session between requests.
  • Cacheable - responses must declare whether they can be cached (via headers like Cache-Control).
  • Uniform Interface - the cornerstone: resources identified by URIs, manipulated through representations, self-descriptive messages, and HATEOAS.
  • Layered System - a client cannot tell whether it talks to the origin server or an intermediary (gateway, proxy, load balancer).
  • Code on Demand (optional) - the server may ship executable code (e.g. JavaScript) to extend the client.

Statelessness is the one most worth stressing: because no server holds session state, any node can serve any request, which is exactly what lets you scale horizontally behind a load balancer. State that must persist (the user's auth) travels in a token (see the JWT chapter), not a server-side session.

Interview Gold Point

Many candidates confuse "stateless" with "no database". Application state (resource data) absolutely lives on the server and in the DB; what must not live there is client session state. Saying this distinction out loud signals seniority.

Q2. What is the Richardson Maturity Model, and what actually makes an API "RESTful"?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

The Richardson Maturity Model grades how closely an HTTP API follows REST, in four levels:

  • Level 0 - The Swamp of POX: one endpoint, one verb (usually POST), RPC-over-HTTP (e.g. SOAP-style).
  • Level 1 - Resources: distinct URIs per resource, but still one verb everywhere.
  • Level 2 - HTTP Verbs: proper use of GET/POST/PUT/DELETE and meaningful status codes. This is where most "REST" APIs in industry sit, and it is genuinely good enough.
  • Level 3 - Hypermedia Controls (HATEOAS): responses include links telling the client what it can do next.

Pragmatically, an API is considered RESTful when it reaches Level 2: noun-based resource URIs, correct verbs, correct status codes, statelessness and cacheability. Level 3 is the academic ideal but rare in practice because client tooling rarely consumes the links.

Interview Favorite

A strong line: "Most production APIs, including those at large companies, are Level 2. HATEOAS is theoretically pure but adds payload weight and complexity that few clients use - so I treat it as optional rather than a failure." This shows judgement, not dogma.

SECTION 2 - HTTP METHODS & STATUS CODES

Q3. Explain the semantics of GET, POST, PUT, PATCH and DELETE.

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

MethodPurposeBody?Typical success code
GETRead a resource or collection; no side effectsNo200
POSTCreate a subordinate resource / trigger a processYes201 (or 200/202)
PUTFull replace of a resource at a known URI (or create-at-id)Yes (full)200 / 204 / 201
PATCHPartial update of a resourceYes (partial/diff)200 / 204
DELETERemove a resourceNo204 (or 200)

Key distinctions: PUT replaces the entire resource - omitted fields are typically cleared - whereas PATCH modifies only the supplied fields. PUT is also used to create a resource when the client controls the identifier (e.g. PUT /users/42); POST is used when the server assigns the id (e.g. POST /users returning Location: /users/42). 

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/v1/orders")
public class OrderController {

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<OrderDto> create(@Valid @RequestBody CreateOrderRequest req) {
        OrderDto created = orderService.create(req);
        URI location = URI.create("/api/v1/orders/" + created.id());
        return ResponseEntity.created(location).body(created); // 201 + Location header
    }

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<OrderDto> get(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok(orderService.findById(id)); // 200
    }

    @DeleteMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<Void> delete(@PathVariable Long id) {
        orderService.delete(id);
        return ResponseEntity.noContent().build(); // 204
    }
}

Common Mistake

Using POST for everything (a Level 0 anti-pattern) or using GET with side effects. A GET must never mutate state - crawlers, prefetchers and proxies will replay GETs freely, and that has caused real production data loss.

Q4. What is the difference between safe and idempotent methods?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

  • Safe = read-only; calling it does not change server state. (GET, HEAD, OPTIONS)
  • Idempotent = calling it N times has the same effect on server state as calling it once. The response may differ, but the resulting state must not.
MethodSafeIdempotent
GET / HEADYesYes
OPTIONSYesYes
PUTNoYes
DELETENoYes
POSTNoNo
PATCHNoNot guaranteed

Why it matters: idempotency is what makes safe retries possible. If a client times out on a PUT or DELETE, it can resend with no harm. POST is not idempotent - two POSTs create two resources - which is exactly why idempotency keys exist (see Q13). PATCH is idempotent only if its semantics are a full-value set (e.g. JSON Merge Patch with absolute values); a relative operation like {"op":"increment","by":1} is not.

Interview Gold Point

"DELETE is idempotent even though the second call returns 404." Deleting twice still ends in the same state (resource gone). The differing status code does not break idempotency - idempotency is about state, not the response code. Saying this catches a subtlety most candidates miss.

Q5. Walk through the HTTP status code families and when you use them.

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

FamilyMeaningCommon codes
2xxSuccess200 OK, 201 Created, 202 Accepted, 204 No Content
3xxRedirection / caching301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified
4xxClient error (the caller must fix the request)400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 429
5xxServer error (not the caller's fault)500, 502, 503, 504

The senior signal here is precision: never return 200 with an {"error": ...} body, and never blanket-return 500 for validation failures. The status line is part of your API contract - clients, gateways and monitoring all branch on it. A spike of 5xx pages the on-call engineer; a spike of 4xx points at a misbehaving client. Conflating them poisons observability.

Common Mistake

Returning 200 OK for failures "to keep the frontend simple". This breaks caching, retries, alerting and any standard HTTP client. The status code is data - use it correctly.

Q6. When do you use 201 vs 200, 400 vs 422, 401 vs 403, and 404 vs 409?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

  • 201 vs 200: 201 Created when a request created a new resource (return a Location header). 200 OK for a successful read or an update that returns a body. Use 204 No Content when the update/delete succeeds but there is nothing to return.
  • 400 vs 422: 400 Bad Request = the request is malformed or unparseable (broken JSON, wrong type, missing required field). 422 Unprocessable Entity = the syntax is fine but the request fails business/semantic validation (e.g. "end date before start date", "email already in a pending state"). Many teams use 400 for both; using 422 for semantic rules is the cleaner, more senior choice.
  • 401 vs 403: 401 Unauthorized = you are not authenticated (no/invalid credentials) - "who are you?". 403 Forbidden = you are authenticated but not allowed - "I know who you are, you still can't". (See the Spring Security chapter.)
  • 404 vs 409: 404 Not Found = the resource does not exist. 409 Conflict = the request conflicts with current state - duplicate creation, or an optimistic-lock version mismatch (stale update). 409 is the natural code for "this changed under you, refetch and retry".

Interview Favorite

On security, some teams deliberately return 404 instead of 403 to avoid leaking the existence of a resource to unauthorized users (resource enumeration). Mentioning this trade-off - correctness vs information disclosure - is a strong senior touch.

SECTION 3 - RESOURCE & URI DESIGN

Q7. What are the conventions for naming resources and designing URIs?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

  • Use nouns, not verbs. The verb is the HTTP method. GET /users/42, not GET /getUser?id=42.
  • Use plural collection names. /orders, /orders/42, /orders/42/items.
  • Nest to express ownership, but stop at one or two levels. /users/42/orders is fine; /users/42/orders/9/items/3/reviews is a smell - reference the leaf directly via /reviews/3.
  • Lowercase, hyphen-separated path segments: /purchase-orders, not /purchaseOrders or /purchase_orders.
  • Query params for filtering/sorting/paging, not for identity: /orders?status=PAID&sort=-createdAt.
  • Use sub-resources for relationships and reserve a few "controller" style endpoints for non-CRUD actions when truly needed (e.g. POST /orders/42/cancel).
GET    /api/v1/customers              # list customers
POST   /api/v1/customers              # create a customer
GET    /api/v1/customers/42           # read one
PUT    /api/v1/customers/42           # replace
PATCH  /api/v1/customers/42           # partial update
DELETE /api/v1/customers/42           # delete
GET    /api/v1/customers/42/orders    # that customer's orders

Common Mistake

Action verbs in the path (/createUser, /deleteOrder). The only widely accepted exception is non-CRUD domain actions that do not map to a verb, modelled as a POST to a sub-resource: POST /accounts/42/transfers.

Q8. What are the API versioning strategies and their trade-offs?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Three mainstream approaches:

StrategyExampleProsCons
URI path/api/v1/ordersExplicit, easy to test in a browser/curl, easy routing & cachingNot "pure" REST (URI should identify a resource, not a version); duplicates routes
Custom / Accept headerAccept: application/vnd.acme.v2+jsonClean URIs; version negotiation is content-drivenHarder to test/debug; easy to forget; weaker cache keys
Query parameter/orders?version=2Simple to addPollutes the query string; clumsy caching; easy to omit

URI versioning is the most common in industry because it is unambiguous and cache-friendly. The purist choice is media-type (header) versioning, which keeps the resource URI stable. Whatever you pick, the real goal is backward compatibility: prefer additive, non-breaking changes (new optional fields, new endpoints) so you rarely need to bump the major version at all. Bump the version only on breaking changes, and support N-1 for a deprecation window.

Interview Gold Point

"The best version bump is the one you never make." Adding fields, never removing or repurposing them, and treating unknown fields as ignorable on the client lets a v1 API evolve for years. This shows you understand evolution, not just syntax.

SECTION 4 - PAYLOADS & ERROR HANDLING

Q9. How do you design pagination - offset vs cursor/keyset?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Offset/limit pagination (?page=3&size=20 or ?offset=40&limit=20) is simple and allows jumping to any page. But it has two problems at scale: it is slow on large offsets (OFFSET 1000000 still scans and discards a million rows), and it suffers from drift - if rows are inserted/deleted while paging, items get skipped or repeated.

Cursor / keyset pagination passes an opaque cursor that encodes the last seen sort key (e.g. ?after=eyJpZCI6MTIzfQ&limit=20). The query becomes WHERE (created_at, id) < (:lastTs, :lastId) ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC LIMIT 20, which uses the index directly - O(limit), not O(offset). It is stable under inserts and the right default for large, frequently-changing or infinite-scroll datasets. The cost: you cannot jump to "page 47".

AspectOffsetCursor/Keyset
Random page accessYesNo (next/prev only)
Performance on deep pagesDegradesConstant
Stable under writesNo (drift)Yes
Best forSmall, admin tables, page numbersFeeds, logs, large/live data

Interview Favorite

The "drift" point wins this question. Saying "offset pagination double-counts or skips rows when the underlying data changes between pages, so for a live feed I default to keyset" demonstrates you have actually paginated production data.

Q10. How do you design filtering and sorting query parameters?

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

  • Filtering: field-named query params - GET /orders?status=PAID&customerId=42. For ranges, use suffixes or paired params: ?createdAfter=2026-01-01&amountGte=100.
  • Sorting: a sort param with a sign or direction - ?sort=-createdAt,name (descending createdAt, then ascending name). Spring Data's Pageable accepts ?sort=createdAt,desc natively.
  • Field selection / sparse fieldsets: ?fields=id,name,total to trim payload size.
  • Whitelist every filterable and sortable field server-side. Never feed a raw client field directly into a SQL ORDER BY or dynamic query - that is an injection vector (see the SQL chapter).
@GetMapping("/orders")
public Page<OrderDto> search(
        @RequestParam(required = false) OrderStatus status,
        @PageableDefault(size = 20, sort = "createdAt",
                         direction = Sort.Direction.DESC) Pageable pageable) {
    return orderService.search(status, pageable);
}

Common Mistake

Allowing arbitrary sort/filter fields straight from the client. Always map to an allow-list of known columns; reject unknown fields with 400. Convenience here becomes a SQL-injection or performance footgun in production.

Q11. How do you design a consistent error response format?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Every error from your API should share one machine-readable shape so clients can parse failures uniformly. The standard is RFC 7807 (Problem Details for HTTP APIs), served as Content-Type: application/problem+json: 

{
  "type": "https://api.acme.com/errors/insufficient-funds",
  "title": "Insufficient funds",
  "status": 422,
  "detail": "Account 42 balance 10.00 is below the requested 50.00",
  "instance": "/api/v1/accounts/42/transfers",
  "traceId": "a1b2c3d4"
}

Spring Boot 3 ships ProblemDetail and supports this natively. Centralize error mapping in a single @RestControllerAdvice (see the Spring Boot chapter for @ControllerAdvice) so you never scatter error formatting across controllers: 

@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(InsufficientFundsException.class)
    public ProblemDetail handle(InsufficientFundsException ex) {
        ProblemDetail pd = ProblemDetail.forStatusAndDetail(
                HttpStatus.UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY, ex.getMessage());
        pd.setTitle("Insufficient funds");
        pd.setType(URI.create("https://api.acme.com/errors/insufficient-funds"));
        pd.setProperty("traceId", MDC.get("traceId"));
        return pd;
    }
}

Always include a correlation/trace id so a support ticket maps to a log line (see distributed tracing in the Microservices chapter). Never leak stack traces or SQL to clients.

Interview Gold Point

Mentioning RFC 7807 / application/problem+json by name immediately signals you have designed real public APIs. Bonus: note that error type should be a stable, documented URI clients can branch on - more reliable than parsing the human-readable detail string.

Q12. How do you handle request validation in a REST API?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

Validate at the edge, declaratively, and return a structured 400/422 listing every field error at once (not one at a time). In Spring, use Bean Validation (Jakarta Validation) annotations on the request DTO plus @Valid on the controller parameter: 

public record CreateUserRequest(
        @NotBlank String name,
        @Email String email,
        @Min(18) int age) {}

@PostMapping("/users")
public ResponseEntity<UserDto> create(@Valid @RequestBody CreateUserRequest req) { ... }

A failed @Valid throws MethodArgumentNotValidException, which you map in the advice to a problem+json body whose errors array lists each rejected field and message. Distinguish two layers: syntactic/format validation (annotations, 400) and semantic/business validation (service layer, often 422), e.g. "this coupon is expired" - a rule annotations cannot express.

Common Mistake

Returning only the first validation error. Clients (and forms) want all errors in one response. Aggregate field errors into a list so the user fixes everything in one round trip.

SECTION 5 - ADVANCED API DESIGN

Q13. What are idempotency keys and why are they needed for POST?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

POST is not idempotent, so a client that times out and retries a "create payment" call risks charging the customer twice. The fix is an idempotency key: the client generates a unique key (e.g. a UUID) and sends it in a header on the POST: 

POST /api/v1/payments
Idempotency-Key: 7c9e6679-7425-40de-944b-e07fc1f90ae7

The server stores the key together with the result of the first successful request (typically in Redis or a dedicated table). On a retry with the same key, it skips re-processing and replays the original response. This is exactly how Stripe and other payment APIs make POST safely retryable.

Key implementation points:

  • Persist key -> (status, response body) with a TTL (e.g. 24h).
  • While the first request is in flight, lock the key so concurrent retries get 409 or wait, not a double-process.
  • Optionally hash the request body and reject reuse of the same key with a different body (400) - protects against accidental key reuse.

Interview Gold Point

Tie it back: "GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent by HTTP semantics, so retries are inherently safe; POST is not, so I add an idempotency key for any create that has financial or side-effecting impact." That sentence connects Q4 to a real production pattern and is a strong senior answer.

Q14. How do you implement rate limiting and what does the API return?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

Rate limiting protects the service from abuse and noisy neighbours and enforces quotas. Common algorithms (covered in the System Design chapter) are token bucket, leaking bucket, fixed window and sliding window; token bucket is the usual default because it allows bursts up to capacity.

When a client exceeds its quota, return 429 Too Many Requests, and tell the client how to behave: 

HTTP/1.1 429 Too Many Requests
Retry-After: 30
X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 0
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1718553600
  • Retry-After - seconds (or a date) to wait before retrying; well-behaved clients honour it with backoff.
  • X-RateLimit-Limit / -Remaining / -Reset - let clients self-throttle before hitting the limit.

Enforce it at the gateway/edge where possible (API Gateway, NGINX, a filter), backed by a shared store like Redis so the limit is global across instances - not per-node in-memory, which a horizontally scaled service would multiply.

Interview Favorite

Stress the shared counter: "In-memory rate limiting breaks the moment you scale to N pods, because each enforces the limit independently - the effective limit becomes N times the intended one. I keep the counter in Redis."

Q15. Explain HATEOAS, HTTP caching (ETag) and content negotiation.

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is REST Level 3: responses embed links describing valid next actions, so the client discovers transitions instead of hard-coding URLs. 

{
  "id": 42,
  "status": "PENDING",
  "_links": {
    "self":   { "href": "/orders/42" },
    "cancel": { "href": "/orders/42/cancel" },
    "pay":    { "href": "/orders/42/payment" }
  }
}

Caching with ETag: the server returns an ETag (a hash/version of the representation). The client caches it and on the next read sends If-None-Match. If unchanged, the server responds 304 Not Modified with no body, saving bandwidth. For state-changing requests, ETag + If-Match gives optimistic concurrency: a stale write fails with 412 Precondition Failed (or 409). Cache-Control (e.g. max-age, no-store, private) governs how long and where a response may be cached. 

GET /orders/42
If-None-Match: "v7"          --> 304 Not Modified (empty body) if still version 7

Content negotiation: the client states what it wants via Accept (e.g. application/json vs application/xml) and describes its body via Content-Type; the server selects a representation or returns 406 Not Acceptable. This is also the mechanism behind media-type API versioning (Q8).

Interview Gold Point

The ETag + If-Match concurrency angle is gold: "ETags do double duty - 304 for caching on reads, and 412/409 optimistic locking on writes, so two clients can't silently clobber each other." This links REST to the optimistic-locking discussion in the JPA chapter.

SECTION 6 - DOCUMENTATION & ALTERNATIVES

Q16. How do you document a REST API, and how do REST, GraphQL and gRPC compare?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Documentation: the standard is the OpenAPI Specification (formerly Swagger) - a machine-readable contract for endpoints, schemas, status codes and auth. In Spring Boot, springdoc-openapi generates this automatically from your controllers and DTOs and serves an interactive Swagger UI: 

<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springdoc</groupId>
    <artifactId>springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui</artifactId>
    <version>2.6.0</version>
</dependency>

# then:
#   /v3/api-docs        -> the OpenAPI JSON
#   /swagger-ui.html    -> interactive docs

@Operation(summary = "Create an order", responses = {
    @ApiResponse(responseCode = "201", description = "Order created"),
    @ApiResponse(responseCode = "422", description = "Validation failed")
})
@PostMapping("/orders")
public ResponseEntity<OrderDto> create(@Valid @RequestBody CreateOrderRequest req) { ... }

Generating docs from code keeps them in sync; you can also drive client SDK generation from the OpenAPI file. A "contract-first" variant writes the OpenAPI spec first and generates the server stubs.

REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC:

AspectRESTGraphQLgRPC
Transport / formatHTTP/1.1+ , JSONHTTP, JSONHTTP/2, Protobuf (binary)
Data fetchingFixed per endpoint; over/under-fetchClient picks exact fields; one round tripFixed, defined by .proto contract
ContractOpenAPIGraphQL schemaProtobuf IDL (strongly typed, codegen)
StreamingLimited (SSE)SubscriptionsFirst-class bidirectional streaming
CachingNative HTTP caching (great)Hard (mostly POST)Not HTTP-cacheable
Best forPublic APIs, CRUD, broad client supportRich frontends with varied data needs, aggregationLow-latency internal service-to-service

Rule of thumb: REST for public-facing CRUD APIs where HTTP caching and ubiquity matter; GraphQL when diverse clients need to shape their own queries and avoid over-fetching; gRPC for high-throughput, low-latency internal microservice communication (see the Microservices chapter), where the binary Protobuf payload and HTTP/2 multiplexing win.

Interview Favorite

"They are not mutually exclusive." A mature system commonly exposes REST/GraphQL at the edge for clients and uses gRPC internally between services. Saying you choose per-boundary, not per-religion, signals architectural maturity.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

  • REST constraints (6): Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand (optional). Statelessness enables horizontal scaling.
  • Richardson Maturity: L0 RPC -> L1 resources -> L2 verbs+status (industry norm) -> L3 HATEOAS.
  • Methods: GET read, POST create (server id), PUT full replace / create at known id, PATCH partial, DELETE remove.
  • Safe = no state change (GET/HEAD/OPTIONS). Idempotent = same state after N calls (GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD). POST & PATCH not guaranteed.
  • Status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created (+Location), 204 No Content, 304 Not Modified, 400 malformed, 401 not authenticated, 403 not allowed, 404 missing, 409 conflict/stale version, 422 semantic validation, 429 rate limited, 5xx server.
  • URIs: plural nouns, lowercase-hyphenated, shallow nesting, query params for filter/sort/page, methods carry the verb.
  • Versioning: URI (common, cache-friendly), header/media-type (purist), query (avoid). Best version bump = none; prefer additive changes.
  • Pagination: offset (random access, drifts, slow deep pages) vs cursor/keyset (stable, O(limit), no jump) - keyset for live/large data.
  • Errors: RFC 7807 problem+json, central @RestControllerAdvice, include traceId, never leak stack traces.
  • Validation: Bean Validation + @Valid at the edge (400/422), return all field errors at once.
  • Idempotency key: header (UUID) stored with first result; replays make POST safely retryable (payments).
  • Rate limit: 429 + Retry-After + X-RateLimit-* headers; shared (Redis) counter, enforce at the edge.
  • Caching/concurrency: ETag + If-None-Match -> 304; ETag + If-Match -> 412/409 optimistic locking. Cache-Control governs lifetime.
  • Content negotiation: Accept / Content-Type; 406 if unsupported; basis of media-type versioning.
  • Docs: OpenAPI/Swagger via springdoc-openapi - auto-generated, interactive, drives SDK codegen.
  • REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC: REST for public CRUD + caching; GraphQL for flexible client queries; gRPC for fast internal service-to-service. Often combined per boundary.

22 - MESSAGING & EVENT STREAMING INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

Once a system grows past a single service, the way components talk to each other becomes a design decision in its own right. Senior backend interviews spend a lot of time here because asynchronous messaging is the backbone of decoupled, resilient, scalable systems. Many engineers have "used Kafka" but cannot explain when a queue beats a stream, what delivery guarantees actually mean, or how to stop duplicate processing. For a Senior role you should be able to confidently discuss: * Why asynchronous messaging exists (decoupling, buffering, resilience) * The difference between point-to-point queues, publish/subscribe, and event streaming * Apache Kafka and Apache RabbitMQ, and when each one fits * Managed options like AWS SQS and SNS * Delivery guarantees: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once * Idempotency, deduplication, ordering, dead letter queues and retries * Consumer lag, backpressure, and integrating all of this with Spring This chapter deliberately keeps the deep Kafka internals short, since the Microservices chapter already covers Kafka, the outbox pattern, the saga pattern and event-driven architecture in depth. Here we focus on breadth: the alternatives, the comparison, the delivery semantics and the reliability patterns. Interviewers usually continue with follow-ups on exactly-once semantics, ordering guarantees, and how you would design a reliable consumer that never loses or double-processes a message.

TOP ROI MESSAGING QUESTIONS

  1. Why Use Asynchronous Messaging At All?
  2. Synchronous (REST) vs Asynchronous Communication
  3. Queue (Point-to-Point) vs Pub/Sub vs Event Streaming
  4. Kafka Core Concepts Recap
  5. How Partitioning Enables Scale & Ordering
  6. Log Retention & Replay
  7. When Is Kafka the Right Choice?
  8. The RabbitMQ / AMQP Model
  9. RabbitMQ Exchange Types
  10. When RabbitMQ Fits Better Than Kafka
  11. Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs SQS/SNS
  12. Delivery Guarantees: At-Most / At-Least / Exactly Once
  13. Idempotent Consumers & Deduplication
  14. Message Ordering
  15. Dead Letter Queues, Retries & Poison Messages
  16. Consumer Lag, Backpressure & Spring Integration

SECTION 1 - FUNDAMENTALS

Q1. Why Use Asynchronous Messaging At All?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Asynchronous messaging lets a producer hand off work to a broker and continue immediately, instead of blocking until a downstream service responds. The three classic reasons are decoupling, buffering and resilience.

Decoupling. The producer does not need to know who consumes the message, how many consumers there are, or whether they are even online right now. It publishes to a topic or queue and walks away. New consumers can be added later without touching the producer. This temporal and spatial decoupling is the single biggest architectural benefit.

Buffering (load levelling). A broker absorbs traffic spikes. If 10,000 orders arrive in one second but the payment service can only process 500 per second, the queue holds the backlog and the consumer drains it at its own pace. Without a buffer, that spike would either crash the consumer or force you to over-provision for peak load you rarely hit.

Resilience. If the consumer is down, messages wait durably in the broker rather than being lost. When the consumer recovers it picks up where it left off. A synchronous REST call to a dead service simply fails; a message to a durable queue survives the outage.

Interview Gold Point

Frame it as "smoothing and surviving." Async messaging smooths spiky load and survives downstream failure. The trade-off you should always mention unprompted is added complexity and eventual consistency: the caller no longer gets an immediate answer, so you must design for "the work will happen, just not right now."

Q2. Synchronous (REST) vs Asynchronous Communication

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

In synchronous communication the caller sends a request and blocks until it receives a response, as with a REST or gRPC call. The two services are coupled in time: both must be available, and the caller's latency is the sum of every downstream hop. It is simple, gives an immediate result, and is the right choice for a query where the user is waiting for an answer (for example "fetch this user's profile").

In asynchronous communication the caller publishes a message and moves on; the work happens later on a separate thread of execution in another service. The two services are decoupled in time, which buys resilience and scalability but costs you immediacy and simplicity. It is the right choice for commands and events that can happen in the background (for example "send a welcome email" or "order placed").

Aspect Synchronous (REST) Asynchronous (Messaging)
Coupling Caller waits for callee Decoupled via broker
Availability Both must be up Consumer may be down
Latency felt by caller Sum of all hops Just the publish
Result Immediate Eventual
Best for Queries needing an answer Background work, events

Common Mistake

Treating it as "async is better." It is not better, it is different. A request the user is staring at (login, search) should be synchronous. The senior answer is "synchronous for queries that need an answer now, asynchronous for side effects and fan-out work that can be eventually consistent." Reaching for messaging where a simple REST call would do just adds operational overhead.

Q3. Queue (Point-to-Point) vs Pub/Sub vs Event Streaming

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

These are three different delivery models and the distinction is a favourite interview question.

Point-to-point (queue). One message is delivered to exactly one consumer among many. If you run five workers on the same queue, the broker load-balances messages across them and each message is processed once. This is the classic "work queue" / competing consumers pattern (RabbitMQ queues, AWS SQS). It is ideal for distributing tasks.

Publish/subscribe (pub/sub). One message is delivered to every interested subscriber. The producer publishes an event and N independent subscribers each get their own copy (RabbitMQ fanout exchange, AWS SNS, JMS topics). This is for fan-out: one "OrderPlaced" event notifies billing, inventory and analytics simultaneously.

Event streaming. Events are appended to a durable, ordered, replayable log that is retained for a long time, and consumers read at their own position (offset). Multiple independent consumer groups can each read the entire history, and a new consumer can replay events from the beginning (Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis). The key difference from pub/sub is that the broker does not delete a message once delivered; it keeps the log, so the data is a re-readable source of truth, not a transient notification.

Interview Favorite

The crisp one-liner: "A queue deletes a message after one consumer handles it; pub/sub copies it to every subscriber and then forgets it; a stream keeps the whole ordered log so anyone can replay it." Streaming = durable log + replay + per-consumer offset. That replay-from-history capability is what makes Kafka fundamentally different from a traditional message queue.

SECTION 2 - APACHE KAFKA

Q4. Kafka Core Concepts Recap (Topic, Partition, Offset, Consumer Group, Broker)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Kafka is an event streaming platform built on a distributed, append-only commit log. The Microservices chapter goes deep on Kafka, so this is just the vocabulary you must be able to rattle off.

A topic is a named stream of events (for example orders). A topic is split into partitions, which are the unit of parallelism and ordering. Each message in a partition gets a monotonically increasing offset, which is just its position in that partition's log. A broker is a Kafka server; a cluster of brokers stores the partitions and replicates them for fault tolerance. Producers write to partitions; consumers read by tracking offsets.

A consumer group is a set of consumers that cooperate to read a topic. Kafka assigns each partition to exactly one consumer within a group, so adding consumers (up to the partition count) scales throughput, and each group gets its own independent view of the offsets. Two different groups reading the same topic each receive the full stream, which is how Kafka does pub/sub on top of a log.

Term One-line meaning
Topic Named stream of events
Partition Ordered shard of a topic; unit of parallelism
Offset Position of a message within a partition
Broker A Kafka server holding partitions
Consumer Group Consumers sharing partitions; own their offsets

Interview Gold Point

The relationship to nail: ordering is guaranteed only within a partition, not across a topic. Parallelism within a group is capped at the number of partitions, so partition count is a capacity decision you make up front. Modern Kafka (KRaft mode) no longer needs ZooKeeper, which is a good detail to drop if asked about cluster coordination.

Q5. How Partitioning Enables Scale & Ordering

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Partitioning is the single mechanism behind both Kafka's horizontal scalability and its ordering guarantee, and explaining how the two interact separates seniors from juniors.

Scale. Because a topic is split into N partitions that can live on different brokers, producers and consumers can work on them in parallel. With 12 partitions, a consumer group can have up to 12 active consumers, each owning a partition, so throughput grows nearly linearly until you hit the partition count.

Ordering. Kafka guarantees ordering only within a single partition. The producer chooses a partition by hashing the message key, so all messages with the same key land in the same partition and are therefore processed in order. If you need every event for a given order processed in sequence, you use the order id as the key. 

// Same key -> same partition -> strict ordering for that key
producer.send(new ProducerRecord<>(
        "orders",
        order.getId(),        // key: drives partition selection
        order.toJson()));     // value

Common Mistake

Claiming "Kafka guarantees ordering." It does not, globally. The tension is real: more partitions means more parallelism but coarser ordering. The senior framing is "choose the partition key to match the entity whose order matters, and accept that ordering across keys is undefined." If you need total ordering, you are forced down to a single partition and lose parallelism.

Q6. Log Retention & Replay

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Unlike a traditional queue, Kafka does not delete a message when it is consumed. Messages stay in the partition log according to a retention policy, and consumers track their own position. This is what makes replay possible.

Retention can be time-based (keep 7 days), size-based (keep 100 GB per partition), or compacted (keep only the latest value per key, useful for changelog/state topics). Because the data is still there, a consumer can reset its offset and re-read history. That powers reprocessing after a bug fix, bootstrapping a brand-new consumer (for example a new analytics pipeline), and event sourcing where the log is the source of truth. 

# Reset a consumer group to replay everything from the start
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
  --group analytics --topic orders \
  --reset-offsets --to-earliest --execute

Interview Favorite

"The consumer owns its offset, not the broker." In RabbitMQ the broker removes a message once acked; in Kafka the broker keeps the log and each consumer group decides where to read. This decoupling of storage from consumption is exactly why Kafka can serve replay, multiple independent consumers and event sourcing, none of which a plain queue does well.

Q7. When Is Kafka the Right Choice?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Reach for Kafka when you need high-throughput event streaming, durable retention, replay, and multiple independent consumers reading the same data. Concretely:

* Very high volume (hundreds of thousands to millions of events per second).
* You want events retained as a re-readable log (event sourcing, replay, audit).
* Many independent consumer groups need the same stream (one event feeds billing, search indexing and analytics).
* Stream processing and aggregations (Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, Flink).
* Ordering per key matters and you can partition by that key.

Kafka is a poor fit when you mainly need flexible per-message routing, complex priorities, or simple low-volume task queues with per-message acknowledgement and short-lived messages. That is RabbitMQ territory. It is also operationally heavier than a managed queue like SQS, so for low volume a managed service is often the pragmatic choice.

Interview Gold Point

Tie it back to the Microservices chapter: Kafka is the typical backbone for event-driven architecture, the transactional outbox relay target, and the event bus behind choreographed sagas. The decision rule to say out loud: "Kafka for streams of events I want to keep and replay; a queue for transient work I want to route and process once."

SECTION 3 - RABBITMQ

Q8. The RabbitMQ / AMQP Model (Exchange, Queue, Binding, Routing Key)

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

RabbitMQ implements AMQP, and its defining feature is that producers never publish directly to a queue. They publish to an exchange, and the exchange decides which queues receive the message based on routing rules. This indirection is what gives RabbitMQ its flexible routing.

The four pieces fit together like this. A producer sends a message with a routing key to an exchange. A binding is a rule that connects an exchange to a queue, optionally matching on the routing key. The exchange evaluates the bindings and copies the message into every matching queue. Consumers then read from queues and send an ack once they have processed a message; if they do not ack (crash, nack), the message is requeued or dead-lettered. 

Producer --(routing key)--> Exchange --(binding)--> Queue --> Consumer (ack)

Interview Gold Point

The mental model to state: "Kafka is a log you read from; RabbitMQ is a router that pushes messages into queues." RabbitMQ's per-message ack and smart routing make it excellent for work distribution; its messages are typically transient and removed once acked, which is the opposite of Kafka's retained log.

Q9. RabbitMQ Exchange Types (Direct / Topic / Fanout)

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

The exchange type controls how routing keys map to queues, and being able to pick the right one is a common practical question.

Direct. Routes to queues whose binding key exactly matches the message's routing key. Use it for precise, point routing, for example send order.created to a single queue bound with that exact key.

Topic. Routes on wildcard pattern matching of dotted routing keys, where * matches one word and # matches zero or more. A queue bound with order.*.eu receives order.created.eu but not order.created.us. This is the most flexible and most used type for category-based fan-out.

Fanout. Ignores the routing key entirely and broadcasts to every bound queue. This is straight pub/sub: every subscriber gets a copy. (There is also a fourth, headers, which routes on message header attributes instead of the routing key, but direct/topic/fanout are what interviewers ask about.)

Exchange Routing rule Typical use
Direct Exact routing-key match Targeted point routing
Topic Wildcard pattern (* and #) Category-based fan-out
Fanout Broadcast to all bound queues Pub/sub notifications

Interview Favorite

If asked "how would you broadcast an event to many services in RabbitMQ?" the answer is a fanout exchange (or a topic exchange with a broad pattern). If asked "how do I route by category?" say topic. The default (nameless) exchange is a direct exchange that routes by queue name, which is why convertAndSend("queueName", msg) just works in simple demos.

Q10. When RabbitMQ Fits Better Than Kafka

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

RabbitMQ is the better fit when your problem is task distribution and routing rather than a high-volume replayable stream. Prefer RabbitMQ when:

* You need flexible, complex routing (topic/header based) to direct messages to specific consumers.
* You need per-message acknowledgement and immediate requeue/retry semantics for a work queue.
* You want priorities, TTLs, delayed delivery, or a built-in dead-letter mechanism out of the box.
* Volume is moderate and messages are transient commands ("resize this image", "send this email") rather than a permanent event log.
* You want a simpler operational footprint than running a Kafka cluster for streaming.

Choose Kafka instead when you need very high throughput, long retention, replay, ordering per key at scale, or many independent consumers reading the same stream.

Interview Gold Point

The one-sentence rule of thumb: "RabbitMQ for smart routing of transient tasks (a message broker); Kafka for high-throughput retained event streams you can replay (a streaming log)." Saying both can do pub/sub but solve different problems, and naming the routing-vs-replay axis, signals real experience rather than buzzword matching.

SECTION 4 - COMPARISON

Q11. Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs AWS SQS/SNS - How To Choose

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

These three (four) cover most of what a backend team will reach for. Kafka is a self-managed event streaming log. RabbitMQ is a feature-rich message broker. AWS SQS is a fully managed queue (point-to-point) and SNS is a fully managed pub/sub notification service; the common AWS pattern is SNS fan-out to multiple SQS queues.

Dimension Kafka RabbitMQ AWS SQS / SNS
Model Streaming log Broker (exchange/queue) Managed queue (SQS) / pub-sub (SNS)
Ordering Per partition Per queue (best effort) FIFO queues only; standard = best effort
Retention Long (days, replayable) Until acked (transient) Up to 14 days, then deleted
Throughput Very high (millions/s) High (tens of thousands/s) High, auto-scaled by AWS
Replay Yes (offset reset) No No
Ops burden Highest (run a cluster) Medium Lowest (fully managed)
Best use case Event streaming, replay, analytics Routing, task/work queues Simple decoupling on AWS

Interview Favorite

The decision framework to verbalise: start from requirements, not tools. Need replay and high-volume streams? Kafka. Need complex routing and per-message control of transient tasks? RabbitMQ. Already on AWS and just need to decouple two services without running infrastructure? SQS, with SNS in front when you need fan-out. The senior signal is choosing the simplest option that meets the requirement, often a managed queue, rather than defaulting to Kafka for everything.

SECTION 5 - DELIVERY GUARANTEES & RELIABILITY

Q12. Delivery Guarantees: At-Most-Once, At-Least-Once, Exactly-Once

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Every messaging system gives you one of three guarantees, determined mostly by when you acknowledge or commit.

At-most-once. A message is delivered zero or one times; it may be lost but never duplicated. You get this by committing the offset (or acking) before processing. Fast and simple, acceptable only when occasional loss is tolerable (for example metrics samples).

At-least-once. A message is delivered one or more times; never lost but possibly duplicated. You get this by committing/acking after successful processing. If the consumer crashes after processing but before committing, it reprocesses on restart. This is the default and most common guarantee, and it is why idempotency matters.

Exactly-once. Each message is effectively processed once, with neither loss nor duplication. This is the hardest because a consumer's two actions, processing the message and recording that it was processed, are not naturally atomic across a network. Kafka offers exactly-once within Kafka via idempotent producers and transactions (atomic read-process-write across topics plus offset commit). But the moment your side effect is an external system (charge a card, send an email), end-to-end exactly-once is generally not achievable, so you settle for at-least-once plus idempotent consumers.

Guarantee Duplicates? Loss? How
At-most-once No Possible Commit before processing
At-least-once Possible No Commit after processing
Exactly-once No No Transactions / dedup; hard with external systems

Interview Gold Point

The line that wins this question: "In practice I aim for at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers, which gives effectively-once processing without the cost and limits of true exactly-once." Then add: Kafka's exactly-once is real but only end-to-end within Kafka; it cannot make an external API call exactly once.

Q13. Idempotent Consumers & Deduplication

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Because at-least-once delivery can deliver the same message twice, the consumer must be safe to run more than once with the same input. That property is idempotency: processing the same message N times produces the same result as processing it once.

There are two main techniques. First, make the operation naturally idempotent: an UPSERT by a business key, or "set status = SHIPPED" rather than "increment count," gives the same end state on retry. Second, when the operation is not naturally idempotent (charge a card, send an email), deduplicate using a unique message id. The consumer records each processed id in a store (a database table with a unique constraint, or Redis with a TTL) and skips the message if the id is already present, ideally in the same transaction as the side effect. 

@KafkaListener(topics = "payments", groupId = "billing")
@Transactional
public void handle(PaymentEvent event) {
    // dedup table has UNIQUE(message_id)
    if (processedRepo.existsById(event.getMessageId())) {
        return; // already handled -> skip duplicate
    }
    processedRepo.save(new Processed(event.getMessageId()));
    paymentService.charge(event); // safe: runs at most once per id
}

Common Mistake

Relying on the broker's "exactly-once" setting and skipping dedup. Even with Kafka transactions, the moment you touch an external system you are back to at-least-once. The robust design is always: at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumer. Note also that the dedup write and the side effect should share a transaction (or use the outbox pattern), otherwise a crash between them reopens the duplicate window.

Q14. Message Ordering

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Ordering is harder than it looks because parallelism and strict ordering pull in opposite directions. The general rule across systems: you can only guarantee ordering within a single sequential channel.

In Kafka, ordering holds within a partition. Route related events to the same partition using a partition key (for example the aggregate id), and a single consumer in the group will process them in order. You lose global ordering across partitions, which is the price of parallelism.

In RabbitMQ, a single queue with a single consumer preserves order, but the moment you add competing consumers for throughput, messages can be processed out of order (and requeue-on-failure can reorder too). In SQS, standard queues are best-effort ordering; only FIFO queues guarantee order, and they do it per message-group id at reduced throughput.

Interview Favorite

The phrase to use: "Order by key, not globally." Identify the entity whose events must stay in sequence, key/group by that, and accept that unrelated entities are processed concurrently. If a candidate demands global total ordering, push back: it forces a single partition / single consumer and destroys scalability, so it is almost never the right requirement.

Q15. Dead Letter Queues, Retries & Poison Messages

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

A poison message is one the consumer can never process successfully (malformed payload, references a deleted record, hits a permanent bug). If you blindly retry it forever, it blocks the partition or queue and starves every message behind it. You need a strategy to retry the transient failures and quarantine the permanent ones.

Retries with exponential backoff. Transient failures (a downstream service briefly down, a timeout) should be retried, but with increasing delays (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s...) plus jitter to avoid hammering the dependency and to avoid a thundering herd. Cap the number of attempts.

Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). After the retry budget is exhausted, route the message to a separate dead-letter queue or topic instead of dropping it or looping forever. The DLQ preserves the message and its failure context so the main flow keeps moving, and a human or an automated job can inspect, fix and replay the dead-lettered messages later. RabbitMQ supports DLX (dead-letter exchanges) natively; SQS has a redrive policy with a maxReceiveCount; Spring Kafka provides a DeadLetterPublishingRecoverer. 

// Spring Kafka: retry with backoff, then route to <topic>.DLT
@Bean
DefaultErrorHandler errorHandler(KafkaTemplate<?, ?> template) {
    var recoverer = new DeadLetterPublishingRecoverer(template);
    // 3 retries, 1s initial, x2 multiplier, max 10s
    var backoff = new ExponentialBackOffWithMaxRetries(3);
    backoff.setInitialInterval(1000L);
    backoff.setMultiplier(2.0);
    backoff.setMaxInterval(10000L);
    return new DefaultErrorHandler(recoverer, backoff);
}

Interview Gold Point

Distinguish transient vs permanent failure out loud: retry the transient with backoff and jitter, dead-letter the permanent. The DLQ is not a graveyard; you should monitor and alert on its depth, because a growing DLQ is an early warning of a bug or a bad deploy. Replaying from the DLQ after a fix is the recovery story interviewers want to hear.

SECTION 6 - PATTERNS & SPRING

Q16. Consumer Lag, Backpressure & Spring Integration

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Consumer lag is how far a consumer group is behind the latest produced offset, in other words the size of the backlog it has not yet read. It is the single most important health metric for a streaming consumer: steadily rising lag means producers are outpacing consumers and you must scale out consumers (up to the partition count), optimise processing, or add partitions. Tools like the consumer-groups CLI, Burrow, or your monitoring stack expose it.

Backpressure is the mechanism that stops a fast producer or broker from overwhelming a slow consumer. A pull-based system like Kafka has natural backpressure: the consumer fetches at its own pace and the durable log buffers the rest, so you bound in-flight work via max.poll.records and concurrency rather than dropping data. Push-based systems like RabbitMQ use a prefetch limit (QoS) so the broker only sends a bounded number of unacked messages per consumer. The buffering property from Q1 is exactly what gives the consumer room to apply backpressure instead of falling over.

The dual-write problem and the transactional outbox. A classic trap is updating the database and publishing an event in two separate steps: if the process crashes between them, you either lose the event or publish one that the DB never committed. The transactional outbox pattern fixes this by writing the event into an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the business change, then a relay (or CDC tool like Debezium) reads the outbox and publishes to Kafka reliably. This is covered in depth in the Microservices chapter alongside the saga pattern; for messaging interviews you just need to name the dual-write problem and that the outbox solves it.

Spring integration. Spring for Apache Kafka gives you @KafkaListener and KafkaTemplate; Spring AMQP gives you @RabbitListener and RabbitTemplate. Both turn a method into a message consumer with container-managed threading, acknowledgement and error handling. 

// Spring Kafka consumer
@KafkaListener(topics = "orders", groupId = "fulfilment", concurrency = "3")
public void onOrder(OrderEvent event, Acknowledgment ack) {
    fulfilmentService.process(event); // idempotent
    ack.acknowledge();                // manual commit AFTER processing
}

// Spring AMQP (RabbitMQ) consumer
@RabbitListener(queues = "orders.queue")
public void onOrder(OrderEvent event) {
    fulfilmentService.process(event);  // ack on normal return
}

Interview Favorite

Mention manual acknowledgement: commit/ack after processing to get at-least-once, never before.
