When a user checks out, an orders row is created representing the whole purchase, and order_items rows capture each individual product, quantity and the price at the time of sale.

The payments table records the financial transactions tied to an order, and the inventory table tracks available stock per product. A crucial design point is that order_items stores the price as it was when purchased rather than referencing the live product price, because product prices change over time and historical orders must remain accurate, this is a deliberate, well-justified denormalization.

Q98. Why Separate Order And Order Items?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

An order and its line items are separated because they model a one-to-many relationship: a single order can contain many different products, each with its own quantity and price. Trying to cram multiple products into one orders row would violate first normal form and make the data impossible to query cleanly.

The orders table holds information about the purchase as a whole, customer, shipping address, order status, total, timestamp, while each order_items row holds the per-product detail. This separation keeps the schema normalized, lets each line item be tracked and fulfilled independently, and makes reporting on product-level sales straightforward.

Relationship


Order

1

↓

Many

Order Items
Q99. Inventory Design Consideration

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Challenge

The central challenge in inventory design is preventing overselling, allowing more units to be sold than are actually in stock, when many customers buy the same scarce item at the same time. This is a classic concurrency problem: two transactions both read "1 in stock" and both proceed to sell it.

Solutions

There are three common approaches. Optimistic locking adds a version column and only commits the decrement if the version is unchanged, retrying on conflict, which works well when contention is low. Pessimistic locking takes an explicit lock on the inventory row (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) so concurrent buyers serialise, which is simpler but reduces concurrency on hot items.

A reservation system temporarily holds stock for a customer during checkout (with a timeout), decoupling the reserve step from the final purchase, which is what high-scale platforms use. The right choice depends on contention levels and user-experience requirements, and articulating that trade-off is exactly what an interviewer is probing for.

  • Optimistic Locking
  • Pessimistic Locking
  • Reservation Systems

SECTION 20 - BOOKING SYSTEM DESIGN

Q100. Design Movie Ticket Booking System

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Core Tables


movies

theaters

screens

shows

seats

bookings

payments

A movie-booking schema is naturally hierarchical. Theaters contain multiple screens, each screen has a fixed set of seats, and a show represents a specific movie playing on a specific screen at a specific time. Bookings link a user to the seats they reserve for a particular show, and payments record the transaction for each booking.

The key modelling insight is that availability is per show, not per seat in the abstract: the same physical seat is independently bookable for each different show. So the booking (or a show_seats) table ties a seat to a show, and that combination is what must be protected against double booking. Getting this granularity right is the heart of the design.

Q101. Biggest Challenge?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

The biggest challenge is preventing double booking, two users reserving the same seat for the same show at the same instant. Booking is a high-contention concurrency problem, because popular shows trigger many simultaneous attempts to grab the same limited seats the moment tickets open.

This is fundamentally the same class of problem as inventory overselling: without proper concurrency control, two transactions can both see a seat as available and both book it. Solving it correctly while keeping the system responsive under a flood of concurrent requests is what makes booking systems a favourite senior design question.

Q102. How To Prevent Double Booking?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

The foundation is a unique constraint on the (show_id, seat_id) combination, so the database itself rejects any second booking of the same seat for the same show, guaranteeing correctness even under a race. Wrapping the reserve-and-confirm steps in a transaction ensures the check and the write happen atomically.

For concurrency control you choose between pessimistic locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the seat rows so concurrent buyers serialise) and optimistic locking (a version column with retry on conflict). High-scale systems typically add a temporary seat-reservation step with a short timeout so a seat is held while the user pays, then released if payment is not completed. Combining the database unique constraint with a locking strategy and a reservation flow is the complete, production-grade answer.

  • Unique Constraints
  • Transactions
  • Optimistic Locking
  • Pessimistic Locking

SECTION 21 - DATABASE TROUBLESHOOTING

Q103. API Suddenly Becomes Slow

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Investigation Flow

When an API suddenly slows down, the senior approach is a systematic, top-down investigation rather than jumping to a guess. Start with application logs to confirm where the latency is, is it the database calls, an external service or the application itself? Then move to database metrics (CPU, connections, active queries) to see if the database is the bottleneck.

Next, examine the execution plans of the suspect queries and the slow query log to find the specific offenders, check whether indexes are being used (a recently changed query or grown table may now scan), and finally review infrastructure metrics like disk I/O, memory and network. Following this chain isolates the root cause methodically; interviewers care more about this structured process than about naming a single magic fix.

  1. Application Logs
  2. Database Metrics
  3. Execution Plans
  4. Slow Query Logs
  5. Index Usage
  6. Infrastructure Metrics
Q104. Database CPU 100%

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Check

A database pegged at 100% CPU is almost always doing too much computation per query. The first things to check are slow queries and missing indexes, because a query forced into a full table scan burns CPU reading and filtering far more rows than necessary, and a sudden traffic increase against such a query can saturate the server.

Also investigate lock contention, where transactions pile up waiting and retrying, and full table scans surfaced by the execution plans. Tools like the database's active-query view (pg_stat_activity in PostgreSQL, SHOW PROCESSLIST in MySQL) reveal what is currently running. The fix is usually to add the right index, rewrite the offending query, or address the workload spike, not simply to throw more hardware at it.

  • Slow Queries
  • Missing Indexes
  • Lock Contention
  • Full Table Scans
Q105. Database Connections Exhausted

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Possible Causes

Running out of database connections usually points to one of three causes. Connection leaks, where the application opens connections but fails to close or return them to the pool (often due to missing try-with-resources or unclosed transactions), steadily consume the pool until none remain. Long-running queries hold their connections for a long time, so fewer are available for new requests.

A connection pool that is simply too small for the traffic is the third cause. The investigation looks at active versus idle connections and how long they are held. The fixes are to ensure connections are always released, to optimise the slow queries that hold them, and to size the pool appropriately, remembering that an oversized pool can itself overwhelm the database, so the right size is bounded by what the database can handle.

  • Connection Leaks
  • Long Queries
  • Small Connection Pool
Q106. What Is Connection Pooling?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Connection pooling is the technique of maintaining a pool of pre-established database connections that the application reuses, rather than opening and closing a brand-new connection for every request. Establishing a connection is expensive, it involves a network handshake, authentication and server-side setup, so reusing connections eliminates that repeated overhead.

The pool hands an idle connection to a request, takes it back when the request finishes, and caps the total number of connections to protect the database from being overwhelmed. In Spring Boot this is handled by HikariCP out of the box. Tuning the pool size correctly is critical: too small starves the application, while too large can exhaust the database's own connection limit and memory.

Q107. Why HikariCP?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

HikariCP is the default connection pool in Spring Boot because it is exceptionally fast and lightweight. Its codebase is small and highly optimised, with minimal locking and lean bytecode, which makes it consistently outperform older pools like Apache DBCP and C3P0 in benchmarks for both throughput and latency.

It is also reliable and battle-tested, with sensible defaults and important safeguards such as leak detection and connection-validation timeouts. Because Spring Boot auto-configures it, you simply tune properties like maximum-pool-size and connection-timeout. Being able to explain why HikariCP is the default, and which pool-size and timeout settings matter, signals real production awareness.

  • Fast
  • Lightweight
  • Reliable
  • Spring Boot Default

SECTION 22 - MOST ASKED DATABASE QUESTIONS

Q108. Top Database Questions Asked In Interviews

ROI: Reality Check ★★★★★

These are the topics that appear again and again across backend interviews, so they deserve the deepest preparation. The list is dominated by performance and concurrency fundamentals, indexes, the clustered versus non-clustered distinction, joins, normalization, transactions, isolation levels and deadlocks, because these separate engineers who memorise SQL syntax from those who understand how the database actually behaves.

Execution plans, pagination and database design round out the list, reflecting the practical skills of diagnosing slow queries and designing schemas that scale. If you can explain each of these confidently with a real example from your own work, you will handle the large majority of database interview questions.

  1. Indexes
  2. Clustered vs Non-Clustered
  3. Joins
  4. Normalization
  5. Transactions
  6. Isolation Levels
  7. Deadlocks
  8. Execution Plans
  9. Pagination
  10. Database Design
Q109. If You Remember Only 10 Database Topics

ROI: Maximum ★★★★★

If your preparation time is limited, these ten topics give the highest return. Indexes and execution plans are the foundation of performance; joins, transactions, isolation levels and deadlocks are the foundation of correctness and concurrency. Mastering just these covers the questions asked in nearly every interview.

Pagination, replication and sharding demonstrate that you can think about scale, while database design proves you can apply all of it to a real problem. The key is not to memorise definitions but to be able to discuss each topic with a concrete example, the trade-off you weighed, and how it played out in a system you actually built or could build.

  1. Indexes
  2. Execution Plans
  3. Joins
  4. Transactions
  5. Isolation Levels
  6. Deadlocks
  7. Pagination
  8. Replication
  9. Sharding
  10. Database Design

SQL & DATABASE DESIGN FILE COMPLETE

Total Coverage

  • SQL Fundamentals
  • Joins
  • Indexes
  • Normalization
  • ACID
  • Transactions
  • Isolation Levels
  • Deadlocks
  • Execution Plans
  • Pagination
  • Replication
  • Sharding
  • PostgreSQL
  • MySQL
  • Database Design
  • Performance Tuning
  • Troubleshooting

NEXT FILE: 13-SYSTEM-DESIGN-FOR-4-YEAR-BACKEND-ENGINEER.html

This is where many Senior Software Engineer interviews are won or lost. Topics include: * URL Shortener * Notification System * Chat System * Job Portal Design * Rate Limiter * API Design * Scalability * Caching * Database Selection * Load Balancing * Real Architecture Discussions This section ties together Java, Spring Boot, Databases, Redis, Kafka, Microservices and Production Architecture.

23 - NOSQL & MONGODB INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

NoSQL questions show up the moment an interviewer wants to test whether you can pick the right tool instead of forcing everything into a relational schema. For 4+ years of backend experience, knowing only SQL is no longer enough. Most modern systems mix a relational store with one or more NoSQL stores, and senior engineers are expected to justify that split. NoSQL does not replace SQL knowledge (covered in the SQL & Database Design chapter) - it complements it. The relational world gives you strong schemas, joins and ACID; NoSQL gives you flexible schemas, horizontal scale and data models that match your access patterns. For 4+ years experience, interviewers expect: * Knowing why NoSQL emerged and the four families * When to choose NoSQL over a relational database * MongoDB document modelling (embedding vs referencing) * Consistency trade-offs (ACID vs BASE, eventual consistency) * Indexing and the aggregation pipeline * Scaling with replica sets and sharding * Spring Data MongoDB in practice They usually continue with deeper questions on data modelling, consistency and how you would scale the store under real production load.

TOP ROI NOSQL QUESTIONS

  1. What Is NoSQL And Why Did It Emerge
  2. The Four Types Of NoSQL Databases
  3. SQL vs NoSQL - When To Choose Which
  4. ACID vs BASE
  5. CAP Theorem Applied To NoSQL
  6. Eventual Consistency And Its Trade-offs
  7. Documents, Collections And BSON
  8. The _id Field
  9. Mongo Document vs Relational Row
  10. Embedding vs Referencing
  11. Modeling One-to-Many And Many-to-Many
  12. The Schema-Design Mindset Shift
  13. CRUD And Basic Queries
  14. Indexes In MongoDB
  15. The Aggregation Pipeline
  16. Replica Sets, Sharding, Transactions And Spring Data MongoDB

SECTION 1 - NOSQL FUNDAMENTALS

Q1. What Is NoSQL And Why Did It Emerge?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

NoSQL ("Not Only SQL") is a family of databases that relax the rigid relational model - fixed schemas, normalized tables and joins - in favour of flexible data models that scale horizontally across commodity machines.

It emerged in the mid-2000s when companies like Google, Amazon and Facebook hit the limits of a single relational server. The pain points were concrete:

  • Scale - relational databases scale vertically (bigger box); web-scale workloads needed to scale horizontally across many cheap nodes.
  • Schema rigidity - rapidly evolving products did not want a migration for every new field.
  • Unstructured / semi-structured data - JSON-like documents, logs and graphs do not map cleanly to tables.
  • Throughput - high write volumes where the cost of joins and strict ACID became a bottleneck.

The key insight: you trade some of the relational guarantees (rich joins, strict consistency) for scalability and a data model that fits your access patterns.

Interview Gold Point

Never say "NoSQL is faster than SQL". It is not inherently faster - it is differently optimized. The correct framing is: NoSQL optimizes for horizontal scale and access-pattern-shaped data at the cost of joins and strong consistency. Saying that signals senior judgement.

Q2. What Are The Four Types Of NoSQL Databases?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

There are four classic NoSQL families, each suited to a different shape of data and access pattern.

TypeStores Data AsExample DBGood For
DocumentJSON / BSON documentsMongoDBFlexible records, content, catalogs
Key-ValueKey → opaque valueRedisCaching, sessions, counters
Column-Family (wide-column)Rows with dynamic columns grouped in familiesCassandraHuge write-heavy time-series / analytics
GraphNodes and edgesNeo4jRelationships - social, fraud, recommendations

A document is essentially a self-contained nested record. A key-value store is the simplest model - a giant distributed hash map. Wide-column stores look table-ish but each row can have different columns and they scale writes massively. Graph databases make traversing relationships ("friends of friends") cheap, which is painful with joins in SQL.

Interview Favorite

Interviewers love "give me a real example for each". Redis for a session cache (see the caching content in the System Design chapter), MongoDB for a product catalog, Cassandra for IoT/event ingestion, Neo4j for a "people you may know" feature. Concrete examples beat definitions.

Q3. SQL vs NoSQL - When Do You Choose Which?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Short Answer

Choose based on data shape, access patterns, consistency needs and scale - not hype. Most real systems use both (polyglot persistence).

DimensionSQL (Relational)NoSQL
SchemaFixed, enforcedFlexible / dynamic
ScalingVertical (mostly)Horizontal (built-in)
JoinsFirst-classLimited / avoided by design
TransactionsStrong ACIDVaries (often eventual / limited)
Best whenComplex relationships, strong consistency, reportingHigh scale, evolving schema, access-pattern-shaped data

Pick SQL when you have well-defined relationships, need multi-table transactions and strong consistency - banking, orders, inventory ledgers. Pick NoSQL when the schema evolves quickly, you need to scale out, or the data is naturally a self-contained document or simple key lookup.

The relational contrast (normalization, joins, ACID) is covered in depth in the SQL & Database Design chapter - this chapter is the complement to it.

Common Mistake

Saying "use NoSQL because it scales" without naming the access pattern. NoSQL scales because it avoids joins and lets you shard - but only if your data model matches your queries. If you need ad-hoc joins and reporting, NoSQL becomes painful. Always tie the choice to the read/write pattern.

SECTION 2 - CONSISTENCY MODELS

Q4. What Is The Difference Between ACID And BASE?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

ACID is the relational guarantee model; BASE is the philosophy many distributed NoSQL systems adopt to favour availability and scale.

ACIDBASE
AtomicityBasically Available
ConsistencySoft state
IsolationEventually consistent
Durability(prioritizes availability over strict consistency)

ACID guarantees every transaction is all-or-nothing, leaves the database in a valid state, is isolated from other transactions and survives crashes. BASE accepts that in a large distributed system the data may be temporarily inconsistent ("soft state") but will converge ("eventually consistent"), in exchange for staying available and scalable.

Note: MongoDB is ACID-compliant at the single-document level by default, and supports multi-document ACID transactions since 4.0 (see Q16). So "NoSQL = no ACID" is outdated.

Interview Gold Point

The senior nuance: ACID vs BASE is a spectrum, not a binary. Modern databases let you tune it - in MongoDB you choose write concern and read preference per operation (Q16), effectively dialing where you sit between strong and eventual consistency.

Q5. How Does The CAP Theorem Apply To NoSQL?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

CAP states that during a network Partition a distributed system can guarantee only Consistency or Availability, not both. Since partitions are unavoidable in a distributed store, you are really choosing between CP and AP when the network splits.

  • CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance) - reject reads/writes that cannot be made consistent. MongoDB leans CP: during a partition the primary side stays consistent, the minority side cannot accept writes.
  • AP (Availability + Partition tolerance) - keep serving and reconcile later. Cassandra leans AP with tunable consistency.

CAP is covered in full in the Microservices chapter (it is central to the database-per-service pattern). For NoSQL the practical takeaway is: read the docs to learn where your store sits, because that decision is baked into its replication design.

Common Mistake

Treating CAP as "pick 2 of 3 always". Partition tolerance is not optional in a distributed system - you only sacrifice C or A while a partition is happening. The rest of the time you can have both. Stating it as a runtime trade-off, not a permanent one, is the senior answer.

Q6. What Is Eventual Consistency And What Are Its Trade-offs?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Eventual consistency means that if no new writes occur, all replicas will eventually return the same value - but for a short window after a write, different replicas may return stale data.

Trade-offs:

  • Pro - high availability and low latency; reads can be served by any replica without coordination.
  • Con - stale reads. A user may update their profile and momentarily see the old value if they read from a lagging secondary.
  • Con - conflict resolution. Concurrent writes to different replicas need a strategy (last-write-wins, vector clocks, application merge).

You mitigate it where it matters: in MongoDB, read from the primary (read preference) or require majority write concern for the data that must be read-your-own-write consistent (e.g. payment status), while letting low-stakes data (e.g. view counts) stay eventually consistent.

Interview Favorite

The killer example: "Why is your Amazon order count sometimes off for a second?" - eventual consistency. Showing you know which data tolerates staleness (analytics, counters) versus which must be strongly consistent (balances, inventory) is exactly the judgement interviewers screen for.

SECTION 3 - MONGODB BASICS

Q7. What Are Documents, Collections And BSON In MongoDB?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

These are MongoDB's core building blocks:

  • Document - a single record stored as a JSON-like structure with fields, nested objects and arrays. It is the unit of storage and roughly maps to a "row".
  • Collection - a group of documents, roughly maps to a "table". Collections are schema-flexible: documents in the same collection can have different fields.
  • BSON - "Binary JSON", the binary-encoded format MongoDB actually stores and transmits. It extends JSON with extra types (ObjectId, Date, Decimal128, Binary, 64-bit int) and is faster to traverse and more compact than text JSON.

Example document: 

{
  "_id": ObjectId("64f0a1c2e13b9a0012ab34cd"),
  "name": "Nasir Khan",
  "email": "nasir@growth-loop.io",
  "roles": ["ADMIN", "USER"],
  "address": {
    "city": "Bengaluru",
    "country": "IN"
  },
  "createdAt": ISODate("2026-06-16T10:00:00Z")
}

Notice the nested address object and the roles array embedded directly in the record - in a relational model these would be separate tables joined at query time.

Interview Gold Point

Be precise: JSON is what you read/write at the API level, BSON is what is stored on disk and on the wire. The driver converts between them. Mentioning that BSON adds typed values like Date and Decimal128 (not in plain JSON) signals you understand the storage layer.

Q8. What Is The _id Field In MongoDB?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

_id is the mandatory primary key of every MongoDB document. It is unique within a collection, automatically indexed, and immutable once set.

  • If you do not supply one, MongoDB generates an ObjectId - a 12-byte value containing a 4-byte timestamp, a 5-byte random value (machine/process) and a 3-byte incrementing counter.
  • Because the first 4 bytes are a timestamp, ObjectIds are roughly time-ordered - you can sort by _id to get near-insertion order and even extract the creation time.
  • You may use your own value (a UUID, an email, a natural key) as _id as long as it is unique.

Interview Favorite

The bonus point: an ObjectId is not sequential like an auto-increment integer. That is deliberate - sequential keys create a write hotspot when sharding (all inserts hit the same shard). ObjectId's randomness spreads writes. Connecting _id design to shard distribution is a strong senior signal.

Q9. How Does A MongoDB Document Map Versus A Relational Row?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

The terms map loosely, but the modelling philosophy is fundamentally different.

RelationalMongoDB
DatabaseDatabase
TableCollection
RowDocument
ColumnField
JoinEmbedding (or $lookup)
Foreign KeyReference (manual)

The big difference: a relational row is flat and references other tables; a Mongo document can nest related data inside itself. An order with its line items might be five rows across three relational tables, but one self-contained document in Mongo. You shape the document around how you read it, not around normalization rules.

Interview Gold Point

The one-liner: "In relational you model the data; in MongoDB you model the access pattern." That sentence captures the entire shift and almost always lands well.

SECTION 4 - DATA MODELING

Q10. Embedding vs Referencing - When Do You Denormalize?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

This is the single most important MongoDB design decision. You either embed related data inside the parent document or reference it by storing its _id.

Embed WhenReference When
Data is read together (read locality)Data is read independently
One-to-few relationshipOne-to-many / many-to-many (unbounded)
Child has no life of its ownChild is shared or queried on its own
You want a single atomic read/writeDocument would grow unbounded (16MB limit)

Embed example (order + items together): 

{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "customer": "Nasir",
  "items": [
    { "sku": "A1", "qty": 2, "price": 19.99 },
    { "sku": "B7", "qty": 1, "price": 9.50 }
  ],
  "total": 49.48
}

Reference example (order points to a shared customer): 

{
  "_id": ObjectId("..."),
  "customerId": ObjectId("64ab..."),
  "items": [ /* ... */ ]
}

Denormalize (embed/duplicate) when reads dominate and the data is read together; normalize (reference) when data is large, shared, or grows without bound. Remember the hard limit: a single BSON document cannot exceed 16MB.

Interview Gold Point

The rule of thumb interviewers want: "embed for read performance, reference to avoid duplication and unbounded growth." Then mention the 16MB document cap as the hard constraint that forces referencing for true one-to-many relationships like comments on a viral post.

Q11. How Do You Model One-to-Many And Many-to-Many In MongoDB?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

One-to-many:

  • One-to-few (a person's few addresses) - embed as an array.
  • One-to-many (a blog post's comments) - embed if bounded, otherwise reference: store postId on each comment document in a separate collection.
  • One-to-squillions (server log lines for a host) - always reference from the "many" side; never embed an unbounded array in the parent.

Many-to-many (students ↔ courses): store an array of references on one or both sides, depending on which way you query most. 

// student document
{ "_id": 1, "name": "Ana", "courseIds": [101, 102] }

// course document
{ "_id": 101, "title": "Algorithms", "studentIds": [1, 5, 9] }

There is no join table. You hold arrays of _ids on the side you read from, and resolve them with a second query or $lookup in an aggregation.

Common Mistake

Embedding an unbounded "many" (e.g. all comments inside the post). It looks clean until the post goes viral, the document blows past 16MB, and every read drags megabytes over the wire. Match the cardinality to the strategy: few = embed, many = reference.

Q12. What Is The Schema-Design Mindset Shift From Relational?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium

Short Answer

In relational design you normalize first (3NF), eliminate duplication, then write joins to assemble data. In MongoDB you do the opposite: you start from the queries and shape documents so the data you read together lives together.

  • Relational: "What is the cleanest normalized structure?" Schema first, queries follow.
  • MongoDB: "What are my read/write patterns?" Queries first, schema follows.
  • Duplication is acceptable (and sometimes desirable) if it saves a join on a hot read path - you trade storage and write complexity for read speed.
  • Schema is enforced by the application (or optional JSON Schema validation), not the engine.

This is why the SQL & Database Design chapter teaches normalization as the goal, while here denormalization is a deliberate, justified tool.

Interview Favorite

Say it crisply: "Relational optimizes for storage and integrity; MongoDB optimizes for the query." Add that you still validate at the application layer so flexibility does not become chaos - that balance is what separates a senior from someone who just dumps unstructured blobs.

SECTION 5 - QUERYING & PERFORMANCE

Q13. What Do Basic CRUD And Queries Look Like In MongoDB?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

MongoDB queries are expressed as documents - the filter is itself a JSON-like object. 

// Create
db.users.insertOne({ name: "Ana", age: 30, city: "Pune" })

// Read - find all users in Pune older than 25
db.users.find({ city: "Pune", age: { $gt: 25 } })

// Read - projection (only name + email), sorted, limited
db.users.find({ city: "Pune" }, { name: 1, email: 1, _id: 0 })
        .sort({ age: -1 })
        .limit(10)

// Update
db.users.updateOne(
  { name: "Ana" },
  { $set: { city: "Mumbai" }, $inc: { age: 1 } }
)

// Delete
db.users.deleteOne({ name: "Ana" })

Operators are prefixed with $: comparison ($gt, $lt, $in), logical ($and, $or), and update operators ($set, $inc, $push). The projection document selects fields (1 = include, 0 = exclude).

Interview Gold Point

A frequent gotcha: updateOne with $set updates only the listed fields, but passing a whole document without $set replaces the entire document (losing other fields). Knowing the difference between modify and replace semantics shows real hands-on experience.

Q14. How Do Indexes Work In MongoDB And Why Do They Matter?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

Like in relational databases, indexes are B-tree structures that let MongoDB locate documents without scanning the whole collection. Without an index, a query does a COLLSCAN (full collection scan) - fine for small data, fatal at scale.

  • Single-field index - on one field: db.users.createIndex({ email: 1 }).
  • Compound index - on multiple fields, order matters: db.users.createIndex({ city: 1, age: -1 }). It supports queries on city, or city + age, but not age alone (the "prefix rule").
  • Other types - multikey (on array fields), text, geospatial, hashed (for sharding), TTL (auto-expire documents).
  • _id is always indexed automatically.

You diagnose query performance with explain(): an IXSCAN stage means the index is used; a COLLSCAN means it is not. 

db.users.find({ city: "Pune", age: { $gt: 25 } }).explain("executionStats")

Common Mistake

Forgetting the compound-index prefix rule and over-indexing. Indexes speed reads but slow writes and consume RAM - every write must update every index. The senior answer: index to match your actual query patterns (verified with explain()), not "just in case". This mirrors the indexing discussion in the SQL & Database Design chapter.

Q15. What Is The Aggregation Pipeline?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

The aggregation pipeline is MongoDB's framework for data processing and analytics - the equivalent of SQL's GROUP BY, joins and transformations. Documents flow through an ordered array of stages, each transforming the stream and passing it to the next.

Common stages: $match (filter, like WHERE), $group (aggregate), $sort, $project (reshape, like SELECT), $lookup (left join to another collection), $unwind (flatten arrays), $limit.

Example - total revenue per city for completed orders: 

db.orders.aggregate([
  { $match: { status: "COMPLETED" } },
  { $group: {
      _id: "$city",
      totalRevenue: { $sum: "$total" },
      orderCount: { $sum: 1 }
  }},
  { $sort: { totalRevenue: -1 } },
  { $limit: 5 }
])

This is the SQL equivalent of SELECT city, SUM(total), COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE status='COMPLETED' GROUP BY city ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 5.

Interview Gold Point

Performance tip worth dropping: put $match and $sort as early as possible so they can use indexes and shrink the dataset before expensive stages like $group or $lookup. Stage ordering is to aggregation what query plans are to SQL.

SECTION 6 - SCALING & SPRING

Q16. Explain Replica Sets, Sharding, Transactions, Write/Read Concern And Spring Data MongoDB.

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

This bundle covers how MongoDB achieves high availability, scale and durability, plus how you use it from Spring.

Replica Set (high availability) - a group of mongod nodes holding the same data: one primary (takes all writes) and one or more secondaries (replicate the primary's oplog). If the primary fails, an automatic election promotes a secondary. This gives failover and redundancy. Secondaries can also serve reads (eventual consistency).

Sharding (horizontal scale) - partitions a collection across multiple shards by a shard key, so data and load spread across machines. Each shard is itself usually a replica set. Used when one server cannot hold the data or handle the write throughput. Choosing a good shard key (high cardinality, even distribution, matches query pattern) is critical to avoid hotspots.

Write concern & read preference - how you tune consistency vs latency:

  • Write concern - how many nodes must acknowledge a write. w:1 = primary only (fast, less durable); w:"majority" = majority of nodes (durable, survives failover).
  • Read preference - where reads go. primary (strong/fresh) vs secondaryPreferred (scale reads, may be stale).

Multi-document transactions - MongoDB always had atomic single-document writes, but multi-document ACID transactions arrived in 4.0 (2018) for replica sets (and 4.2 for sharded clusters). This was a big deal because it removed the last "but it's not ACID" objection - you can now update several documents atomically when you genuinely need it. The guidance is still to model so you rarely need them.

Spring Data MongoDB - gives you a repository abstraction just like Spring Data JPA (covered in the Spring Data JPA / Hibernate chapter). You extend MongoRepository and derived query methods are generated for you: 

@Document(collection = "users")
public class User {
    @Id
    private String id;          // maps to _id
    private String name;
    private String city;
    private int age;
    // getters / setters
}

public interface UserRepository extends MongoRepository<User, String> {
    List<User> findByCity(String city);
    List<User> findByCityAndAgeGreaterThan(String city, int age);
}

@Service
public class UserService {
    private final UserRepository repo;
    public UserService(UserRepository repo) { this.repo = repo; }

    public List<User> usersInCity(String city) {
        return repo.findByCity(city);   // generates the Mongo query
    }
}

For anything beyond derived queries you use MongoTemplate (criteria queries, aggregations, bulk ops).

Breadth (mention these for range): Redis is an in-memory key-value store, ideal for caching, sessions and rate limiting - the caching strategy details (cache-aside, TTL, eviction) are in the System Design chapter. Cassandra is a wide-column store built for massive write-heavy, multi-datacenter workloads with tunable (AP-leaning) consistency. Naming the right tool for each job shows polyglot-persistence maturity.

Interview Gold Point

Tie it together: "Replica sets give me availability, sharding gives me scale, write concern and read preference let me dial consistency per operation, and transactions are there for the rare cases my document model can't make atomic." That one sentence demonstrates you understand the whole operational picture, not just the API.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

One-Line Recalls

  • NoSQL - flexible schema + horizontal scale, trades joins and strict consistency.
  • Four types - Document (MongoDB), Key-Value (Redis), Wide-Column (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j).
  • Choose NoSQL - evolving schema, scale-out, access-pattern-shaped data; SQL for relationships + strong ACID.
  • ACID vs BASE - strict transactions vs basically-available, soft-state, eventually-consistent.
  • CAP - during a partition pick C or A; MongoDB is CP-leaning, Cassandra AP-leaning (see Microservices chapter).
  • Eventual consistency - replicas converge; tolerate it for counters, not for balances.
  • Document / Collection / BSON - record / table-equivalent / binary JSON stored on disk.
  • _id - mandatory, unique, indexed PK; ObjectId is time-ordered + random (good for sharding).
  • Embed vs reference - embed for read locality & one-to-few; reference for unbounded / shared (16MB cap).
  • Mindset shift - relational models the data; MongoDB models the query.
  • Indexes - single & compound; prefix rule; verify with explain() (IXSCAN good, COLLSCAN bad).
  • Aggregation - staged pipeline ($match → $group → $sort); push $match early.
  • Replica set - primary + secondaries, automatic failover = HA.
  • Sharding - partition by shard key for horizontal scale; pick a high-cardinality key.
  • Write concern / read preference - per-operation knobs between strong and eventual consistency.
  • Transactions - multi-document ACID since Mongo 4.0; single-document was always atomic.
  • Spring Data MongoDB - MongoRepository + derived queries; MongoTemplate for advanced ops.
  • Breadth - Redis = caching (System Design chapter), Cassandra = wide-column write-heavy.

19 - DEVOPS, DOCKER & KUBERNETES INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

A modern backend engineer is expected to own their service all the way to production, not just hand a JAR to an ops team. Interviewers increasingly probe how you package, deploy, configure and operate an application. You do not need to be a full-time SRE, but for a 4-year Senior role you should confidently discuss: * Containerising a Spring Boot app with Docker * The basics of orchestration with Kubernetes * How a CI/CD pipeline takes code to production * Externalised configuration and the 12-factor mindset * How deployments roll out safely without downtime This chapter focuses on the concepts and the "why", with the commands and YAML you are most likely to be asked to explain.

TOP ROI DEVOPS QUESTIONS

  1. What Is Docker and Why Use It?
  2. Image vs Container
  3. Anatomy of a Dockerfile
  4. What Is a Multi-Stage Build?
  5. Docker Image Layers & Caching
  6. What Is docker-compose?
  7. What Is Kubernetes and Why?
  8. Pod vs Deployment vs Service
  9. Liveness vs Readiness Probes
  10. ConfigMaps and Secrets
  11. How Does a Rolling Update Work?
  12. Rolling vs Blue-Green vs Canary
  13. What Is CI/CD?
  14. The 12-Factor App / Externalised Config
  15. Maven vs Gradle

SECTION 1 - DOCKER

Q1. What Is Docker and Why Use It?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Docker packages an application together with its dependencies, runtime and configuration into a single portable image that runs identically anywhere a container engine exists. It solves the classic "works on my machine" problem.

Unlike a virtual machine, a container shares the host OS kernel and isolates only the process — so it starts in milliseconds and uses a fraction of the memory. That lightweight isolation is what makes containers ideal for microservices.

Aspect Container Virtual Machine
Isolation Process-level, shared kernel Full OS per VM
Startup Milliseconds Seconds to minutes
Size MBs GBs

Interview Gold Point

The key distinction: VMs virtualise hardware; containers virtualise the operating system. Containers are not "lightweight VMs" — they are isolated processes, which is why density and speed are so much higher.

Q2. What Is the Difference Between an Image and a Container?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

An image is a read-only template (the blueprint) built from a Dockerfile. A container is a running instance of an image — you can start many containers from one image, just as you create many objects from one class.

Interview Favorite

The class-vs-object analogy lands well: image = class, container = object. Images are immutable and versioned by tag; containers are ephemeral and should be treated as disposable (any state worth keeping goes in a volume or external store).

Q3. Walk Through a Dockerfile for a Spring Boot App

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

A Dockerfile is the recipe for building an image. Each instruction describes a step: 

FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre        # base image (Java 21 runtime)
WORKDIR /app                       # working directory inside image
COPY target/app.jar app.jar        # copy the built jar in
EXPOSE 8080                        # document the port
ENTRYPOINT ["java","-jar","app.jar"]  # command run on start

Interview Gold Point

Know the difference between ENTRYPOINT and CMD: ENTRYPOINT sets the fixed executable, CMD provides default arguments that are easy to override at docker run. Also use a slim jre base, not the full jdk, for a smaller production image.

Q4. What Is a Multi-Stage Build and Why Use It?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

A multi-stage build uses one stage to compile the app (with the full JDK and build tools) and a second, clean stage that copies only the resulting artifact into a tiny runtime image. The heavy build tooling never ships to production. 

# Stage 1: build
FROM maven:3.9-eclipse-temurin-21 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN mvn -q clean package -DskipTests

# Stage 2: runtime (small)
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=build /src/target/app.jar app.jar
ENTRYPOINT ["java","-jar","app.jar"]

Interview Favorite

The payoff is a dramatically smaller, more secure final image — less attack surface, faster pulls, no compilers or source code shipped to production. This is a standard senior-level best practice.

Q5. How Do Docker Image Layers and Caching Work?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Each Dockerfile instruction creates a cached layer. On rebuild, Docker reuses cached layers up to the first instruction that changed, then rebuilds everything after it. Ordering instructions from least- to most-frequently-changed maximises cache hits.

Interview Gold Point

The practical tip: copy your dependency descriptor (pom.xml) and download dependencies before copying source code. Then a code change reuses the cached dependency layer instead of re-downloading the world — much faster builds.

Q6. What Is docker-compose?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

docker-compose defines and runs multi-container applications from a single YAML file — for example your Spring Boot service plus a Postgres database and Redis — started together with one command (docker compose up).

Interview Favorite

Compose is ideal for local development and integration tests (it pairs naturally with Testcontainers). It is not a production orchestrator — for production scale and self-healing you move to Kubernetes.

SECTION 2 - KUBERNETES

Q7. What Is Kubernetes and What Problem Does It Solve?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestrator. Once you have many containers across many machines, you need something to schedule them, restart failed ones, scale them up and down, roll out new versions safely, and route traffic to them. K8s does all of this declaratively.

You describe the desired state ("I want 3 replicas of this image") and Kubernetes continuously works to make reality match — restarting crashed pods and rescheduling them if a node dies. This self-healing is its core value.

Interview Gold Point

The phrase to use is "declarative desired-state reconciliation". You do not tell K8s how to do things step by step; you declare the end state and its control loop converges toward it.

Q8. Explain Pod vs Deployment vs Service

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Object Role
Pod Smallest unit — one or more containers sharing network/storage
Deployment Manages a set of identical pods: replicas, rollouts, self-healing
Service Stable network endpoint + load balancing across the pods

Pods are ephemeral and get new IPs when recreated, so you never talk to a pod directly. A Service gives a stable DNS name and load-balances across whichever pods are currently healthy.

Interview Favorite

A common follow-up: "why not just run a container directly?" — because a bare pod is not recreated if it dies. A Deployment guarantees the declared number of replicas and handles rolling updates and rollbacks.

Q9. Liveness vs Readiness Probes

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Both are health checks, but they trigger different actions:

  • Liveness probe — "is the app alive?" If it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container (recovers from deadlocks/hangs).
  • Readiness probe — "is the app ready to serve traffic?" If it fails, K8s stops routing requests to that pod but does not restart it.

Interview Gold Point

Spring Boot Actuator exposes /actuator/health/liveness and /actuator/health/readiness out of the box — tie this straight back to the Actuator content in your Spring Boot chapter. Getting the two probes mixed up causes restart loops or traffic sent to a not-yet-ready app, so the distinction matters in production.

Q10. What Are ConfigMaps and Secrets?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Both externalise configuration out of the image so the same image runs in every environment. A ConfigMap holds non-sensitive config (URLs, feature flags). A Secret holds sensitive data (passwords, API keys, tokens) and is handled more carefully.

Interview Favorite

Be honest about Secrets: by default they are only base64-encoded, not encrypted. Production setups enable encryption-at-rest and often integrate an external vault (e.g. HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Saying this shows real-world awareness.

Q11. How Does a Rolling Update Work?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

A rolling update replaces old pods with new ones gradually — spinning up new-version pods, waiting for their readiness probes to pass, then terminating old pods a few at a time. The service stays available throughout, and if the new version is unhealthy you can roll back.

Interview Gold Point

This is why readiness probes matter: K8s only shifts traffic to a new pod once it reports ready, guaranteeing zero-downtime deploys. kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous version if something goes wrong.

Q12. Rolling vs Blue-Green vs Canary Deployments

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Strategy How It Works
Rolling Gradually replace old pods with new ones
Blue-Green Run two full environments; switch traffic all at once
Canary Send a small % of traffic to the new version, then ramp up

Blue-green gives instant rollback (just switch back) at the cost of double the infrastructure. Canary limits blast radius by exposing the new version to a few users first and watching metrics before a full rollout.

Interview Favorite

Match the strategy to risk: canary for high-risk changes where you want real-traffic validation, blue-green when you need an instant, clean cutover, rolling as the sensible low-cost default.

SECTION 3 - CI/CD & BUILD

Q13. What Is CI/CD?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

CI (Continuous Integration) means every code push is automatically built and tested, so integration problems surface within minutes instead of at a painful end-of-sprint merge. CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) automates releasing that validated build to environments — delivery stops at a manual approval, deployment goes all the way to production automatically.

Typical Pipeline Stages

Commit -> Build -> Unit Tests -> Package (Docker image)
       -> Integration Tests -> Push to Registry
       -> Deploy to Staging -> Deploy to Production

Interview Gold Point

Name tools you have touched — Jenkins, GitHub Actions, GitLab CI. The core value to articulate: fast feedback, repeatable releases, and smaller, safer changes shipped frequently rather than big risky batches.

Q14. What Is the 12-Factor App / Externalised Configuration?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Medium-High

The 12-Factor App is a set of principles for building cloud-native services. The one interviewers probe most is config in the environment: never hard-code environment-specific values (DB URLs, credentials) in the build — inject them at runtime via environment variables or config services.

This is what lets the same immutable image run in dev, staging and production, differing only by injected config. Other key factors: stateless processes, treat backing services as attached resources, and disposability (fast startup/graceful shutdown).

Interview Gold Point

Connect to Spring: profiles and application.yml overridden by environment variables, or Spring Cloud Config for centralised config, are exactly this principle in practice. "Build once, deploy anywhere with injected config" is the phrase to land.

Q15. Maven vs Gradle

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium

Aspect Maven Gradle
Config XML (pom.xml), declarative Groovy/Kotlin DSL, flexible
Speed Slower Faster (incremental + build cache)
Learning curve Simpler, rigid lifecycle Steeper, more powerful

Both manage dependencies and the build lifecycle. Maven's rigid, convention-driven lifecycle (validate → compile → test → package → install → deploy) is predictable; Gradle trades some predictability for speed and scripting flexibility.

Interview Favorite

Know dependency scopes (Maven) / configurations (Gradle): compile/implementation, provided/compileOnly, test, runtime. A frequent follow-up is how transitive dependency conflicts are resolved and how to exclude a transitive dependency.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

  • Container = isolated process (shares kernel); VM = full OS. Image = class, container = object
  • Dockerfile builds an image; ENTRYPOINT (fixed) vs CMD (overridable args)
  • Multi-stage build → small, secure runtime image (no build tools shipped)
  • Order Dockerfile steps for layer cache hits; copy deps before source
  • docker-compose = local multi-container; Kubernetes = production orchestration
  • K8s = declarative desired-state reconciliation + self-healing
  • Pod (smallest unit) < Deployment (replicas/rollout) + Service (stable endpoint)
  • Liveness probe restarts; readiness probe gates traffic
  • ConfigMap = config, Secret = sensitive (base64, not encrypted by default)
  • Deploys: rolling (default), blue-green (instant switch), canary (gradual %)
  • CI = auto build+test on push; CD = automated release pipeline
  • 12-factor: config in the environment → build once, deploy anywhere

20 - CLOUD & AWS INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

Almost every backend role today runs in the cloud, and AWS is the most common platform you will be asked about. For a 4-year Senior Java engineer, interviewers are not testing whether you can recite the AWS certification syllabus — they want to know that you understand the building blocks, can pick the right service for a problem, and appreciate the trade-offs in cost, availability and security. The bar is "can this person design and operate a Spring Boot service in production on AWS without making naive mistakes". Expect questions that connect cloud concepts to things you already know — caching, databases, messaging and deployment. For a Senior backend role you should confidently discuss: * The cloud service models (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS) and the Shared Responsibility Model * Compute choices: EC2 vs containers (ECS/EKS/Fargate) vs Lambda * Storage and databases: S3, EBS/EFS, RDS, DynamoDB, ElastiCache * Decoupling with SQS, SNS and EventBridge * Networking and security basics: VPC, security groups, IAM least privilege, secrets * Operating at scale: CloudWatch, Auto Scaling, load balancers and cost awareness They usually continue with a scenario like "you have a Spring Boot API that is getting slow under load — how would you scale it on AWS?", so be ready to reason out loud across compute, caching, the database tier and load balancing.

TOP ROI CLOUD QUESTIONS

  1. What Is Cloud Computing and Why Use It?
  2. IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS
  3. Regions, Availability Zones & the Shared Responsibility Model
  4. What Is EC2 and When Do You Use It?
  5. ECS vs EKS vs Fargate
  6. AWS Lambda / Serverless and Cold Starts
  7. How Would You Deploy a Spring Boot App on AWS?
  8. What Is S3 and When Should You NOT Store Data in a Database?
  9. EBS vs EFS vs S3
  10. RDS and Read Replicas
  11. DynamoDB — When to Use NoSQL
  12. ElastiCache (Redis) and Caching Strategy
  13. SQS — Visibility Timeout and Dead-Letter Queues
  14. SNS vs SQS vs EventBridge
  15. IAM, Least Privilege and Secrets Management
  16. CloudWatch, Auto Scaling and Load Balancers (ALB vs NLB)

SECTION 1 - CLOUD FUNDAMENTALS

Q1. What Is Cloud Computing and Why Use It?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Cloud computing is renting compute, storage, networking and managed services on demand over the internet, instead of buying and running your own data centre. You pay for what you use and can provision or release capacity in minutes through an API.

The real wins for a backend team are elasticity (scale up for Black Friday, scale down at night), no capital expenditure (turn fixed CapEx into variable OpEx), speed (a new environment in minutes, not weeks of hardware procurement), and managed services that remove undifferentiated heavy lifting — you let AWS run the database, the message queue and the patching while you focus on business logic.

Interview Gold Point

The mature framing is "use managed services so the team spends its time on the product, not on operating infrastructure". Naming the trade-off — you give up some control and risk vendor lock-in in exchange for velocity — signals seniority far more than just listing benefits.

Q2. Explain IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

They are layers of abstraction describing how much the provider manages versus how much you manage. As you move from IaaS to SaaS, you give up control but also give up operational burden.

Model You Manage Provider Manages AWS Example
IaaS OS, runtime, app, data Hardware, virtualisation, networking EC2, EBS, VPC
PaaS App code and data only OS, runtime, scaling, patching Elastic Beanstalk, RDS, Lambda
SaaS Just configuration / your data Everything else Gmail, Salesforce, Cognito

Interview Favorite

A clean analogy: IaaS is leasing an empty kitchen, PaaS is a kitchen with the appliances installed, SaaS is ordering a finished meal. Note that real architectures mix all three — e.g. your Spring Boot app on Fargate (PaaS-ish), data in RDS (PaaS), auth via Cognito (SaaS).

Q3. What Are Regions, Availability Zones and the Shared Responsibility Model?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

A Region is a geographic area (e.g. eu-west-1, Ireland). Each Region contains multiple Availability Zones (AZs) — physically separate data centres with independent power and networking, connected by low-latency links. You deploy across multiple AZs so that the failure of one data centre does not take your service down.

The Shared Responsibility Model defines who secures what: AWS is responsible for security of the cloud (the physical hardware, the hypervisor, the managed-service internals), while you are responsible for security in the cloud (your OS patches on EC2, IAM policies, security group rules, encryption settings, and your application code).

Why It Matters in Design

  • Multi-AZ = high availability within a Region (RDS Multi-AZ failover, ASG spanning AZs).
  • Multi-Region = disaster recovery and lower latency for global users, at higher cost and complexity.
  • Data residency and compliance (e.g. GDPR) often dictate which Region you must run in.

Common Mistake

Candidates assume "it's in the cloud, so AWS secures it". AWS secures the data centre, but a public S3 bucket or an over-broad IAM policy is your mistake. The line shifts with the service: on EC2 you patch the OS; on Lambda or RDS, AWS does — but you always own IAM and your data.

SECTION 2 - COMPUTE

Q4. What Is EC2 and When Do You Use It?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud) gives you virtual machines — you pick an instance type (CPU/memory profile), an AMI (the OS image), and you are responsible for patching, the runtime and the app. It is the IaaS workhorse: maximum control and flexibility.

Instance families are tuned for workloads: t (burstable, cheap, dev/low-traffic), m (general purpose, balanced), c (compute-optimised, CPU-heavy), r (memory-optimised, in-memory caches/JVM heaps). Pricing models: On-Demand (flexible, pay per second), Reserved/Savings Plans (1–3 year commit, big discount for steady load), and Spot (up to ~90% off but can be reclaimed — good for fault-tolerant batch work).

When to Use vs Avoid

  • Use EC2 when you need full control of the OS, special networking, GPUs, or you are lifting-and-shifting a legacy app.
  • Prefer containers/serverless for new stateless services — you usually do not want to be hand-patching VMs in 2026.

Interview Gold Point

Mention the pricing trade-off proactively: "steady baseline on Savings Plans, bursty traffic on On-Demand, and Spot for the batch tier". Cost-awareness is a strong senior signal — see also Q16 on right-sizing.

Q5. ECS vs EKS vs Fargate

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

All three run containers (this ties directly to the Docker/Kubernetes chapter). The choice is about orchestration engine and how much infrastructure you manage:

Service What It Is Best For
ECS AWS-native container orchestrator, simpler than K8s Teams all-in on AWS who want low operational overhead
EKS Managed Kubernetes control plane Teams that need the K8s ecosystem or multi-cloud portability
Fargate Serverless launch type for ECS or EKS — no servers to manage Not wanting to manage EC2 worker nodes at all

The key insight: Fargate is not a separate orchestrator — it is a compute mode. You can run ECS or EKS on EC2 nodes (you manage and patch the nodes) or on Fargate (AWS runs the nodes; you just declare CPU/memory per task). Fargate trades a little per-unit cost for zero node management.

Interview Favorite

A crisp summary: "ECS for AWS-native simplicity, EKS when you specifically need Kubernetes, and Fargate on top of either when you do not want to manage worker nodes." If a candidate already knows K8s from the DevOps chapter, EKS is just managed K8s — the concepts (Pods, Deployments, Services) carry straight over.

Q6. What Is AWS Lambda / Serverless, and What About Cold Starts?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

Lambda runs your code in response to events (an HTTP request via API Gateway, an S3 upload, an SQS message) with no servers to manage. You are billed per invocation and per millisecond of execution. It scales automatically from zero to thousands of concurrent executions.

A cold start is the latency penalty the first time a function runs after being idle — AWS has to provision an execution environment and initialise your runtime. For the JVM this hurts: class loading and JIT warm-up can add hundreds of milliseconds to seconds. Mitigations: Provisioned Concurrency (keep warm instances ready), smaller deployment packages, GraalVM native images (e.g. Spring Native / Quarkus), or simply choosing a lighter runtime for latency-critical paths.

When to Use Lambda

  • Good fit: spiky or unpredictable traffic, event-driven glue (process an uploaded file, react to a queue), cron jobs, low-to-moderate steady volume.
  • Poor fit: long-running or very high-throughput workloads (the per-request cost overtakes a steady container), latency-critical APIs where cold starts are unacceptable, or anything needing >15 min execution.

Common Mistake

Saying "serverless is always cheaper". It is cheaper for low/spiky usage but a steady, high-volume service is often cheaper on Fargate or EC2. The honest senior answer weighs request volume, the cold-start sensitivity of the JVM, and operational simplicity — not a blanket rule.

Q7. How Would You Deploy a Spring Boot App on AWS?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Short Answer

There is no single right answer — the senior move is to give the spectrum and justify a default. Pick based on team maturity and traffic shape:

  • Elastic Beanstalk — upload the JAR, AWS provisions EC2 + load balancer + auto scaling. Fastest path; least control.
  • Container on ECS Fargate (my usual default) — build a Docker image, push to ECR, run it as a Fargate service behind an ALB with an Auto Scaling policy. No servers to patch, clean CI/CD story.
  • EKS — if the org already standardises on Kubernetes.
  • EC2 + ASG — when you need full OS control.

A Concrete Fargate Flow

The pipeline ties the Docker chapter and this one together: 

1. Build a Docker image (multi-stage Dockerfile, slim JRE base)
2. Push it to ECR (Elastic Container Registry)
3. ECS Fargate Service runs N tasks across multiple AZs
4. An Application Load Balancer fronts the tasks (HTTPS via ACM cert)
5. Target Auto Scaling on CPU / request count
6. Config via environment variables; secrets from Secrets Manager
7. RDS (Multi-AZ) for the database, ElastiCache for caching
8. Logs and metrics to CloudWatch; alarms on error rate / latency

Health checks matter: expose Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/health and point the ALB target group and ECS health check at it — this mirrors the readiness probe idea from Kubernetes.

Interview Gold Point

Externalise everything (12-factor): build the image once, then inject config per environment via env vars and pull secrets from Secrets Manager at runtime. Never bake DB passwords into the image. That single point connects deployment, security and the 12-factor mindset from the DevOps chapter.

SECTION 3 - STORAGE & DATABASES

Q8. What Is S3, and When Should You NOT Store Data in a Database?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

S3 (Simple Storage Service) is object storage: you store files ("objects") in "buckets", addressed by a key, accessed over HTTP. It is virtually unlimited, durable (11 nines — 99.999999999%), and cheap. It is not a filesystem and not a block device — you cannot mount it and update a file in place; you PUT and GET whole objects.

The rule: do not store large binary blobs in a relational database. Images, videos, PDFs, user uploads, backups and logs belong in S3. In the DB you store only the S3 key / URL plus metadata (owner, size, content-type, timestamps). Putting blobs in the DB bloats backups, kills query performance, and wastes expensive transactional storage.

Useful S3 Features

  • Storage classes — Standard, Infrequent Access, Glacier (archival) for cost tiering.
  • Lifecycle policies — auto-transition old objects to cheaper tiers or delete them.
  • Pre-signed URLs — let a client upload/download directly to S3 with a time-limited, scoped URL, so file bytes never pass through your app servers.
  • Versioning + encryption (SSE) — protect against accidental deletes and secure data at rest.

Interview Gold Point

Pre-signed URLs are a favourite follow-up: "how do you let a user upload a 2 GB video without it touching your Spring Boot servers?" Answer: the backend generates a pre-signed PUT URL, the browser uploads directly to S3, then notifies your API of the resulting key. Offloads bandwidth and scales effortlessly.

Q9. EBS vs EFS vs S3

ROI: ★★★☆☆ Frequency: Medium-High

Short Answer

These are the three AWS storage shapes — block, file and object. Picking the right one is a common scenario question.

Type Service Attaches To Use Case
Block EBS One EC2 instance (one AZ) OS disk, database storage — like a hard drive
File EFS Many EC2 instances (NFS, multi-AZ) Shared filesystem across servers
Object S3 Anything over HTTP Files, media, backups, static assets

Rule of thumb: EBS = a disk for one machine; EFS = a shared network drive for many machines; S3 = an API for blobs at internet scale. EBS volumes are AZ-bound, so a Multi-AZ design relies on snapshots or replication, not on the volume itself moving.

Interview Favorite

"Block vs file vs object" in one breath wins points. If asked where a self-managed database's data files go, the answer is EBS (low-latency block storage) — never EFS for a high-throughput OLTP database.

Q10. What Is RDS, and How Do Read Replicas Help?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: Very High

Short Answer

RDS (Relational Database Service) is managed relational databases — PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, plus Aurora. AWS handles provisioning, patching, backups, and failover, so you operate it through configuration rather than running it on EC2 yourself. The schema, queries and indexing (the SQL chapter material) are still entirely your responsibility.

Two features matter for interviews and they solve different problems:

  • Multi-AZ — a synchronous standby in another AZ for high availability. If the primary fails, RDS automatically fails over. The standby does not serve reads; it is purely for resilience.
  • Read Replicas — asynchronous copies for read scaling. You route read-heavy traffic (reports, dashboards) to replicas to offload the primary. Because replication is async, replicas can lag — so they are eventually consistent.

Common Mistake

Confusing Multi-AZ with read replicas. Multi-AZ = availability (standby, no reads); read replicas = scaling reads (lag allowed). A read replica is not a failover target in the same automatic way, and the standby is not a query target. Knowing the distinction is a classic senior differentiator.

Q11. What Is DynamoDB, and When Do You Choose NoSQL?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

DynamoDB is AWS's fully managed key-value / document NoSQL database. It offers single-digit-millisecond latency at essentially unlimited scale, with no servers to manage and automatic partitioning. You design around access patterns, not around a normalised schema — you pick a partition key (and optional sort key) so your queries hit a single partition.

Choose DynamoDB when you need predictable low latency at massive scale, a flexible schema, and your queries are known and key-based — e.g. user sessions, shopping carts, IoT events, leaderboards. Choose RDS when you need complex joins, ad-hoc queries, strong relational integrity and ACID transactions across many tables.

Aspect DynamoDB (NoSQL) RDS (Relational)
Schema Flexible, schema-less Fixed, normalised
Scaling Horizontal, near-unlimited Vertical + read replicas
Queries Key-based; joins are an anti-pattern Rich SQL, joins, aggregations
Best for Known access patterns, huge scale Complex relationships, ad-hoc reporting

Interview Gold Point

The senior framing: "with DynamoDB you model the data to fit the query, not the query to fit the data." If you cannot define your access patterns up front, that is a signal you probably want a relational database. Avoid the trap of treating DynamoDB like SQL — scanning or joining at scale is exactly what it is bad at.

Q12. What Is ElastiCache (Redis) and How Does It Fit Your Caching Strategy?

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

ElastiCache is managed in-memory caching, most commonly Redis (also Memcached). It sits between your application and the database to serve hot data with sub-millisecond latency, reducing load on RDS and cutting response times — this is the System Design caching layer made concrete on AWS.

The most common pattern is cache-aside (lazy loading): the app checks the cache first; on a miss it reads the DB, writes the result into the cache with a TTL, then returns it. Redis also serves distributed sessions, rate-limiting counters, leaderboards, and distributed locks. 

// Cache-aside in a Spring service
public Product getProduct(String id) {
    Product cached = redis.get("product:" + id);
    if (cached != null) {
        return cached;                  // cache hit
    }
    Product p = repository.findById(id) // cache miss -> DB
                          .orElseThrow();
    redis.set("product:" + id, p, Duration.ofMinutes(10)); // TTL
    return p;
}

Interview Favorite

Be ready for the hard parts of caching: invalidation (set sensible TTLs, evict on write), stampede (many misses hammering the DB at once — mitigate with locks or request coalescing), and consistency (the cache can serve stale data). "There are only two hard things: cache invalidation and naming things" usually earns a smile and shows you respect the trade-offs.

SECTION 4 - MESSAGING & INTEGRATION

Q13. What Is SQS, and Explain Visibility Timeout and Dead-Letter Queues

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

SQS (Simple Queue Service) is a fully managed message queue used to decouple producers from consumers. A producer drops a message on the queue; one or more workers poll and process it. This smooths traffic spikes (the queue acts as a buffer) and lets a slow downstream task run asynchronously — the same async decoupling theme from the microservices chapter.

Two concepts always come up:

  • Visibility Timeout — when a consumer receives a message, it becomes invisible to other consumers for a set period. The consumer must delete the message after successful processing; if it crashes or the timeout expires first, the message reappears and is retried. This is what gives at-least-once delivery — so consumers must be idempotent.
  • Dead-Letter Queue (DLQ) — after a message fails processing N times (the maxReceiveCount), SQS moves it to a separate DLQ so it stops blocking the main queue. You inspect, alert on, and replay DLQ messages.

Queue types: Standard (high throughput, at-least-once, best-effort ordering) and FIFO (exactly-once processing and strict ordering, lower throughput).

Interview Gold Point

The line that lands: "SQS is at-least-once, so I design consumers to be idempotent — e.g. dedupe on a business key or message ID — and I always configure a DLQ so poison messages do not block the queue." That single sentence shows production scars.

Q14. SNS vs SQS vs EventBridge

ROI: ★★★★☆ Frequency: High

Short Answer

All three move messages between components, but the delivery model differs:

Service Model Use Case
SQS Queue (1 message → 1 consumer group, pull) Decouple and buffer work for async processing
SNS Pub/sub fan-out (1 message → many subscribers, push) Broadcast an event to multiple endpoints at once
EventBridge Event bus with content-based routing rules Event-driven architectures, SaaS & AWS event integration

A classic combination is the SNS-to-SQS fan-out: publish one event to an SNS topic, which fans it out to several SQS queues — each downstream service consumes from its own queue at its own pace, and each queue can have its own DLQ. EventBridge goes further with rich rule-based filtering and routing, and built-in integration with dozens of AWS and third-party SaaS event sources.

Interview Favorite

The crisp distinction: SQS is pull and point-to-point; SNS is push and one-to-many; EventBridge is a smart router for event-driven systems. When asked "notify the billing, email and analytics services when an order is placed", the textbook answer is SNS fan-out into three SQS queues (or EventBridge rules).

SECTION 5 - NETWORKING & SECURITY

Q15. Explain VPC, Security Groups, IAM and Secrets Management

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

Networking Basics (VPC)

A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is your private, isolated network in AWS. You carve it into subnets: public subnets (routed to an Internet Gateway — for load balancers) and private subnets (no direct internet inbound — for app servers and databases). A typical layout puts the ALB in public subnets, the Spring Boot tasks and RDS in private subnets, so the database is never reachable from the internet.

Security Groups are stateful virtual firewalls attached to instances/ENIs — you allow traffic by port and source (an IP range or, better, another security group). "Stateful" means a reply to an allowed inbound request is automatically allowed out. A good pattern: the ALB's SG allows 443 from the world; the app SG allows 8080 only from the ALB's SG; the RDS SG allows 5432 only from the app SG.

IAM — Identity and Access

  • Users — long-lived identities for humans (avoid for app workloads).
  • Roles — assumable identities with temporary, auto-rotated credentials — the right way for services to get permissions.
  • Policies — JSON documents granting/denying specific actions on specific resources.
  • Least privilege — grant only the permissions actually needed, nothing more.

The big one: use instance/task roles, not access keys. Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance or ECS task; the AWS SDK in your Spring Boot app then receives temporary credentials automatically — nothing to store, nothing to leak, and they rotate on their own. Hard-coded access keys in code or env files are how breaches happen.

Secrets Manager vs Parameter Store

Never bake DB passwords or API keys into images or source. Use Secrets Manager (built for secrets — supports automatic rotation, e.g. of RDS credentials) or SSM Parameter Store (great for general config; SecureString parameters cover secrets cheaply). The app reads them at startup using its IAM role — this is the AWS realisation of the 12-factor "config in the environment" rule from the DevOps chapter.

Interview Gold Point

Two phrases signal seniority instantly: "least privilege" and "use IAM roles, not long-lived access keys". Add "the database lives in a private subnet, reachable only from the app's security group" and you have demonstrated defence in depth across network and identity layers.

SECTION 6 - OPERATIONS & SCALING

Q16. Explain CloudWatch, Auto Scaling, Load Balancers (ALB vs NLB) and Cost Right-Sizing

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Very High

CloudWatch — Observability

CloudWatch is the AWS monitoring backbone: metrics (CPU, memory, request count, latency), logs (your app logs streamed to log groups), and alarms that fire when a metric breaches a threshold (e.g. p99 latency > 500 ms or 5xx rate too high). Alarms can page you via SNS or trigger an Auto Scaling action. Point your Spring Boot Micrometer/Actuator metrics at CloudWatch for end-to-end visibility.

Auto Scaling Groups

An Auto Scaling Group (ASG) keeps a desired number of instances running across multiple AZs and replaces unhealthy ones. With a target tracking policy you say "keep average CPU at 50%" and AWS adds/removes instances automatically. This gives elasticity (scale out under load, in when quiet, saving money) and self-healing (a failed instance is replaced) — for containers, ECS/EKS provide the equivalent service auto scaling.

Load Balancers — ALB vs NLB

Aspect ALB (Application LB) NLB (Network LB)
OSI Layer Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) Layer 4 (TCP/UDP)
Routing Path/host-based, headers, WebSockets Pure connection forwarding
Performance Rich features, slight overhead Ultra-low latency, millions of req/s
Use For Web apps & REST APIs (the usual choice) Extreme throughput, non-HTTP, static IP

For a Spring Boot REST API, the answer is almost always an ALB — it does HTTP routing, TLS termination, and health checks against /actuator/health. Reach for an NLB only for raw TCP/UDP, extreme throughput, or when you need a fixed static IP.

Cost & Right-Sizing

  • Right-size — use CloudWatch data to drop oversized instances; do not run an m5.4xlarge at 10% CPU.
  • Buy smart — Savings Plans/Reserved for steady baseline, Spot for fault-tolerant batch.
  • Scale to demand — auto scaling so you are not paying for peak capacity 24/7.
  • Tier storage — S3 lifecycle policies to Glacier; delete unattached EBS volumes and old snapshots.

Interview Gold Point

Tying scaling to cost is the senior signal: "I size from CloudWatch metrics, auto scale to demand rather than provisioning for peak, and commit Savings Plans only for the steady baseline." Engineers who talk about the bill — not just availability — stand out, because in the cloud architecture decisions are cost decisions.

QUICK REVISION SHEET

  • IaaS (EC2, you manage OS) < PaaS (RDS/Lambda, manage app only) < SaaS (use it). Mix all three.
  • Region = geography; AZ = isolated data centre. Deploy Multi-AZ for HA. Shared Responsibility: AWS secures of the cloud, you secure in the cloud.
  • Compute ladder: EC2 (control) → ECS/EKS (containers) → Fargate (no nodes) → Lambda (no servers, watch JVM cold starts).
  • Default Spring Boot deploy: Docker image → ECR → ECS Fargate behind an ALB, RDS + ElastiCache, secrets from Secrets Manager.
  • S3 = object storage (11 nines); store blobs in S3, keep only the key in the DB. Pre-signed URLs for direct client uploads.
  • EBS = disk for one instance; EFS = shared network drive; S3 = blobs over HTTP.
  • RDS Multi-AZ = availability (standby, no reads); read replicas = read scaling (async, can lag). Do not confuse them.
  • DynamoDB = key-value NoSQL at scale; model data to fit the query. RDS for joins, ad-hoc queries and ACID.
  • ElastiCache (Redis) = cache-aside with TTLs; mind invalidation, stampede and staleness.
  • SQS = pull queue, at-least-once → idempotent consumers + DLQ; visibility timeout protects in-flight messages.
  • SNS = push fan-out; EventBridge = rule-based event router. Classic pattern: SNS → multiple SQS queues.
  • Security: VPC with public/private subnets, stateful security groups chained, IAM least privilege, instance/task roles — not access keys.
  • Ops: CloudWatch metrics/logs/alarms, Auto Scaling Groups for elasticity + self-healing, ALB (L7) for APIs / NLB (L4) for raw throughput.
  • Cost: right-size from metrics, scale to demand, Savings Plans for baseline + Spot for batch, lifecycle-tier storage.

24 - GIT & VERSION CONTROL INTERVIEW HANDBOOK

Every backend engineer uses Git daily, yet very few can explain what actually happens under the hood or recover gracefully when history goes wrong. Interviewers use Git questions as a quick proxy for how you collaborate, how disciplined your commit history is, and whether you can be trusted with a shared repository. For a 4-year Senior role, nobody expects you to recite man pages. They expect you to discuss Git the way a teammate would: * How you keep a branch's history clean and reviewable * When you reach for merge vs rebase, and why you never rebase shared history * How you recover from a bad commit, a lost branch, or a leaked secret * Which branching strategy fits a given team and release cadence * How a pull request and code review workflow actually flows day to day This chapter focuses on the mental model and the judgement calls, with the commands you are most likely to be asked to explain or run live. They usually continue with a "show me how you'd undo this" scenario, so be ready to reason about reset, revert and reflog out loud.

TOP ROI GIT QUESTIONS

  1. What is Git and how does a distributed VCS differ from a centralized one?
  2. Explain the three areas: working directory, staging area, and repository.
  3. What is a commit really? What does it store?
  4. What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
  5. What is a branch in Git, and why is it so cheap?
  6. Merge vs rebase: what is the difference and when do you use each?
  7. Fast-forward vs three-way merge — what is the difference?
  8. How do you resolve a merge conflict?
  9. Explain git reset --soft, --mixed, --hard versus git revert.
  10. How do you squash commits and use interactive rebase?
  11. What makes a good commit message, and how do tags / semantic versioning work?
  12. Compare Git Flow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development.
  13. Walk me through a pull request and code review workflow.
  14. How do you undo common mistakes and recover with reflog?
