When a thread enters a synchronized method or block it acquires that monitor, and any other thread attempting to acquire the same monitor blocks until it is released. The lock is reentrant, so the same thread can re-enter synchronized code guarded by a lock it already holds without deadlocking itself.

Q44. Static Synchronization

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Locks the Class object instead of an instance.

Because the lock is held on the single Class object rather than on a per-instance basis, a static synchronized method serializes access across all instances of that class, not just one. This matters when the shared state being protected is itself static. A common trap is mixing instance and static synchronization on the same data, since they use different monitors and therefore do not mutually exclude each other. 


synchronized static method

SECTION 8 - VOLATILE

Q45. What Is volatile?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

volatile ensures visibility of changes across threads.

When one thread updates a volatile variable, other threads immediately see the updated value.

Q46. Why Is volatile Needed?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Problem

Threads may cache variables locally. Updates may not become visible immediately.

volatile Solution

Forces reads and writes from main memory.

Q47. Does volatile Provide Thread Safety?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Answer

No. volatile provides visibility. It does not provide atomicity.

Example


count++

Still unsafe.

Q48. volatile vs synchronized

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

volatile synchronized
Visibility Visibility + Atomicity
Lightweight Heavier
No Locking Uses Locking

SECTION 9 - ATOMIC CLASSES

Q49. Why Atomic Classes?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Provide thread-safe operations without traditional synchronization.

Q50. Common Atomic Classes

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

  • AtomicInteger
  • AtomicLong
  • AtomicBoolean
  • AtomicReference
Q51. What Is CAS?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Full Form

Compare And Swap

Interview Ready Answer

CAS updates a value only if it still matches the expected value. 


Current Value

↓

Expected?

↓

Update

No locking required.

Q52. AtomicInteger vs synchronized Counter

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Answer

AtomicInteger usually performs better under moderate contention because it avoids heavyweight locking.

Internally it relies on a lock-free Compare-And-Swap loop rather than blocking the thread, so there is no context switching or lock acquisition cost. Under very high contention the CAS retries can themselves become wasteful, but for the common case of incrementing a counter it is both faster and simpler than a synchronized block. Use it when the operation is a single atomic update; for compound operations across multiple variables you still need explicit locking.

SECTION 10 - DEADLOCKS

Q53. What Is Deadlock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Deadlock occurs when two or more threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other.

Q54. Deadlock Example

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High 


Thread A

Lock 1

↓

Waiting Lock 2

Thread B

Lock 2

↓

Waiting Lock 1

Neither thread can proceed.

Q55. How To Prevent Deadlocks?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

  • Consistent Lock Ordering
  • Timeouts
  • Reduce Lock Scope
  • Avoid Nested Locks
Q56. How To Investigate Deadlock In Production?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Strong Answer

  1. Capture Thread Dump
  2. Analyze Blocked Threads
  3. Identify Lock Ownership
  4. Find Circular Dependencies
  5. Fix Locking Strategy

Tools


jstack

VisualVM

JConsole

PART 3 COMPLETE

Next Part: * Executor Framework * Thread Pools * Future * CompletableFuture * ForkJoinPool * Concurrent Collections * Producer Consumer Pattern * BlockingQueue * Java 8 Functional Programming * Streams API These topics are extremely common in senior Java interviews and often appear immediately after multithreading fundamentals.

SECTION 11 - EXECUTOR FRAMEWORK

Most modern Java applications do not create threads manually. Instead, they use thread pools managed by the Executor Framework. For 4+ years experience, interviewers expect practical knowledge here.

Q57. What Is Executor Framework?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

The Executor Framework provides a higher-level abstraction for managing and executing asynchronous tasks. Instead of manually creating threads: 


new Thread(...)

tasks are submitted to a thread pool.

Benefits

  • Thread Reuse
  • Better Performance
  • Resource Management
  • Simpler Concurrency
Q58. Why Not Create Threads Manually?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Thread creation is expensive. Creating thousands of threads can lead to:

  • Memory Pressure
  • Context Switching Overhead
  • Poor Performance
  • Resource Exhaustion

Thread pools solve this by reusing threads.

Q59. What Is ExecutorService?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

ExecutorService is the primary interface used to manage and execute tasks asynchronously.

Example


ExecutorService executor =
Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);

executor.submit(task);
Q60. Common Thread Pool Types

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Pool Type Use Case
FixedThreadPool Known Workload
CachedThreadPool Short Tasks
SingleThreadExecutor Sequential Tasks
ScheduledThreadPool Periodic Tasks
Q61. Fixed Thread Pool

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Maintains a fixed number of worker threads.

Example


newFixedThreadPool(10)

At most 10 threads execute simultaneously. Additional tasks wait in queue.

Q62. Cached Thread Pool Risk

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

CachedThreadPool can create unlimited threads.

Under heavy traffic it may exhaust system resources.

Production Advice

Use carefully. Bounded pools are often safer.

Q63. Difference Between submit() And execute()

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

submit() execute()
Returns Future No Return
Supports Callable Runnable Only

SECTION 12 - FUTURE & COMPLETABLEFUTURE

Q64. What Is Future?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation.

Example


Future

Result becomes available later.

Q65. Limitation Of Future

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

  • Cannot Chain Tasks Easily
  • Blocking get()
  • Limited Composition
Q66. What Is CompletableFuture?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

CompletableFuture is an enhanced Future introduced in Java 8. It supports:

  • Asynchronous Execution
  • Task Chaining
  • Combining Tasks
  • Exception Handling
Q67. Why CompletableFuture Is Popular?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

It enables non-blocking asynchronous programming.

Example


Fetch User

↓

Fetch Orders

↓

Combine Results

Without blocking threads unnecessarily.

Q68. thenApply() vs thenCompose()

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

thenApply thenCompose
Transforms Result Chains Async Operations
Map FlatMap

Senior Insight

thenCompose avoids nested CompletableFuture objects.

Q69. How To Handle Exceptions In CompletableFuture?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Common Methods

  • exceptionally()
  • handle()
  • whenComplete()
Q70. Production Example Of CompletableFuture

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Scenario


Get User

Get Orders

Get Notifications

Execute concurrently and combine results. This reduces overall response time.

SECTION 13 - CONCURRENT COLLECTIONS

Q71. Why Concurrent Collections?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Standard collections are generally not thread-safe. Concurrent collections support safe concurrent access.

Q72. Common Concurrent Collections

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

  • ConcurrentHashMap
  • CopyOnWriteArrayList
  • ConcurrentLinkedQueue
  • BlockingQueue
Q73. What Is CopyOnWriteArrayList?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Whenever modifications occur, a new copy of the array is created.

Good For

Read-heavy workloads.

Bad For

Frequent writes.

Q74. What Is BlockingQueue?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

A queue that blocks operations when necessary.

Examples


Producer

↓

BlockingQueue

↓

Consumer
Q75. Producer Consumer Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

One thread produces data. Another consumes data. 


Producer

↓

Queue

↓

Consumer

Real Examples

  • Kafka Consumers
  • Notification Systems
  • Order Processing

SECTION 14 - LOCKS

Q76. synchronized vs ReentrantLock

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

synchronized ReentrantLock
Simple Flexible
Implicit Lock Explicit Lock
No Timeout Supports Timeout
No Fairness Control Fair Lock Option
Q77. What Is ReentrantLock?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

A thread can acquire the same lock multiple times without deadlocking itself.

ReentrantLock keeps a hold count that increments on each acquire and decrements on each unlock, releasing the lock only when the count returns to zero. Beyond reentrancy it gives capabilities that intrinsic synchronized locks lack: tryLock with a timeout, interruptible lock acquisition, and an optional fairness policy. The trade-off is that you must release it in a finally block, otherwise a thrown exception can leave the lock permanently held.

Q78. ReadWriteLock

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

Allows multiple readers simultaneously. Only one writer at a time.

Useful For

Read-heavy systems.

Q79. Semaphore

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Controls access to a limited number of resources.

A Semaphore holds a fixed number of permits; a thread calls acquire to take a permit before using a resource and release to return it, blocking when no permits remain. Unlike a lock, permits are not tied to ownership, so one thread can acquire while another releases, which makes it well suited to bounding concurrency, such as throttling the number of simultaneous calls to a downstream service.

Example


Database Connection Pool

Max 10 Connections
Q80. CountDownLatch

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Allows one or more threads to wait until a set of operations completes.

It is initialized with a count; worker threads call countDown as they finish, and waiting threads block on await until the count reaches zero. The key limitation versus CyclicBarrier is that a CountDownLatch is one-shot, it cannot be reset and reused. It is ideal for coordinating a startup sequence or for fanning out several parallel calls and resuming only once all of them have returned.

Example


Wait For

Service A

Service B

Service C

↓

Continue

SECTION 15 - JAVA 8 FEATURES

Java 8 is one of the most frequently asked interview areas. Many interviewers use Java 8 questions to assess whether a candidate writes modern Java code or still relies entirely on pre-Java 8 approaches.

Q81. What Were The Major Features Introduced In Java 8?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

  • Lambda Expressions
  • Streams API
  • Functional Interfaces
  • Optional
  • Method References
  • Default Methods
  • Date Time API
  • CompletableFuture

Senior Insight

Java 8 introduced a more functional programming style while maintaining backward compatibility.

Q82. What Is A Lambda Expression?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

A lambda expression is a concise way to represent anonymous functions.

Before Java 8


new Runnable() {
    public void run() {
        ...
    }
}

Java 8


() -> { ... }

Benefits

  • Less Boilerplate
  • Readable Code
  • Functional Style
Q83. What Is A Functional Interface?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

A Functional Interface contains exactly one abstract method.

Examples


Runnable

Callable

Comparator

Consumer

Supplier

Function

Predicate

Annotation


@FunctionalInterface
Q84. Can Functional Interface Have Multiple Methods?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

It can have multiple default and static methods. It must have only one abstract method.

Q85. What Is Method Reference?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Method references provide a shorthand notation for lambda expressions.

Example


System.out::println

Instead of: 


x -> System.out.println(x)

SECTION 16 - STREAMS API

Q86. What Is Streams API?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Streams API allows processing collections declaratively.

Instead of manually iterating through collections, operations can be chained together.

Example


list.stream()
    .filter(...)
    .map(...)
    .collect(...)
Q87. Advantages Of Streams

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

  • Readable
  • Concise
  • Functional Style
  • Parallel Processing Support
Q88. Stream Pipeline

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer


Source

↓

Intermediate Operations

↓

Terminal Operation

Example


List

↓

filter()

↓

map()

↓

collect()
Q89. Intermediate Operations

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Examples

  • filter()
  • map()
  • sorted()
  • distinct()
  • limit()
Q90. Terminal Operations

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Examples

  • collect()
  • forEach()
  • count()
  • reduce()
  • findFirst()
Q91. filter() vs map()

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

filter() map()
Select Data Transform Data

Example


filter

Salary > 50000

map

Employee → EmployeeDTO
Q92. What Is flatMap()?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

flatMap transforms and flattens nested structures.

Example


List>

↓

List

Analogy

map() creates layers. flatMap() removes layers.

Q93. What Is reduce()?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

reduce combines elements into a single result.

It repeatedly applies a binary operator, carrying an accumulator across the stream so that each element is folded into the running result. The form taking an identity value is preferred because it gives a well-defined result for an empty stream and supports safe parallel execution, provided the operator is associative. When the goal is simply summing or counting, the specialized operations such as sum or count are clearer than a hand-written reduce.

Example


Sum

Maximum

Concatenation
Q94. What Is collect()?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

collect() is a terminal stream operation that gathers the elements of a stream into a mutable result container such as a List, Set or Map.

It works through the Collector interface, which defines how elements are supplied, accumulated and combined, and it is the primary mechanism used with the Collectors utility methods to reduce a stream into a concrete data structure.

Examples


List

Set

Map

Grouping
Q95. Common Collectors

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

  • toList()
  • toSet()
  • toMap()
  • groupingBy()
  • partitioningBy()
Q96. groupingBy Example

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Scenario


Employees

↓

Group By Department

Result


Map>
Q97. What Is Lazy Evaluation?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Intermediate stream operations are not executed until a terminal operation is invoked.

Example


filter()

map()

Nothing happens until: 


collect()

SECTION 17 - OPTIONAL

Q98. What Is Optional?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Optional is a container object that may or may not contain a value.

Purpose

Reduce NullPointerExceptions.

Q99. Why Optional?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Before


if(user != null)

After


optional.map(...)
Q100. Common Optional Methods

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

  • isPresent()
  • ifPresent()
  • orElse()
  • orElseGet()
  • orElseThrow()
  • map()
Q101. orElse() vs orElseGet()

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

orElse orElseGet
Always Evaluates Lazy Evaluation

Important

orElse() executes even when value exists. orElseGet() executes only when value is absent.

Q102. Should Optional Be Used In Entity Fields?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

Generally No.

Optional is primarily intended for return types. Using it inside entities often creates serialization and framework integration issues.

SECTION 18 - PARALLEL STREAMS

Q103. What Is Parallel Stream?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Parallel streams split work across multiple threads using ForkJoinPool.

Example


list.parallelStream()
Q104. Are Parallel Streams Always Faster?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Answer

No.

Performance depends on:

  • Dataset Size
  • CPU Cores
  • Task Complexity
  • Thread Overhead
Q105. When Would You Avoid Parallel Streams?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

  • Small Collections
  • Shared Mutable State
  • Blocking Operations
  • Database Calls

PART 5 COMPLETE

Next Part: * Object-Oriented Programming * SOLID Principles * Design Patterns * Immutable Objects * Serialization * Reflection * Annotations * JVM Deep Dive * Class Loading * Advanced Senior-Level Java Questions This is where many architecture and framework discussions begin.

SECTION 19 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING (OOP)

This section is deceptively important. Many senior interviewers start with Spring Boot and eventually come back to OOP fundamentals because Spring itself is built heavily around OOP principles.

Q106. What Is Object-Oriented Programming?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm that models software using objects containing state (data) and behavior (methods).

Main Goals

  • Reusability
  • Maintainability
  • Scalability
  • Encapsulation
Q107. Four Pillars Of OOP

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

  • Encapsulation
  • Inheritance
  • Polymorphism
  • Abstraction
Q108. Explain Encapsulation

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Encapsulation means hiding internal implementation details and exposing only necessary functionality.

Example


private fields

public getters/setters

Benefits

  • Data Protection
  • Better Maintainability
  • Reduced Coupling
Q109. Explain Abstraction

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Abstraction focuses on exposing essential behavior while hiding implementation details.

Example


PaymentService

↓

Pay()

Users know payment happens. They do not need to know internal implementation.

Q110. Explain Inheritance

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Inheritance allows a class to acquire properties and behavior from another class.

Example


Animal

↓

Dog

Benefit

Code reuse.

Q111. Explain Polymorphism

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Polymorphism allows the same interface to behave differently depending on the object implementation.

Example


PaymentService

↓

CardPayment

UPIPayment

NetBankingPayment
Q112. Compile-Time vs Runtime Polymorphism

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Compile Time Runtime
Method Overloading Method Overriding
Static Binding Dynamic Binding

SECTION 20 - SOLID PRINCIPLES

This is one of the highest ROI architecture topics. Almost every senior backend interview eventually reaches SOLID.

Q113. What Is SOLID?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Answer


S - Single Responsibility

O - Open Closed

L - Liskov Substitution

I - Interface Segregation

D - Dependency Inversion
Q114. Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

A class should have only one reason to change.

Bad Example


UserService

Creates User

Sends Email

Generates PDF

Good Example


UserService

EmailService

PdfService
Q115. Open Closed Principle (OCP)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification.

Example


Payment Interface

↓

UPI

Card

Wallet

Add new payment types without modifying existing code.

Q116. Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Derived classes should be replaceable for their base classes without changing correctness.

Classic Example

Square extending Rectangle often violates LSP.

Q117. Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use.

Bad


Huge Interface

20 Methods

Good


Small Focused Interfaces
Q118. Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations.

Example


PaymentService

↓

PaymentGateway

Interface

Not directly tied to Stripe or Razorpay implementation.

Spring Connection

Dependency Injection is heavily based on DIP.

SECTION 21 - DESIGN PATTERNS

Q119. What Is A Design Pattern?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

A design pattern is a proven, reusable solution to a commonly recurring problem in software design. It is not code you copy directly but a template that describes how to structure classes and objects to solve a particular kind of problem.

Patterns give teams a shared vocabulary (Singleton, Factory, Strategy, Observer) so that intent can be communicated quickly, and they encode design experience that improves maintainability and flexibility.

Q120. Singleton Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Ensures only one instance exists.

Examples

  • Spring Beans
  • Logger
  • Configuration Objects
Q121. Singleton Thread Safety Problem

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

Multiple threads can create multiple instances if implementation is incorrect.

Solutions

  • Enum Singleton
  • Static Initialization
  • Double Checked Locking
Q122. Factory Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Factory Pattern creates objects without exposing creation logic.

Example


PaymentFactory

↓

CardPayment

UPIPayment
Q123. Strategy Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Defines multiple interchangeable algorithms.

Example


Payment Strategy

↓

Card

UPI

Wallet

Spring Boot Usage

Very common in service selection logic.

Q124. Observer Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

One object notifies multiple dependent objects about state changes.

Examples

  • Event Listeners
  • Kafka Consumers
  • Notification Systems
Q125. Builder Pattern

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Constructs complex objects step-by-step.

Example


User.builder()
    .name(...)
    .email(...)
    .build()

Benefits

  • Readable
  • Immutable Objects
  • Avoid Constructor Explosion

SECTION 22 - IMMUTABILITY

Q126. What Is An Immutable Object?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

An immutable object cannot change after creation.

Examples


String

LocalDate

BigInteger
Q127. Benefits Of Immutability

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

  • Thread Safety
  • Predictability
  • Easier Debugging
  • Safe Sharing
Q128. How To Create Immutable Class?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

  1. Make Class Final
  2. Make Fields Final
  3. No Setters
  4. Defensive Copying
  5. Initialize Through Constructor
Q129. Why Is String Immutable?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Strong Answer

  • Security
  • String Pooling
  • Thread Safety
  • HashMap Optimization

Example

Database URLs, file paths and security credentials depend on String immutability.

Q130. Real Production Benefit Of Immutability

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

Immutable objects can be safely shared across threads without synchronization. This reduces bugs and improves reliability in concurrent systems.

PART 6 COMPLETE

Next Part: * Serialization * Reflection * Annotations * Class Loaders * JVM Internals * JIT Compiler * Metaspace * Escape Analysis * Advanced Java Questions Asked By Senior Interviewers This is where many architect-level Java discussions begin.

SECTION 23 - SERIALIZATION

Serialization questions are common because they connect Java fundamentals with distributed systems, caching, messaging and microservices.

Q131. What Is Serialization?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Serialization is the process of converting a Java object into a byte stream so that it can be:

  • Stored in Files
  • Transferred Across Network
  • Sent Through Kafka
  • Stored In Redis

Flow


Object

↓

Byte Stream

↓

Storage / Network
Q132. What Is Deserialization?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Deserialization is the reverse process. 


Byte Stream

↓

Object
Q133. Why Does Serializable Interface Have No Methods?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

Serializable is a Marker Interface. It contains no methods. Its purpose is to indicate to the JVM that the class supports serialization.

Other Marker Interfaces


Serializable

Cloneable

Remote
Q134. What Is serialVersionUID?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

serialVersionUID is a version identifier used during serialization and deserialization.

Purpose

Ensures compatibility between serialized objects and class definitions.

Problem Without It

Changing class structure may cause: 


InvalidClassException
Q135. transient Keyword

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Fields marked as transient are excluded from serialization.

Example


password

otp

token

Production Use

Sensitive information should often be transient.

Q136. Java Serialization Problems

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

  • Slow Performance
  • Versioning Issues
  • Security Risks
  • Large Payload Size

Modern Alternative

  • JSON
  • Protocol Buffers
  • Avro

SECTION 24 - REFLECTION

Q137. What Is Reflection?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Reflection allows inspection and manipulation of classes, methods, fields and constructors at runtime.

Example


Get Class Name

Invoke Method

Read Fields

Create Object
Q138. Why Reflection Is Important In Spring?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Spring heavily uses reflection for:

  • Dependency Injection
  • Bean Creation
  • Annotation Processing
  • AOP
  • ORM Mapping
Q139. Drawbacks Of Reflection

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

  • Slower Than Direct Calls
  • Breaks Encapsulation
  • Harder To Debug
  • Security Concerns
Q140. Can Reflection Access Private Methods?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Answer

Yes. 


setAccessible(true)

can bypass access checks.

SECTION 25 - ANNOTATIONS

Q141. What Are Annotations?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Interview Ready Answer

Annotations provide metadata about code.

They do not directly change program logic but can influence framework behavior.

Q142. Common Java Annotations

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High 


@Override

@Deprecated

@SuppressWarnings
Q143. Common Spring Annotations

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High 


@Component

@Service

@Repository

@RestController

@Autowired
Q144. Retention Policies

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Policy Availability
SOURCE Compile Only
CLASS Bytecode
RUNTIME Runtime Reflection
Q145. Why Runtime Retention For Spring?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Answer

Spring discovers annotations using reflection at runtime. Without RUNTIME retention, annotations would not be available.

SECTION 26 - CLASS LOADING

Q146. What Is Class Loader?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Interview Ready Answer

Class Loader loads classes into JVM memory dynamically.

Q147. Types Of Class Loaders

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High 


Bootstrap

↓

Platform

↓

Application
Q148. Bootstrap ClassLoader

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

Answer

Loads core Java classes.

Examples


String

Object

ArrayList
Q149. Parent Delegation Model

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Interview Ready Answer

Class loading requests are delegated upward first. 


Application

↓

Platform

↓

Bootstrap

This prevents duplicate class loading and enhances security.

SECTION 27 - JVM INTERNALS

Q150. What Happens When A Java Program Starts?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

  1. JVM Starts
  2. Main Class Loaded
  3. Bytecode Verified
  4. Class Initialized
  5. Main Method Invoked
Q151. What Is JIT Compiler?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

Full Form

Just-In-Time Compiler

Interview Ready Answer

JIT converts frequently executed bytecode into native machine code at runtime.

Benefit

Improves performance significantly.

Q152. JVM vs JRE vs JDK

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Guaranteed

JVM JRE JDK
Executes Java Runtime Development Kit
Engine JVM + Libraries JRE + Tools
Q153. What Is Metaspace?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Extremely High

Interview Ready Answer

Metaspace stores class metadata.

Java 7


PermGen

Java 8+


Metaspace

Moved to native memory.

Q154. PermGen vs Metaspace

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: High

PermGen Metaspace
Fixed Size Native Memory
Java 7 Java 8+
Q155. What Is Escape Analysis?

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Architect Level

Interview Ready Answer

JVM optimization technique that determines whether objects escape a method or thread.

Benefits

  • Stack Allocation
  • Lock Elimination
  • Reduced Garbage Collection
Q156. JVM Performance Investigation Approach

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

Strong Answer

  1. CPU Metrics
  2. Memory Metrics
  3. GC Analysis
  4. Thread Dumps
  5. Heap Dumps
  6. Database Performance
  7. External Dependencies

Production Insight

Never assume the JVM is the problem before collecting evidence.

Q157. Most Common JVM Issues In Production

ROI: ★★★★★ Frequency: Senior Favorite

  • Memory Leaks
  • Excessive GC
  • Thread Pool Exhaustion
  • Deadlocks
  • CPU Spikes
  • Improper Heap Sizing
Q158. Senior Interview Question: Application Suddenly Becomes Slow. Where Do You Start?

ROI: Maximum ★★★★★

Expected Senior Answer

  1. Check Monitoring Dashboards
  2. CPU Usage
  3. Memory Usage
  4. GC Activity
  5. Thread Pool Metrics
  6. Database Queries
  7. Recent Deployments
  8. External Service Latency
  9. Logs
  10. Distributed Traces

The interviewer is evaluating troubleshooting methodology, not just technical knowledge.
